title: On Tackling Complex Tasks with Reward Machines and Signal Temporal Logics
summary: We propose a Reinforcement Learning (RL) based control design framework for handling complex tasks. The approach extends the concept of Reward Machines (RM) with Signal Temporal Logic (STL) formulas that can be used for event generation. The use of STL allows not only a more efficient representation of rewards for complex tasks but also guiding the training process to converge towards behaviors satisfying specified requirements. We also propose an implementation of the framework that leverages the STL online monitoring algorithms. We illustrate the framework with three case studies (minigrid, cart-pole and high-way environments) with non-trivial tasks.

AI.tldr: RL-based control framework combining Reward Machines with Signal Temporal Logic (STL) for complex tasks.
AI.motivation: Designing RL controllers for complex, temporally-extended tasks is difficult because reward functions become complicated and training may not converge to behaviors that satisfy formal requirements. The authors aim to create a structured, logic-based way to specify and monitor complex tasks and rewards.
AI.method: Extend Reward Machines by integrating STL formulas for event generation and reward structuring; use STL online monitoring algorithms within the RL loop to guide learning. Implement the framework and test it on multiple environments with non-trivial tasks.
AI.result: The proposed RL+STL+RM framework is implemented and validated on three case studies—minigrid, cart-pole, and highway driving—showing it can handle non-trivial, temporally-extended tasks more effectively than naïve reward shaping (implied by the abstract).
AI.conclusion: Combining Reward Machines with STL provides a compact, expressive way to encode complex task rewards and guide RL training toward behaviors that satisfy formal temporal logic requirements, demonstrated across several benchmark environments.

title: AIBuildAI: An AI Agent for Automatically Building AI Models
summary: AI models underpin modern intelligent systems, driving advances across science, medicine, finance, and technology. Yet developing high-performing AI models remains a labor-intensive process that requires expert practitioners to iteratively design architectures, engineer representations, implement training pipelines and refine approaches through empirical evaluation. Existing AutoML methods partially alleviate this burden but remain limited to narrow aspects such as hyperparameter optimization and model selection within predefined search spaces, leaving the full development lifecycle largely dependent on human expertise. To address this gap, we introduce AIBuildAI, an AI agent that automatically builds AI models from a task description and training data. AIBuildAI adopts a hierarchical agent architecture in which a manager agent coordinates three specialized sub-agents: a designer for modeling strategy, a coder for implementation and debugging, and a tuner for training and performance optimization. Each sub-agent is itself a large language model (LLM) based agent capable of multi-step reasoning and tool use, enabling end-to-end automation of the AI model development process that goes beyond the scope of existing AutoML approaches. We evaluate AIBuildAI on MLE-Bench, a benchmark of realistic Kaggle-style AI development tasks spanning visual, textual, time-series and tabular modalities. AIBuildAI ranks first on MLE-Bench with a medal rate of 63.1%, outperforming all existing baseline methods and matching the capability of highly experienced AI engineers. These results demonstrate that hierarchical agent systems can automate the full AI model development process from task specification to deployable model, suggesting a pathway toward broadly accessible AI development with minimal human intervention.

AI.tldr: AIBuildAI is a hierarchical LLM-agent system that automatically designs, codes, and tunes AI models from task descriptions and data, outperforming existing AutoML methods on realistic benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Building high-performing AI models typically requires intensive expert effort across multiple stages—architecture design, feature engineering, pipeline implementation, and iterative tuning. Existing AutoML tools only cover narrow components (e.g., hyperparameters, search over fixed model families) and still rely heavily on human practitioners for most of the development lifecycle. The authors aim to remove this bottleneck by creating an agentic AI system that can take over the complete AI development workflow, making advanced model building more accessible and less labor intensive.
AI.method: The authors propose AIBuildAI, a hierarchical multi-agent system built from large language model (LLM) agents. A top-level manager agent orchestrates three specialized sub-agents: (1) a designer that decides on modeling strategy and high-level approach, (2) a coder that implements the model, data pipeline, and debugging steps, and (3) a tuner that manages training, hyperparameter optimization, and performance refinement. Each sub-agent performs multi-step reasoning and uses external tools as needed, enabling end-to-end automation from task specification and dataset to a working, optimized model. The system is evaluated on the MLE-Bench benchmark, which consists of realistic Kaggle-style tasks over different data modalities (vision, text, time series, tabular).
AI.result: On the MLE-Bench benchmark, AIBuildAI achieves a medal rate of 63.1%, ranking first among all evaluated methods. It outperforms existing baselines, including traditional AutoML approaches, and its performance is comparable to that of highly experienced AI engineers working on the same tasks.
AI.conclusion: AIBuildAI shows that a hierarchical LLM-based agent architecture can effectively automate the entire AI model development workflow—from understanding a task description and dataset to producing a deployable, well-tuned model. This extends beyond the limited scope of conventional AutoML and suggests that multi-agent LLM systems can make sophisticated AI development broadly accessible with minimal human oversight or manual engineering.

title: SAGE Celer 2.6 Technical Card
summary: We introduce SAGE Celer 2.6, the latest in our line of general-purpose Celer models from SAGEA. Celer 2.6 is available in 5B, 10B, and 27B parameter sizes and benefits from extensive architectural modifications and further pre-training on an undisclosed model. Using our Inverse Reasoning (IR) pipeline, SAGEA natively trains Celer 2.6 to validate its own logic paths, minimizing cascading error and hallucination in complex reasoning tasks. Celer 2.6 also boasts natively integrated multimodal functionality with an end-to-end vision encoder to avoid common pitfalls in adapter-based approaches. Celer 2.6 provides highly competitive results on mathematics, coding, and general intelligence benchmarks (ACUMEN), along with low latency. Most importantly, Celer 2.6 is specifically optimized for South Asian language support, with a custom tokenizer for the Devanagari script and strong performance in both Nepali and Hindi without sacrificing English reasoning ability.

AI.tldr: SAGE Celer 2.6 is a new family of general-purpose, multimodal language models with improved reasoning reliability and strong South Asian language support.
AI.motivation: Existing general-purpose language models struggle with cascading reasoning errors, hallucinations in complex tasks, suboptimal multimodal integration via adapters, and limited support for South Asian languages like Hindi and Nepali, especially with Devanagari script. The paper aims to build a competitive, low-latency model that is both better at validating its own reasoning and more inclusive linguistically, without degrading English performance.
AI.method: The authors develop SAGE Celer 2.6 models in 5B, 10B, and 27B sizes using (1) architectural modifications to a prior undisclosed base model, (2) additional pre-training, (3) an Inverse Reasoning (IR) training pipeline where the model is natively trained to check and validate its own reasoning chains to reduce hallucinations and error propagation, and (4) fully integrated multimodal capability via an end-to-end vision encoder rather than using separate adapters. They also design a custom tokenizer tailored to Devanagari script and optimize training data and objectives for South Asian languages while preserving English reasoning skills.
AI.result: Celer 2.6 achieves highly competitive performance on mathematics, coding, and general intelligence benchmarks (ACUMEN) while maintaining low latency. It demonstrates strong capabilities in South Asian languages, particularly Hindi and Nepali, supported by the Devanagari-focused tokenizer, and retains robust English reasoning performance. Its multimodal design avoids common issues seen in adapter-based vision-language setups.
AI.conclusion: SAGE Celer 2.6 represents an improved general-purpose, multimodal LLM family that combines better reasoning reliability through Inverse Reasoning, efficient end-to-end vision integration, and strong, optimized support for South Asian languages, especially Devanagari-based ones, all without sacrificing English performance or inference efficiency.

title: Improving Human Performance with Value-Aware Interventions: A Case Study in Chess
summary: AI systems are increasingly used to assist humans in sequential decision-making tasks, yet determining when and how an AI assistant should intervene remains a fundamental challenge. A potential baseline is to recommend the optimal action according to a strong model. However, such actions assume optimal follow-up actions, which human decision makers may fail to execute, potentially reducing overall performance. In this work, we propose and study value-aware interventions, motivated by a basic principle in reinforcement learning: under the Bellman equation, the optimal policy selects actions that maximize the immediate reward plus the value function. When a decision maker follows a suboptimal policy, this policy-value consistency no longer holds, creating discrepancies between the actions taken by the policy and those that maximize the immediate reward plus the value of the next state. We show that these policy-value inconsistencies naturally identify opportunities for intervention. We formalize this problem in a Markov decision process where an AI assistant may override human actions under an intervention budget. In the single-intervention regime, we show that the optimal strategy is to recommend the action that maximizes the human value function. For settings with multiple interventions, we propose a tractable approximation that prioritizes interventions based on the magnitude of the policy-value discrepancy. We evaluate these ideas in the domain of chess by learning models of humans from large-scale gameplay data. In simulation, our approach consistently outperforms interventions based on the strongest chess engine (Stockfish) in a wide range of settings. A within-subject human study with 20 players and 600 games further shows that our interventions significantly improve performance for low- and mid-skill players while matching expert-engine interventions for high-skill players.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes value-aware AI interventions that selectively override human actions in sequential decision-making (e.g., chess), using discrepancies between human policy and value estimates to decide when and how to intervene, achieving better performance than always recommending the engine-optimal move.
AI.motivation: As AI systems increasingly assist humans in sequential decision-making, naively recommending the action optimal for a superhuman policy (e.g., a chess engine move) may not yield the best human-AI team performance, because humans cannot reliably execute optimal follow-up actions. The authors seek a principled way to decide when an AI should intervene and what it should recommend, taking into account the human's actual (suboptimal) policy and value estimates, and respecting a limited intervention budget.
AI.method: The authors model the interaction between a human decision maker and an AI assistant as a Markov decision process with an intervention budget. They leverage the Bellman equation to define policy-value consistency: under an optimal policy, chosen actions maximize immediate reward plus next-state value. For humans following a suboptimal policy, this consistency is violated. The authors define policy-value discrepancies as a signal for intervention opportunities. In the single-intervention setting, they prove that the optimal intervention is to recommend the action maximizing the human value function. For multiple interventions, they propose a tractable heuristic that ranks states by the magnitude of the policy-value discrepancy and intervenes accordingly. They instantiate this in chess by learning human policies and value functions from large-scale gameplay data and simulate different intervention policies. They also run a within-subject user study with 20 human players over 600 games.
AI.result: In simulations using learned human models, value-aware intervention policies that exploit policy-value discrepancies consistently outperform baseline interventions that always follow the strongest chess engine (Stockfish), across different budget and skill settings. In a human study with 20 players and 600 games, the proposed interventions significantly improve the performance of low- and mid-skill players relative to their baselines, and achieve performance on par with expert-engine-based interventions for high-skill players.
AI.conclusion: Value-aware interventions based on policy-value inconsistencies provide a principled and effective way to decide when and how an AI assistant should override human decisions in sequential tasks. Rather than always suggesting the globally optimal action under an ideal policy, tailoring interventions to the human's own value function and selectively deploying them where policy-value discrepancies are large leads to better human-AI team performance, particularly under limited intervention budgets and for non-expert users.

title: Chronological Knowledge Retrieval: A Retrieval-Augmented Generation Approach to Construction Project Documentation
summary: In large-scale construction projects, the continuous evolution of decisions generates extensive records, most often captured in meeting minutes. Since decisions may override previous ones, professionals often need to reconstruct the history of specific choices. Retrieving such information manually from raw archives is both labor-intensive and error-prone. From a user perspective, we address this challenge by enabling conversational access to the whole set of project meeting minutes. Professionals can pose natural-language questions and receive answers that are both semantically relevant and explicitly time-annotated, allowing them to follow the chronology of decisions. From a technical perspective, our solution employs a Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) framework that integrates semantic search with large language models to ensure accurate and context-aware responses. We demonstrate the approach using an anonymized, industry-sourced dataset of meeting minutes from a completed construction project by a large company in Belgium. The dataset is annotated and enriched with expert-defined queries to support systematic evaluation. Both the dataset and the open-source implementation are made available to the community to foster further research on conversational access to time-annotated project documentation.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a conversational RAG system that lets construction professionals query time-annotated meeting minutes to reconstruct the chronology of project decisions.
AI.motivation: In large construction projects, decisions evolve over time and are mostly recorded in meeting minutes. Later decisions may override earlier ones, and professionals often need to reconstruct the history of a specific choice (e.g., why a design changed, when a requirement was introduced or dropped). Manually searching long, unstructured archives of minutes is tedious and error-prone. There is a need for an efficient, accurate way to navigate these documents and track the temporal evolution of decisions using natural language.
AI.method: The authors design a Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) architecture that combines semantic search over meeting minutes with large language models. Meeting minutes from a real, completed project are preprocessed, annotated, and indexed. When a user asks a natural-language question, the system retrieves semantically relevant excerpts and feeds them to an LLM that generates an answer. Crucially, the answer is explicitly time-annotated, i.e., it ties statements to specific dates or times to reflect the chronology of decisions. The system is implemented as a conversational interface. For evaluation, the dataset is enriched with expert-defined queries to systematically assess answer quality.
AI.result: The paper demonstrates the feasibility of conversational, time-aware querying of project meeting minutes using RAG. Using an anonymized dataset from a large Belgian construction project, the system can return semantically relevant answers that are temporally grounded. The authors provide both the curated dataset (with time annotations and expert queries) and the open-source implementation, enabling reproducibility and comparison. Quantitative metrics are not detailed in the abstract, but the system is shown to work effectively as a proof of concept on real-world data.
AI.conclusion: Conversational, time-annotated access to project meeting minutes is achievable and useful for reconstructing the decision history in large construction projects. By combining semantic retrieval with LLM-based generation in a RAG framework, professionals can ask natural-language questions and obtain chronologically structured answers. The release of an anonymized, industry-sourced dataset and open-source code creates a foundation for further research on conversational interfaces to time-stamped project documentation in construction and potentially other domains.

title: Response-Aware User Memory Selection for LLM Personalization
summary: A common approach to personalization in large language models (LLMs) is to incorporate a subset of the user memory into the prompt at inference time to guide the model's generation. Existing methods select these subsets primarily using similarity between user memory items and input queries, ignoring how features actually affect the model's response distribution. We propose Response-Utility optimization for Memory Selection (RUMS), a novel method that selects user memory items by measuring the mutual information between a subset of memory and the model's outputs, identifying items that reduce response uncertainty and sharpen predictions beyond semantic similarity. We demonstrate that this information-theoretic foundation enables more principled user memory selection that aligns more closely with human selection compared to state-of-the-art methods, and models $400\times$ larger. Additionally, we show that memory items selected using RUMS result in better response quality compared to existing approaches, while having up to $95\%$ reduction in computational cost.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces RUMS, an information-theoretic method for selecting user memory for LLM personalization that outperforms similarity-based selection in quality, human alignment, and efficiency.
AI.motivation: Current LLM personalization typically selects user memories based on semantic similarity to the query, which ignores how those memories actually change the model’s output distribution. This can lead to suboptimal or inefficient personalization because the chosen memories may not meaningfully affect or improve the response, and memory retrieval is computationally expensive. The authors aim to design a more principled, model-aware way to choose which user memories to include.
AI.method: They formulate memory selection as an information-theoretic optimization problem and propose RUMS (Response-Utility optimization for Memory Selection). RUMS measures the mutual information between subsets of user memory and the LLM’s outputs, then selects memory items that maximally reduce response uncertainty and sharpen predictions, instead of relying on semantic similarity alone. The approach is evaluated against state-of-the-art similarity-based methods and on models up to 400× larger than in prior studies.
AI.result: RUMS selects memory items that align more closely with human judgments of which memories are relevant, when compared with existing similarity-based and heuristic methods. The selected memories lead to improved response quality from the LLM and do so while reducing computational cost by up to 95% relative to competing approaches, even when scaling to much larger models.
AI.conclusion: Information-theoretic, response-aware memory selection is more effective than simple similarity-based methods for LLM personalization. By explicitly optimizing for mutual information between memory and model outputs, RUMS yields more human-aligned memory choices, higher response quality, and substantial computational savings, suggesting a better foundation for personalized LLM systems.

title: Stateful Evidence-Driven Retrieval-Augmented Generation with Iterative Reasoning
summary: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) grounds Large Language Models (LLMs) in external knowledge but often suffers from flat context representations and stateless retrieval, leading to unstable performance. We propose Stateful Evidence-Driven RAG with Iterative Reasoning, a framework that models question answering as a progressive evidence accumulation process. Retrieved documents are converted into structured reasoning units with explicit relevance and confidence signals and maintained in a persistent evidence pool capturing both supportive and non-supportive information. The framework performs evidence-driven deficiency analysis to identify gaps and conflicts and iteratively refines queries to guide subsequent retrieval. This iterative reasoning process enables stable evidence aggregation and improves robustness to noisy retrieval. Experiments on multiple question answering benchmarks demonstrate consistent improvements over standard RAG and multi-step baselines, while effectively accumulating high-quality evidence and maintaining stable performance under substantial retrieval noise.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a RAG framework that maintains a persistent, structured evidence pool and iteratively refines retrieval based on evidence gaps and conflicts, yielding more stable, robust QA performance under noisy retrieval.
AI.motivation: Standard RAG systems treat retrieved context as a flat, one-shot input and ignore state across retrieval steps, which leads to unstable answers and vulnerability to noisy or partially relevant documents. The authors aim to make RAG more robust and reliable by modeling QA as an explicit, progressive accumulation of evidence, rather than a single retrieval step with unstructured context.
AI.method: They design a Stateful Evidence-Driven RAG framework with Iterative Reasoning. Retrieved documents are decomposed into structured reasoning units that carry explicit relevance and confidence annotations. These units are stored in a persistent evidence pool that tracks both supportive and non-supportive evidence. The system periodically performs evidence-driven deficiency analysis to detect information gaps and conflicts in the pool, then refines the retrieval queries accordingly. This loop of retrieval → structuring → deficiency analysis → query refinement continues, guiding future retrieval towards missing or clarifying evidence.
AI.result: On multiple QA benchmarks, their method outperforms standard RAG and existing multi-step baselines. It accumulates higher-quality evidence, shows more stable performance, and remains robust even when a large fraction of retrieved documents is noisy or irrelevant.
AI.conclusion: Modeling QA as stateful, iterative evidence accumulation with structured evidence units and deficiency-aware retrieval improves the robustness and stability of RAG systems. Maintaining a persistent, explicitly reasoned evidence pool helps mitigate noisy retrieval and yields more reliable answers across diverse QA tasks.

title: Evo-MedAgent: Beyond One-Shot Diagnosis with Agents That Remember, Reflect, and Improve
summary: Tool-augmented large language model (LLM) agents can orchestrate specialist classifiers, segmentation models, and visual question-answering modules to interpret chest X-rays. However, these agents still solve each case in isolation: they fail to accumulate experience across cases, correct recurrent reasoning mistakes, or adapt their tool-use behavior without expensive reinforcement learning. While a radiologist naturally improves with every case, current agents remain static. In this work, we propose Evo-MedAgent, a self-evolving memory module that equips a medical agent with the capacity for inter-case learning at test time. Our memory comprises three complementary stores: (1)~\emph{Retrospective Clinical Episodes} that retrieve problem-solving experiences from similar past cases, (2)~an \emph{Adaptive Procedural Heuristics} bank curating priority-tagged diagnostic rules that evolves via reflection, much like a physician refining their internal criteria, and (3)~a \emph{Tool Reliability Controller} that tracks per-tool trustworthiness. On ChestAgentBench, Evo-MedAgent raises multiple-choice question (MCQ) accuracy from 0.68 to 0.79 on GPT-5-mini, and from 0.76 to 0.87 on Gemini-3 Flash. With a strong base model, evolving memory improves performance more effectively than orchestrating external tools on qualitative diagnostic tasks. Because Evo-MedAgent requires no training, its per-case overhead is bounded by one additional retrieval pass and a single reflection call, making it deployable on top of any frozen model.

AI.tldr: Evo-MedAgent is a training-free, self-evolving memory module that enables LLM-based medical imaging agents to learn across chest X-ray cases at test time, improving diagnostic accuracy and tool use efficiency.
AI.motivation: Existing tool-augmented LLM radiology agents treat each chest X-ray case independently, lacking the ability to accumulate experience, correct recurring reasoning errors, or adapt their tool-usage strategies without costly reinforcement learning. In contrast, human radiologists naturally improve over time by learning from previous cases. The paper aims to close this gap by enabling inter-case learning in LLM agents without retraining or RL, particularly in high-stakes medical imaging settings where continuous adaptation is critical but data labeling and model updates are expensive.
AI.method: The authors introduce Evo-MedAgent, a self-evolving memory module that can be attached to a frozen medical LLM agent. The memory has three components: (1) Retrospective Clinical Episodes, which retrieve and reuse experiences from similar past cases to guide current reasoning; (2) an Adaptive Procedural Heuristics bank, a dynamic repository of prioritized diagnostic rules that are updated via reflection based on previous successes and failures, analogous to a clinician refining internal criteria; and (3) a Tool Reliability Controller, which tracks the historical trustworthiness of each external tool and adjusts tool selection and weighting accordingly. The system operates entirely at test time, adding only one extra retrieval step and one reflection step per case, without any gradient-based training.
AI.result: On the ChestAgentBench benchmark for chest X-ray interpretation, Evo-MedAgent substantially boosts multiple-choice diagnostic accuracy: from 0.68 to 0.79 when the base agent uses GPT-5-mini and from 0.76 to 0.87 with Gemini-3 Flash. Qualitative evaluations on more open-ended diagnostic tasks show that, given a strong base model, the evolving memory module yields greater performance gains than adding more external tools, indicating that better experience reuse and tool calibration can be more beneficial than expanding the toolset alone.
AI.conclusion: Evo-MedAgent demonstrates that a lightweight, training-free, evolving memory can significantly enhance tool-augmented LLM medical agents by enabling inter-case learning at inference time. The three-part memory—episodic recall, adaptive heuristics, and tool reliability tracking—improves diagnostic accuracy and tool orchestration with minimal computational overhead. This suggests a practical path to continually improving clinical AI assistants built on frozen foundation models, without the need for frequent retraining or reinforcement learning, and highlights the importance of structured, reflective memory in medical reasoning agents.

title: Benchmarking Linguistic Adaptation in Comparable-Sized LLMs: A Study of Llama-3.1-8B, Mistral-7B-v0.1, and Qwen3-8B on Romanized Nepali
summary: Romanized Nepali, the Nepali language written in the Latin alphabet, is the dominant medium for informal digital communication in Nepal, yet it remains critically underresourced in the landscape of Large Language Models (LLMs). This study presents a systematic benchmarking of linguistic adaptation across three comparable-sized open-weight models: Llama-3.1-8B, Mistral-7B-v0.1, and Qwen3-8B. We evaluate these architectures under zero-shot and fine-tuned settings using a curated bilingual dataset of 10,000 transliterated instruction-following samples. Performance is quantified across five metrics spanning seven measurement dimensions: Perplexity (PPL), BERTScore, chrF++, ROUGE-1, ROUGE-2, ROUGE-L, and BLEU, capturing fluency, phonetic consistency, and semantic integrity. Models were fine-tuned using Quantized Low-Rank Adaptation (QLoRA) with Rank-Stabilized LoRA (rsLoRA) at rank r=32 on dual NVIDIA Tesla T4 GPUs, training only approximately 1% of each model's parameters in under 27 total GPU-hours. At zero-shot, all three models fail to generate Romanized Nepali, each exhibiting a distinct architecture-specific failure mode. Following fine-tuning, all three resolve these failures and converge to BERTScore approximately 0.75 and chrF++ greater than 23. Overall dimension-wise assessment across ten criteria identifies Qwen3-8B as the overall recommended architecture, being the only model to produce semantically relevant zero-shot output and leading all structural alignment metrics post-SFT. The adaptation headroom hypothesis is confirmed: Llama-3.1-8B, despite its weakest zero-shot baseline, achieves the largest absolute fine-tuning gains in PPL (Delta = -49.77) and BERTScore (Delta = +0.3287), making it the preferred choice for iterative low-resource development pipelines. This work establishes the first rigorous baseline for Romanized Nepali adaptation in comparable-sized open-weight LLMs.

AI.tldr: The paper benchmarks how well three open-weight LLMs can be adapted to Romanized Nepali using efficient fine-tuning, finding that all can be successfully adapted and establishing the first systematic baseline for this underresourced setting.
AI.motivation: Romanized Nepali is widely used online but is not directly supported by current LLMs, which typically handle either English or native-script Nepali. This lack of support limits NLP applications for a very common real-world writing style. The authors aim to understand how different open-weight LLM architectures can be efficiently adapted to this low-resource, transliterated language and to provide a rigorous baseline for future work.
AI.method: The authors curate a 10,000-sample bilingual, transliterated instruction-following dataset and benchmark three similar-sized open-weight models (Llama-3.1-8B, Mistral-7B-v0.1, Qwen3-8B). They evaluate models in zero-shot and after supervised fine-tuning using QLoRA with rsLoRA (rank 32), training about 1% of parameters on dual Tesla T4 GPUs (<27 GPU-hours). Performance is measured with PPL, BERTScore, chrF++, ROUGE-1/2/L, and BLEU to capture fluency, phonetic consistency, and semantic faithfulness. They also perform a dimension-wise assessment across ten criteria to compare architectures and test the adaptation headroom hypothesis.
AI.result: Zero-shot, none of the models natively generate Romanized Nepali and each fails in different, architecture-specific ways; only Qwen3-8B produces semantically relevant but structurally misaligned output. After fine-tuning, all three models learn to generate Romanized Nepali, achieving around 0.75 BERTScore and chrF++ above 23. Qwen3-8B leads on structural alignment metrics and is the recommended model overall. Llama-3.1-8B, though worst at zero-shot, shows the largest absolute improvements in perplexity (−49.77) and BERTScore (+0.3287), supporting the adaptation headroom hypothesis.
AI.conclusion: Efficient low-rank fine-tuning is sufficient to adapt open-weight LLMs to Romanized Nepali, overcoming severe zero-shot limitations and yielding strong performance across multiple metrics. Qwen3-8B is the best all-round choice for immediate deployment, while Llama-3.1-8B offers the greatest gains under continued fine-tuning, making it ideal for iterative low-resource development. The work provides the first systematic baseline and evaluation framework for Romanized Nepali LLM adaptation, enabling future research on transliterated, low-resource languages.

title: Seeing Through Circuits: Faithful Mechanistic Interpretability for Vision Transformers
summary: Transparency of neural networks' internal reasoning is at the heart of interpretability research, adding to trust, safety, and understanding of these models. The field of mechanistic interpretability has recently focused on studying task-specific computational graphs, defined by connections (edges) between model components. Such edge-based circuits have been defined in the context of large language models, yet vision-based approaches so far only consider neuron-based circuits. These tell which information is encoded, but not how it is routed through the complex wiring of a neural network. In this work, we investigate whether useful mechanistic circuits can be identified through computational graphs in vision transformers. We propose an effective method for Automatic Visual Circuit Discovery (Vi-CD) that recovers class-specific circuits for classification, identifies circuits underlying typographic attacks in CLIP, and discovers circuits that lend themselves for steering to correct harmful model behavior. Overall, we find that insightful and actionable edge-based circuits can be recovered from vision transformers, adding transparency to the internal computations of these models.

AI.tldr: The paper presents Automatic Visual Circuit Discovery (Vi-CD) to uncover edge-based mechanistic circuits in vision transformers, improving interpretability and enabling behavior steering.
AI.motivation: Mechanistic interpretability has progressed in language models with edge-based circuits but vision research largely relies on neuron-based circuits, which reveal what is encoded but not how information flows through the network. There is a gap in understanding and tracing the routing of information in vision transformers, limiting transparency, trust, safety, and actionable control over model behavior.
AI.method: The authors introduce Automatic Visual Circuit Discovery (Vi-CD), a method that recovers task- and class-specific computational graphs (edge-based circuits) in vision transformers. The technique analyzes connections between model components to extract circuits for standard classification tasks, for circuits involved in typographic attacks on CLIP, and for circuits that can be manipulated to steer model behavior away from harmful outputs.
AI.result: Using Vi-CD, the authors successfully recover class-specific edge-based circuits in vision transformers for classification; they identify the circuits responsible for typographic attack behavior in CLIP; and they find circuits that, when intervened on, can steer the model to correct or mitigate harmful behavior. These results demonstrate that meaningful and actionable edge-based mechanistic circuits exist in vision transformers and can be extracted automatically.
AI.conclusion: The study shows that vision transformers possess discoverable edge-based circuits that meaningfully explain internal computations. Vi-CD provides a practical way to expose these circuits, thereby improving transparency and offering levers for analyzing and steering model behavior, including mitigating undesirable or harmful outputs.

title: Tug-of-War within A Decade: Conflict Resolution in Vulnerability Analysis via Teacher-Guided Retrieval-Augmented Generations
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) are essential for analyzing and addressing vulnerabilities in cybersecurity. However, among over 200,000 vulnerabilities were discovered in the past decade, more than 30,000 have been changed or updated. This necessitates frequent updates to the training datasets and internal knowledge bases of LLMs to maintain knowledge consistency. In this paper, we focus on the problem of knowledge discrepancy and conflict within CVE (Common Vulnerabilities and Exposures) detection and analysis. This problem hinders LLMs' ability to retrieve the latest knowledge from original training datasets, leading to knowledge conflicts, fabrications of factually incorrect results, and generation hallucinations. To address this problem, we propose an innovative two-stage framework called CRVA-TGRAG (Conflict Resolution in Vulnerability Analysis via Teacher-Guided Retrieval-Augmented Generation). First, to improve document retrieval accuracy during the retrieval stage, we utilize Parent Document Segmentation and an ensemble retrieval scheme based on semantic similarity and inverted indexing. Second, to enhance LLMs' capabilities based on the retrieval of CVE dataset in generation stage, we employ a teacher-guided preference optimization technique to fine-tune LLMs. Our framework not only enhances the quality of content retrieval through RAG but also leverages the advantages of preference fine-tuning in LLMs to answer questions more effectively and precisely. Experiments demonstrate our method achieves higher accuracy in retrieving the latest CVEs compared to external knowledge bases. In conclusion, our framework significantly mitigates potential knowledge conflicts and inconsistencies that may arise from relying solely on LLMs for knowledge retrieval.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces CRVA-TGRAG, a two-stage framework that reduces knowledge conflicts and hallucinations in CVE-related cybersecurity analysis by combining improved retrieval with teacher-guided LLM fine-tuning.
AI.motivation: CVE data changes frequently, with tens of thousands of vulnerabilities updated after initial publication. Static LLM training causes outdated or conflicting knowledge when analyzing vulnerabilities, leading to factually incorrect answers and hallucinations. The paper aims to ensure LLMs can reliably use the latest CVE information while minimizing internal knowledge conflicts.
AI.method: They design a two-stage framework called CRVA-TGRAG. In the retrieval stage, they improve document recall and precision using Parent Document Segmentation and an ensemble retrieval strategy that combines semantic similarity search with classical inverted-index retrieval. In the generation stage, they fine-tune LLMs using teacher-guided preference optimization, leveraging retrieved CVE documents to steer the model toward accurate, up-to-date outputs and away from conflicting or outdated knowledge.
AI.result: Experiments show that CRVA-TGRAG retrieves the latest CVEs more accurately than external knowledge-base solutions and improves the reliability of LLM answers for vulnerability-related questions, with reduced hallucinations and conflicts, although exact metrics are not provided in the abstract.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework effectively mitigates knowledge conflicts and inconsistencies in CVE detection and analysis by jointly improving retrieval and generation. By integrating advanced RAG techniques with preference-based fine-tuning, it enables LLMs to provide more precise, current, and trustworthy cybersecurity vulnerability analyses than using static LLM knowledge alone.

title: Pushing the Limits of On-Device Streaming ASR: A Compact, High-Accuracy English Model for Low-Latency Inference
summary: Deploying high-quality automatic speech recognition (ASR) on edge devices requires models that jointly optimize accuracy, latency, and memory footprint while operating entirely on CPU without GPU acceleration. We conduct a systematic empirical study of state-of-the-art ASR architectures, encompassing encoder-decoder, transducer, and LLM-based paradigms, evaluated across batch, chunked, and streaming inference modes. Through a comprehensive benchmark of over 50 configurations spanning OpenAI Whisper, NVIDIA Nemotron, Parakeet TDT, Canary, Conformer Transducer, and Qwen3-ASR, we identify NVIDIA's Nemotron Speech Streaming as the strongest candidate for real-time English streaming on resource-constrained hardware. We then re-implement the complete streaming inference pipeline in ONNX Runtime and conduct a controlled evaluation of multiple post-training quantization strategies, including importance-weighted k-quant, mixed-precision schemes, and round-to-nearest quantization, combined with graph-level operator fusion. These optimizations reduce the model from 2.47 GB to as little as 0.67 GB while maintaining word error rate (WER) within 1% absolute of the full-precision PyTorch baseline. Our recommended configuration, the int4 k-quant variant, achieves 8.20% average streaming WER across eight standard benchmarks, running comfortably faster than real-time on CPU with 0.56 s algorithmic latency, establishing a new quality-efficiency Pareto point for on-device streaming ASR.

AI.tldr: Systematic benchmark of modern ASR models on CPU-only edge settings, followed by an optimized ONNX Runtime implementation and quantization of NVIDIA Nemotron Speech Streaming, yielding a highly efficient int4 k-quant model with near-baseline accuracy and strong real-time streaming performance.
AI.motivation: Edge devices often lack GPUs and have tight latency and memory constraints, yet users demand high-quality, real-time speech recognition. Existing ASR work typically optimizes accuracy on powerful hardware or focuses on a single model family, leaving a gap in understanding which architectures and deployment strategies best balance accuracy, speed, and footprint for CPU-only, on-device streaming ASR.
AI.method: (1) Empirically benchmark over 50 ASR configurations (encoder-decoder, transducer, and LLM-based) across batch, chunked, and streaming modes on CPU, comparing models such as Whisper, Nemotron, Parakeet TDT, Canary, Conformer Transducer, and Qwen3-ASR. (2) Identify Nemotron Speech Streaming as the best-performing candidate for real-time English streaming under resource constraints. (3) Re-implement its streaming pipeline in ONNX Runtime. (4) Systematically evaluate multiple post-training quantization strategies (importance-weighted k-quant, mixed precision, round-to-nearest) along with graph-level operator fusion to reduce memory and improve efficiency. (5) Measure WER, latency, and model size across quantized variants.
AI.result: Nemotron Speech Streaming emerges as the top model for CPU-only real-time streaming. The ONNX Runtime implementation with aggressive but carefully chosen quantization and graph optimizations shrinks the model size from 2.47 GB to 0.67 GB at best, while keeping WER within 1% absolute of the full-precision PyTorch baseline. The int4 k-quant configuration yields an average streaming WER of 8.20% over eight benchmarks and runs faster than real-time with 0.56 s algorithmic latency on CPU.
AI.conclusion: A carefully selected and engineered transducer-style ASR model, specifically Nemotron Speech Streaming, can be heavily optimized for CPU-only edge deployment via ONNX Runtime and advanced quantization while preserving near-baseline recognition quality. The proposed int4 k-quant configuration establishes a new Pareto frontier for on-device streaming ASR, jointly optimizing accuracy, latency, and memory footprint for real-time English applications on resource-constrained hardware.

title: Correcting Suppressed Log-Probabilities in Language Models with Post-Transformer Adapters
summary: Alignment-tuned language models frequently suppress factual log-probabilities on politically sensitive topics despite retaining the knowledge in their hidden representations. We show that a 786K-parameter (approximately 0.02% of the base model) post-transformer adapter, trained on frozen hidden states, corrects this suppression on 31 ideology-discriminating facts across Qwen3-4B, 8B, and 14B. The adapter memorizes all 15 training facts and generalizes to 11--39% of 16 held-out facts across 5 random splits per scale, with zero knowledge regressions via anchored training. Both gated (SwiGLU) and ungated (linear bottleneck) adapters achieve comparable results; neither consistently outperforms the other (Fisher exact p > 0.09 at all scales). On instruct models, the adapter corrects log-probability rankings. When applied at all token positions during generation, the adapter produces incoherent output; however, when applied only at the current prediction position (last-position-only), the adapter produces coherent, less censored text. A logit-space adapter operating after token projection fails to produce coherent generation at any application mode, suggesting hidden-state intervention is the correct level for generation correction. A previously undocumented silent gradient bug in Apple MLX explains all null results in earlier iterations of this work: the standard pattern nn.value_and_grad(model, fn)(model.parameters()) returns zero gradients without error; the correct pattern nn.value_and_grad(model, fn)(model, data) resolves this. We provide a minimal reproduction and discuss implications for other adapter research using MLX.

AI.tldr: Tiny post-transformer adapters can undo alignment-induced censorship in LMs by acting on hidden states, not logits, and a MLX gradient bug previously obscured this effect.
AI.motivation: Alignment tuning often causes large language models to suppress or mis-rank factual knowledge on politically sensitive topics, even when the underlying information is still encoded in hidden states. The authors want to test whether small, targeted interventions can restore truthful log-probabilities and generation without retraining the whole model, and to understand at what level of the network such interventions are effective. They are also motivated by unexplained null results in prior experiments, which they trace to a silent gradient bug in Apple MLX, with implications for other adapter-based research.
AI.method: They freeze Qwen3 models at 4B, 8B, and 14B parameters and train small post-transformer adapters (~786K parameters, about 0.02% of the base model) on their hidden states. These adapters are trained on 15 ideology-discriminating factual statements, using anchored training to avoid regressions on other knowledge, and evaluated on 16 held-out facts across 5 random train/validation splits for each scale. They compare gated (SwiGLU) vs ungated (linear bottleneck) adapter architectures using Fisher’s exact test, and study different modes of applying the adapter during generation: at all positions vs only at the current (last) position. They also implement a logit-space adapter operating after token projection to contrast with hidden-state intervention. During development, they diagnose a silent gradient issue in Apple MLX, compare incorrect and correct usage patterns of nn.value_and_grad, and provide a minimal reproduction.
AI.result: The post-transformer adapter fully memorizes all 15 training facts and generalizes to between 11% and 39% of the 16 held-out ideology-discriminating facts, depending on model scale and random split, with no observed regressions due to the anchored training scheme. Gated and ungated adapters perform similarly across all tested scales, with no statistically significant difference (Fisher exact p-values all above 0.09). On instruction-tuned models, the adapter successfully restores or corrects log-probability rankings that were previously suppressed by alignment tuning. When applied at all token positions during generation, the adapter yields incoherent text, but applying it only at the last (current prediction) position restores coherent, less censored generations. The logit-space adapter fails to produce coherent text under any application mode, indicating that manipulation at the logit layer is not effective for this purpose. The authors confirm that earlier null results were entirely due to a bug in Apple MLX where the common invocation pattern nn.value_and_grad(model, fn)(model.parameters()) silently returned zero gradients; using nn.value_and_grad(model, fn)(model, data) fixes the issue.
AI.conclusion: A very small post-transformer adapter can reliably counteract alignment-induced suppression of specific factual knowledge in large language models by operating on hidden states rather than logits, without retraining the full model and without causing observable regressions elsewhere when anchored training is used. Both simple linear and gated SwiGLU adapters are viable, with no clear advantage to gating in this setting. For generation, the correct intervention granularity matters: applying the adapter only at the last token position preserves coherence while reducing censorship, whereas applying it at all positions or only in logit space breaks generation quality. The discovery of a silent gradient bug in Apple MLX underscores the need for careful tooling and may affect prior adapter research built on MLX, so they document a minimal reproduction and the correct usage pattern to avoid zero-gradient failures.

title: Improving Machine Learning Performance with Synthetic Augmentation
summary:
Synthetic augmentation is increasingly used to mitigate data scarcity in financial machine learning, yet its statistical role remains poorly understood. We formalize synthetic augmentation as a modification of the effective training distribution and show that it induces a structural bias--variance trade-off: while additional samples may reduce estimation error, they may also shift the population objective whenever the synthetic distribution deviates from regions relevant under evaluation. To isolate informational gains from mechanical sample-size effects, we introduce a size-matched null augmentation and a finite-sample, non-parametric block permutation test that remains valid under weak temporal dependence.
  We evaluate this framework in both controlled Markov-switching environments and real financial datasets, including high-frequency option trade data and a daily equity panel. Across generators spanning bootstrap, copula-based models, variational autoencoders, diffusion models, and TimeGAN, we vary augmentation ratio, model capacity, task type, regime rarity, and signal-to-noise. We show that synthetic augmentation is beneficial only in variance-dominant regimes, such as persistent volatility forecasting-while it deteriorates performance in bias-dominant settings, including near-efficient directional prediction. Rare-regime targeting can improve domain-specific metrics but may conflict with unconditional permutation inference. Our results provide a structural perspective on when synthetic data improves financial learning performance and when it induces persistent distributional distortion.

AI.tldr: The paper studies when synthetic data augmentation helps or hurts financial machine learning, framing it as a bias–variance trade-off and providing a testing framework to separate real informational gains from mere sample-size effects.
AI.motivation: In finance, labeled data are often scarce or limited to specific regimes, so practitioners increasingly rely on synthetic data generators (bootstraps, generative models, etc.) to augment datasets. However, there is little formal understanding of how such augmentation changes the effective training distribution and under what conditions it actually improves predictive performance versus introducing distortions. The authors aim to provide a structural, statistically grounded perspective on this practice.
AI.method: They formalize synthetic augmentation as changing the effective training distribution and analyze how this induces a structural bias–variance trade-off. To empirically separate informational gains from mechanical gains due to more samples, they define a size-matched null augmentation and propose a finite-sample, non-parametric block permutation test that remains valid under weak temporal dependence, appropriate for time-series. They then run controlled experiments in Markov-switching models and real financial datasets (high-frequency option trades, daily equity panel) using multiple classes of synthetic generators (bootstrap, copula-based models, VAEs, diffusion models, TimeGAN). They systematically vary augmentation ratio, model capacity, prediction task, regime rarity, and signal-to-noise ratio.
AI.result: The experiments show that synthetic augmentation improves performance primarily in variance-dominant regimes, such as persistent volatility forecasting, where estimation error is the main bottleneck and extra data help. In bias-dominant regimes, like near-efficient directional return prediction, augmentation tends to degrade performance because the synthetic distribution shifts the population objective away from what matters at evaluation. Targeting rare regimes with synthetic data can improve specific domain metrics but may contradict conclusions from unconditional permutation-based inference.
AI.conclusion: Synthetic data augmentation in financial ML should be viewed as modifying the training distribution, not just increasing sample size, and it introduces a fundamental bias–variance trade-off. It is beneficial mainly when variance reduction is crucial and harmful when bias from distributional distortion dominates, especially in near-efficient markets or directional prediction tasks. Their testing framework and empirical results give guidance on when and how to use synthetic augmentation and caution against uncritical application that may induce persistent misalignment with the evaluation distribution.

title: QU-NLP at ArchEHR-QA 2026: Two-Stage QLoRA Fine-Tuning of Qwen3-4B for Patient-Oriented Clinical Question Answering and Evidence Sentence Alignment
comment: Accepted for publication at CL4Health 2026 workshop, LREC2026 conference
summary: We present a unified system addressing both Subtask 3 (answer generation) and Subtask 4 (evidence sentence alignment) of the ArchEHR-QA Shared Task. For Subtask 3, we apply two-stage Quantised Low-Rank Adaptation (QLoRA) to Qwen3-4B loaded in 4-bit NF4 quantisation: first on 30,000 samples from the emrQA-MedSQuAD corpus to establish clinical domain competence, then on the 20 annotated development cases to learn the task-specific output style. Our system achieves an overall score of 32.87 on the official test-2026 split (BLEU = 9.42, ROUGE-L = 27.04, SARI = 55.42, BERTScore = 43.00, AlignScore = 25.28, MEDCON = 37.04). For Subtask 4, we develop a weighted ensemble of three retrieval methods - BM25 with relative thresholding, TF-IDF cosine similarity, and a fine-tuned cross-encoder - to identify note sentences supporting a given gold answer, achieving a micro-F1 of 67.16 on the 100-case test set. Experiments reveal that both subtasks expose the same fundamental challenge: 20 annotated training cases are insufficient to distinguish relevant from irrelevant clinical sentences, pointing to data augmentation as the highest-leverage future direction.

AI.tldr: A unified system tackles both clinical QA answer generation and evidence sentence alignment using a two-stage QLoRA-tuned Qwen model and an ensemble retriever, but performance is limited by having only 20 annotated training cases.
AI.motivation: The ArchEHR-QA Shared Task requires models to both generate accurate clinical answers and identify supporting evidence sentences in electronic health records. Existing models struggle with low supervision, domain specificity, and the need for faithful evidence alignment. The authors aim to build a single system that can reasonably handle both subtasks under very limited annotated data and explore how data scarcity affects performance.
AI.method: For Subtask 3 (answer generation), they fine-tune the Qwen3-4B language model with 4-bit NF4 quantisation using a two-stage QLoRA approach: first, domain adaptation on 30k emrQA-MedSQuAD clinical QA pairs, then task-specific tuning on the 20 provided development cases to match the required answer style. For Subtask 4 (evidence alignment), they construct a weighted ensemble of three retrieval strategies to select supporting sentences: BM25 with a relative score threshold, TF-IDF cosine similarity, and a fine-tuned cross-encoder that scores sentence–answer pairs. They evaluate with shared-task metrics on held-out test sets.
AI.result: On the official test-2026 split for Subtask 3, the system obtains an overall score of 32.87 with component metrics BLEU 9.42, ROUGE-L 27.04, SARI 55.42, BERTScore 43.00, AlignScore 25.28, and MEDCON 37.04. For Subtask 4, the ensemble evidencer achieves a micro-F1 of 67.16 on the 100-case test set, indicating moderately strong evidence retrieval performance despite limited supervision.
AI.conclusion: Both answer generation and evidence alignment are fundamentally constrained by the extremely small set of 20 annotated training cases, which makes it hard for the model to reliably distinguish relevant from irrelevant clinical sentences. The authors conclude that the most promising direction to improve performance is data augmentation or otherwise expanding effective training supervision, rather than solely refining architectures or training tricks.

title: Geometric Metrics for MoE Specialization: From Fisher Information to Early Failure Detection
summary: Expert specialization is fundamental to Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) model success, yet existing metrics (cosine similarity, routing entropy) lack theoretical grounding and yield inconsistent conclusions under reparameterization. We present an information-geometric framework providing the first rigorous characterization of MoE specialization dynamics. Our key insight is that expert routing distributions evolve on the probability simplex equipped with the Fisher information metric, enabling formal analysis via Riemannian geometry. We prove that standard heuristic metrics violate parameterization invariance (Theorem 1), establish that specialization corresponds to geodesic flow with quantified approximation bounds (Theorem 2), and derive a failure predictor with theoretical threshold justification (Theorem 3). The framework introduces two principled metrics: Fisher Specialization Index (FSI) achieving r=0.91+/-0.02 correlation with downstream performance, and Fisher Heterogeneity Score (FHS) predicting training failure at 10% completion with AUC=0.89+/-0.03 -- outperforming validation-loss-based early stopping by 23% while requiring 40x fewer compute cycles. We validate intervention protocols achieving 87% recovery rate when FHS>1 is detected. Comprehensive experiments across language modeling (WikiText-103, C4), vision MoE (ImageNet), and scaling studies (8-64 experts, 125M-2.7B parameters) validate our theoretical predictions.

AI.tldr: Introduces an information-geometric, Fisher-metric-based framework to rigorously quantify and predict expert specialization and failure in Mixture-of-Experts models, yielding new metrics that correlate strongly with performance and enable early failure detection and intervention.
AI.motivation: Existing measures of expert specialization in Mixture-of-Experts models, such as cosine similarity and routing entropy, lack theoretical foundations and are not invariant under reparameterization, which leads to inconsistent or misleading conclusions about when and how experts specialize. This creates difficulty in understanding specialization dynamics, predicting MoE success or failure, and designing reliable early-stopping or intervention strategies. The paper aims to provide a principled, theoretically grounded framework to analyze and quantify specialization dynamics in MoEs.
AI.method: The authors model the evolution of expert routing distributions as trajectories on the probability simplex endowed with the Fisher information metric, using tools from information geometry and Riemannian geometry. They prove that commonly used heuristic metrics are not parameterization-invariant (Theorem 1). They show that true specialization corresponds approximately to geodesic flow on this Fisher information manifold, and derive bounds on this approximation (Theorem 2). Building on this geometric view, they derive a theoretically justified failure predictor and a threshold for detecting problematic training runs (Theorem 3). From this framework they define two new metrics: the Fisher Specialization Index (FSI) to measure the quality of specialization, and the Fisher Heterogeneity Score (FHS) to detect unstable or failing training dynamics. They then empirically evaluate these metrics and derived intervention protocols across multiple MoE settings.
AI.result: The Fisher Specialization Index (FSI) exhibits a strong correlation (r = 0.91 ± 0.02) with downstream performance, indicating it is a robust indicator of useful specialization. The Fisher Heterogeneity Score (FHS) can predict training failure as early as 10% into training with an AUC of 0.89 ± 0.03. Compared with validation-loss-based early stopping, FHS-based prediction improves failure detection performance by 23% while using 40 times less compute. Additionally, when runs are flagged with FHS > 1 and the proposed interventions are applied, 87% of those runs recover successfully. These findings are consistently validated across language modeling tasks (WikiText-103, C4), vision MoE on ImageNet, and scaling regimes from 8 to 64 experts and 125M to 2.7B parameters.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that an information-geometric treatment of Mixture-of-Experts routing, via the Fisher information metric on the probability simplex, yields the first principled description of specialization dynamics. This approach exposes the limitations of traditional heuristic metrics, provides invariant and theoretically justified metrics (FSI and FHS) that tightly track model quality and failure, and enables compute-efficient, early prediction of failing training runs. The validated intervention protocols demonstrate that these metrics are not only diagnostic but actionable, supporting more reliable and efficient training of large-scale MoE models across modalities and scales.

title: Listen, Correct, and Feed Back: Spoken Pedagogical Feedback Generation
summary: Grammatical error correction (GEC) and explanation (GEE) have made rapid progress, but real teaching scenarios also require \emph{learner-friendly pedagogical feedback} that is actionable, level-appropriate, and encouraging. We introduce \textbf{SPFG} (\textbf{S}poken \textbf{P}edagogical \textbf{F}eedback \textbf{G}eneration), a dataset built based on the Speak \& Improve Challenge 2025 corpus, pairing fluency-oriented transcriptions with GEC targets and \emph{human-verified} teacher-style feedback, including preferred/rejected feedback pairs for preference learning. We study a transcript-based Spoken Grammatical Error Correction (SGEC) setting and evaluate three instruction-tuned LLMs (Qwen2.5, Llama-3.1, and GLM-4), comparing supervised fine-tuning (SFT) with preference-based alignment (using DPO and KTO) for jointly generating corrections and feedback. Results show that SFT provides the most consistent improvements, while DPO/KTO yield smaller or mixed gains, and that correction quality and feedback quality are weakly coupled. Our implementation is available at https://github.com/Skywalker-Harrison/spfg.

AI.tldr: They build a new dataset and benchmark (SPFG) for generating spoken grammatical error corrections plus learner-friendly feedback, and compare fine-tuning vs preference-based alignment of LLMs for this joint task.
AI.motivation: Existing grammatical error correction and explanation systems don’t fully meet real classroom needs: teachers must give feedback that is not only correct but also pedagogically useful—actionable, level-appropriate, and encouraging—especially for spoken language learners. There is a lack of datasets and benchmarks that combine spoken fluency-oriented transcripts, gold GEC targets, and high-quality, teacher-style feedback with preference labels for training and evaluating such systems.
AI.method: 1) Construct SPFG, a dataset derived from the Speak & Improve Challenge 2025 corpus. They pair spoken, fluency-oriented transcripts with target grammatical corrections and human-verified teacher-style feedback. This feedback includes both preferred and rejected variants to support preference learning. 2) Define a transcript-based Spoken Grammatical Error Correction (SGEC) setting where models must both correct errors and generate pedagogical feedback. 3) Evaluate three instruction-tuned LLMs (Qwen2.5, Llama-3.1, GLM-4). They compare (a) supervised fine-tuning (SFT) on the paired data and (b) preference-based alignment using DPO and KTO, focusing on the joint objective of producing corrections plus feedback. 4) Analyze how these training strategies affect both correction quality and feedback quality, and the relationship between the two.
AI.result: Supervised fine-tuning consistently improves both grammatical correction and feedback quality across the evaluated models. In contrast, preference-based alignment methods (DPO and KTO) produce smaller or mixed improvements, suggesting they are less reliable for this joint correction+feedback task in the current setup. Empirically, the quality of grammatical correction and the quality of generated pedagogical feedback are only weakly correlated: better corrections do not automatically lead to better, more learner-friendly feedback.
AI.conclusion: SPFG provides a new benchmark and resource for research on spoken grammatical error correction with pedagogical feedback, including a valuable signal for preference learning. For the models and settings tested, supervised fine-tuning is the most robust way to improve joint correction and feedback generation, while preference-based alignment brings limited or inconsistent benefits. The weak coupling between correction and feedback quality indicates that future systems should model pedagogical feedback as a distinct objective rather than assuming that improving correction alone will yield better teaching-style responses.

title: Perspective on Bias in Biomedical AI: Preventing Downstream Healthcare Disparities
summary: Healthcare disparities persist across socioeconomic boundaries, often attributed to unequal access to screening, diagnostics, and therapeutics. However, this perspective highlights that critical biases can emerge much earlier, during data collection and research prioritization, long before clinical implementation in cases where the focus of the studies and the data that is collected is at the molecular level. A vast number of studies focus on collecting omics data but the demographic information associated with these datasets is often not reported in the studies, and when it is reported, it shows big biases. An automated analysis of 4719 PubMed-indexed omics publications from 2015 to 2024 reveals that only a small fraction report ancestry or ethnicity information, with ancestry reporting improving slightly. Analysis of large-scale datasets commonly used for model training, such as CellxGene and GEO, reveals substantial population bias where European-ancestry data dominates. As biomedical foundation models become central to biomedical discovery with a paradigm in which base models are pretrained on large datasets and reusing them time and again for many different downstream tasks, they risk perpetuating or amplifying these early-stage biases, leading to cascading inequities that regulatory interventions cannot fully reverse. We propose a community-wide focus on three foundational principles: Provenance, Openness, and Evaluation Transparency to improve equity and robustness in biomedical AI. This approach aims to foster biomedical innovation that more effectively serves underserved populations and improves health outcomes.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that molecular/omics datasets and publications rarely report or include diverse ancestries, leading to biased foundation models in biomedical AI, and proposes principles to mitigate this.
AI.motivation: To highlight that health inequities originate not only from clinical access but from early research stages, particularly in molecular/omics data collection and documentation, where demographic under-reporting and population bias are pervasive, threatening to encode inequity into emerging biomedical foundation models.
AI.method: The authors perform an automated text-mining analysis of 4719 PubMed-indexed omics papers (2015–2024) to quantify reporting of ancestry/ethnicity, and inspect major public omics resources such as CellxGene and GEO for population distribution. They then conceptually analyze how these biases propagate into foundation models and formulate a set of guiding principles.
AI.result: They find that only a small share of omics publications report ancestry or ethnicity, with only modest improvement over time. Public omics repositories widely used for model pretraining are dominated by European-ancestry samples, demonstrating substantial and systematic population imbalance. They argue that these skewed inputs are likely to bias downstream biomedical foundation models and that later-stage regulation is insufficient to fully undo the damage.
AI.conclusion: The authors conclude that ensuring equity in biomedical AI requires intervening at the level of data and research pipelines through three principles: Provenance (clear, standardized documentation of sample origins and demographics), Openness (broadly accessible data and methods), and Evaluation Transparency (explicit, stratified performance reporting). Adopting these practices across the community is necessary to avoid amplifying existing health disparities and to better serve underserved populations.

title: An Underexplored Frontier: Large Language Models for Rare Disease Patient Education and Communication -- A scoping review
summary:
Rare diseases affect over 300 million people worldwide and are characterized by complex care pathways, limited clinical expertise, and substantial unmet communication needs throughout the long patient journey. Recent advances in large language models (LLMs) offer new opportunities to support patient education and communication, yet their application in rare diseases remains unclear.
  We conducted a scoping review of studies published between January 2022 and March 2026 across major databases, identifying 12 studies on LLM-based rare disease patient education and communication. Data were extracted on study characteristics, application scenarios, model usage, and evaluation methods, and synthesized using descriptive and qualitative analyses.
  The literature is highly recent and dominated by general-purpose models, particularly ChatGPT. Most studies focus on patient question answering using curated question sets, with limited use of real-world data or longitudinal communication scenarios. Evaluations are primarily centered on accuracy, with limited attention to patient-centered dimensions such as readability, empathy, and communication quality. Multilingual communication is rarely addressed.
  Overall, the field remains at an early stage. Future research should prioritize patient-centered design, domain-adapted methods, and real-world deployment to support safe, adaptive, and effective communication in rare diseases.

AI.tldr: Scoping review of recent studies using large language models to support education and communication for rare disease patients, finding early-stage, accuracy-focused work dominated by general-purpose models and calling for more patient-centered, domain-adapted, real-world approaches.
AI.motivation: Rare disease patients face complex care pathways, scarce specialist knowledge, and ongoing communication gaps; recent progress in LLMs could potentially alleviate these issues, but their concrete use and evidence base in rare disease contexts were unclear and needed systematic mapping.
AI.method: The authors conducted a scoping review of literature from January 2022 to March 2026 across major databases, selected 12 relevant studies on LLM-based rare disease patient education and communication, extracted data on study characteristics, application scenarios, model types and usage, and evaluation methods, and synthesized the findings using descriptive statistics and qualitative analysis.
AI.result: The review found that the existing work is very recent and largely uses general-purpose LLMs such as ChatGPT, mostly in patient question-answering settings with curated questions rather than real-world or longitudinal interactions; evaluations predominantly measure accuracy, with comparatively little focus on dimensions important to patients such as readability, empathy, communication quality, and multilingual support.
AI.conclusion: Research on LLMs for rare disease patient communication is nascent; to meaningfully improve care, future work should emphasize patient-centered design, adapt models to the rare-disease domain, and test them in real-world settings to ensure safe, flexible, and effective communication support.

title: Mind DeepResearch Technical Report
summary: We present \textbf{Mind DeepResearch (MindDR)}, an efficient multi-agent deep research framework that achieves leading performance with only \textasciitilde30B-parameter models through a meticulously designed data synthesis and multi-stage training pipeline. The core innovation of MindDR lies in a collaborative three-agent architecture (Planning Agent, DeepSearch Agent, and Report Agent) and a four-stage agent-specialized training pipeline comprising SFT cold-start, Search-RL, Report-RL and preference alignment. With this regime, MindDR demonstrates competitive performance even with \textasciitilde30B-scale models. Specifically, MindDR achieves 45.7\% on BrowseComp-ZH, 42.8\% on BrowseComp, 46.5\% on WideSearch, 75.0\% on xbench-DS, and 52.5 on DeepResearch Bench, outperforming comparable-scale open-source agent systems and rivaling larger-scale models. MindDR has been deployed as an online product in Li Auto. Furthermore, we introduce \textbf{MindDR Bench}, a curated benchmark of 500 real-world Chinese queries from our internal product user interactions, evaluated through a comprehensive multi-dimensional rubric system rather than relying on a single RACE metric. On MindDR Bench, MindDR achieves a state-of-the-art score of 51.8.

AI.tldr: Mind DeepResearch (MindDR) is a multi-agent deep research framework that, using only ~30B-parameter models and a specialized training pipeline, matches or surpasses larger systems on web-based research benchmarks and a new real-world benchmark, MindDR Bench.
AI.motivation: Large deep-research agents typically rely on very large models and simplistic evaluation metrics, making them costly and not always aligned with real product needs. The paper aims to build an efficient, smaller-scale yet highly capable research system and to introduce a more realistic benchmark based on real user queries with richer evaluation dimensions.
AI.method: The authors design a three-agent architecture (Planning Agent, DeepSearch Agent, Report Agent) that collaborate for complex research tasks. They train these agents via a four-stage pipeline: (1) supervised fine-tuning (SFT) for cold-start behavior, (2) Search-RL to optimize search and browsing behaviors, (3) Report-RL to improve final report quality, and (4) preference alignment to better match human preferences. They also construct MindDR Bench, a benchmark of 500 real-world Chinese queries with a multi-dimensional rubric for evaluation instead of just a single automatic metric.
AI.result: MindDR attains strong performance on multiple deep research benchmarks using only ~30B-parameter models: 45.7% on BrowseComp-ZH, 42.8% on BrowseComp, 46.5% on WideSearch, 75.0% on xbench-DS, and 52.5 on DeepResearch Bench, outperforming comparable open-source systems at similar scale and approaching or rivaling larger models. On the new MindDR Bench, it reaches a state-of-the-art score of 51.8. The system has also been deployed in a real product at Li Auto.
AI.conclusion: A carefully designed multi-agent architecture combined with staged, agent-specialized training can enable ~30B-parameter models to perform deep research tasks competitively with much larger systems. The new MindDR Bench offers a more realistic, multi-dimensional evaluation of deep research capabilities based on real user queries, and MindDR achieves state-of-the-art results on it while being efficient enough for real-world deployment.

title: Internal Knowledge Without External Expression: Probing the Generalization Boundary of a Classical Chinese Language Model
summary:
We train a 318M-parameter Transformer language model from scratch on a curated corpus of 1.56 billion tokens of pure Classical Chinese, with zero English characters or Arabic numerals. Through systematic out-of-distribution (OOD) testing, we investigate whether the model can distinguish known from unknown inputs, and crucially, whether it can express this distinction in its generated text.
  We find a clear dissociation between internal and external uncertainty. Internally, the model exhibits a perplexity jump ratio of 2.39x between real and fabricated historical events (p = 8.9e-11, n = 92 per group), with semi-fabricated events (real figures + fictional events) showing the highest perplexity (4.24x, p = 1.1e-16), demonstrating genuine factual encoding beyond syntactic pattern matching. Externally, however, the model never learns to express uncertainty: classical Chinese epistemic markers appear at lower rates for OOD questions (3.5%) than for in-distribution questions (8.3%, p = 0.023), reflecting rhetorical conventions rather than genuine metacognition.
  We replicate both findings across three languages (Classical Chinese, English, Japanese), three writing systems, and eight models from 110M to 1.56B parameters. We further show that uncertainty expression frequency is determined entirely by training data conventions, with Classical Chinese models showing a "humility paradox" (more hedging for known topics), while Japanese models almost never hedge. We argue that metacognitive expression -- the ability to say "I don't know" -- does not emerge from language modeling alone and requires explicit training signals such as RLHF.
