AI.tldr: The paper studies whether language models trained only with next-token prediction can both internally recognize unknown information and externally express uncertainty, finding a strong internal OOD signal but almost no honest “I don’t know” behavior without explicit training.
AI.motivation: To test if metacognitive-like behavior—recognizing what one does not know and saying so—emerges purely from language modeling, especially in a low-resource, stylistically distinct setting (pure Classical Chinese) and across languages. The authors want to separate internal uncertainty signals from the model’s outward verbal hedging, and to see how much of uncertainty expression is just copying human rhetorical conventions from the training data.
AI.method: They train a 318M-parameter Transformer on 1.56B tokens of strictly Classical Chinese text. They design in-distribution and out-of-distribution question sets about historical events, including real, fully fabricated, and semi-fabricated (real people in fake scenarios) events. They measure internal uncertainty using perplexity differences between these categories and analyze generated answers for epistemic markers (hedges, explicit admissions of ignorance). They replicate the experiments across three languages (Classical Chinese, English, Japanese), multiple scripts, and eight models of varying sizes, and compare hedging rates to the conventions present in the training data.
AI.result: The model’s internal uncertainty strongly distinguishes real from fake or semi-fake events: perplexity roughly doubles for fabricated events and is even higher for semi-fabricated ones, indicating real factual knowledge beyond surface statistics. However, in its outputs the model almost never expresses uncertainty appropriately: epistemic markers are actually less frequent for OOD queries than for in-distribution ones. Cross-lingual experiments show similar internal–external dissociations, and that hedging frequency is almost fully explained by training corpus style, e.g., Classical Chinese models hedge more on known topics while Japanese models rarely hedge at all.
AI.conclusion: Internal uncertainty and external uncertainty expression are dissociable in language models: next-token training yields strong internal OOD signals but does not produce genuine metacognitive expressions like “I don’t know.” Instead, verbal hedging is largely a stylistic artifact inherited from the training data. Therefore, honest uncertainty expression is unlikely to emerge from language modeling alone and requires additional supervised or RLHF-style training signals that explicitly reward appropriate admissions of ignorance.

title: Quantifying Cross-Query Contradictions in Multi-Query LLM Reasoning
comment: Accepted at the ICLR 2026 Workshop on Logical Reasoning of Large Language Models. 9 pages, 6 tables, code and data at https://huggingface.co/datasets/rohitspider/cross_query_benchmark
summary: Large language models frequently produce mutually inconsistent answers when reasoning over multiple related queries. We study case-file logical consistency: maintaining a globally satisfiable belief state across interdependent queries. We introduce a benchmark of 390 multi-query reasoning instances with entailment/contradiction/unknown labels and propose set-level metrics including Case Satisfiability Rate, Contradiction Density and Revision Cost. Our solver-augmented approach extracts commitments, verifies global satisfiability and performs counterexample-guided repair. Across four reasoning domains, our method substantially reduces cross-query contradictions (SetCons: 0.56 to 0.94) while preserving per-query accuracy, demonstrating that global coherence is critical for robust multi-query reasoning.

AI.tldr: The paper tackles the problem of large language models giving logically inconsistent answers across related questions, introducing a benchmark and metrics for multi-query logical consistency, and a solver-assisted method that repairs inconsistencies while keeping accuracy high.
AI.motivation: Large language models often answer related questions in ways that contradict each other, which is problematic for applications that require a coherent, globally consistent belief state rather than isolated answers. Existing evaluations mostly assess single-query accuracy and overlook whether a model’s answers can all be simultaneously true. The authors aim to define and measure this notion of cross-query consistency and to develop methods that enforce it.
AI.method: The authors define the task of case-file logical consistency: answering sets of interdependent queries while maintaining a jointly satisfiable belief state. They build a benchmark of 390 multi-query instances labeled with entailment, contradiction, or unknown relations among statements. They design set-level metrics like Case Satisfiability Rate (whether the whole set of answers is jointly satisfiable), Contradiction Density (how many contradictions arise), and Revision Cost (how much needs to change to restore consistency). Their approach augments the language model with an automated solver that: (1) extracts explicit logical commitments from the model’s answers, (2) checks whether these commitments are globally satisfiable, and (3) uses counterexamples from the solver to guide repairs of the model’s answers until a consistent set is found.
AI.result: On four different reasoning domains, their solver-augmented method significantly improves cross-query logical consistency. For example, their SetCons metric improves from 0.56 to 0.94, indicating a large reduction in contradictions across the answer sets, while maintaining the original per-question accuracy. This shows that enforcing consistency need not sacrifice performance on individual questions.
AI.conclusion: Global logical coherence across multiple related queries is a key dimension of robust reasoning for large language models, distinct from and complementary to per-query accuracy. By defining case-file logical consistency, providing a benchmark and metrics, and demonstrating a solver-based repair method that greatly reduces contradictions without hurting accuracy, the paper argues that future systems should explicitly model and enforce global belief-state consistency.

title: Dissecting Failure Dynamics in Large Language Model Reasoning
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) achieve strong performance through extended inference-time deliberation, yet how their reasoning failures arise remains poorly understood. By analyzing model-generated reasoning trajectories, we find that errors are not uniformly distributed but often originate from a small number of early transition points, after which reasoning remains locally coherent but globally incorrect. These transitions coincide with localized spikes in token-level entropy, and alternative continuations from the same intermediate state can still lead to correct solutions. Based on these observations, we introduce GUARD, a targeted inference-time framework that probes and redirects critical transitions using uncertainty signals. Empirical evaluations across multiple benchmarks confirm that interventions guided by these failure dynamics lead to more reliable reasoning outcomes. Our findings highlight the importance of understanding when and how reasoning first deviates, complementing existing approaches that focus on scaling inference-time computation.

AI.tldr: The paper studies where and how reasoning errors arise in LLM chain-of-thought, finds that mistakes typically emerge at a few early, high-entropy decision points, and proposes GUARD, an inference-time method that detects and redirects these critical transitions using uncertainty, improving reliability on reasoning benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Although large language models can reason better with long chain-of-thought style deliberation, they still fail in ways that are not well understood. Existing work mostly scales up inference-time compute (e.g., more samples, deeper reasoning) without carefully analyzing when the reasoning process first goes wrong or how local coherence can mask global errors. The authors want to pinpoint the dynamics of failure within reasoning trajectories to design more targeted, efficient interventions that improve reliability instead of just doing more brute-force sampling.
AI.method: 1) Empirically analyze model-generated reasoning traces on multiple tasks to locate where incorrect trajectories diverge from correct ones. 2) Measure token-level entropy along the chain-of-thought to see how uncertainty correlates with these divergence points. 3) Show that early errors are concentrated at a few critical transitions, that subsequent reasoning is locally consistent but globally wrong, and that alternative continuations from the same intermediate state can succeed. 4) Develop GUARD, an inference-time framework that monitors uncertainty signals (e.g., entropy spikes) to detect critical transitions, selectively probes alternative continuations at these points, and steers the reasoning toward correct solutions. 5) Evaluate GUARD against baselines on several reasoning benchmarks.
AI.result: The analysis reveals that reasoning errors tend to originate from a small number of early decision points characterized by localized spikes in token-level entropy, after which the model continues with coherent but incorrect reasoning. From these same intermediate states, alternative continuations often yield correct answers, indicating that the model has the necessary knowledge but makes the wrong move at critical junctures. Using these insights, the GUARD framework, which focuses computational effort on probing and redirecting these high-uncertainty transitions, achieves better reasoning reliability than baselines on multiple benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Reasoning failures in LLMs are not uniformly spread across the chain-of-thought but are concentrated at a few early, high-entropy transitions where the model first deviates from a correct path. Monitoring and intervening at these critical points, as done by the GUARD framework, can significantly improve reasoning reliability without simply scaling up overall inference-time compute. This suggests that understanding the dynamics of when and how reasoning breaks down is crucial and complementary to existing methods that focus on more or longer deliberation alone.

title: The PICCO Framework for Large Language Model Prompting: A Taxonomy and Reference Architecture for Prompt Structure
comment: Presents the novel PICCO framework for LLM prompting, derived through a structured multi-database search and rigorous comparative synthesis of 11 published prompting frameworks. Submitted in PDF/A format to preserve the structure and readability of several multi-page tables central to the framework and methodology; these contain dense structured information that is best preserved in PDF form
summary: Large language model (LLM) performance depends heavily on prompt design, yet prompt construction is often described and applied inconsistently. Our purpose was to derive a reference framework for structuring LLM prompts. This paper presents PICCO, a framework derived through a rigorous synthesis of 11 previously published prompting frameworks identified through a multi-database search. The analysis yields two main contributions. First, it proposes a taxonomy that distinguishes prompt frameworks, prompt elements, prompt generation, prompting techniques, and prompt engineering as related but non-equivalent concepts. Second, it derives a five-element reference architecture for prompt generation: Persona, Instructions, Context, Constraints, and Output (PICCO). For each element, we define its function, scope, and relationship to other elements, with the goal of improving conceptual clarity and supporting more systematic prompt design. Finally, to support application of the framework, we outline key concepts relevant to implementation, including prompting techniques (e.g., zero-shot, few-shot, chain-of-thought, ensembling, decomposition, and self-critique, with selected variants), human and automated approaches to iterative prompt engineering, responsible prompting considerations such as security, privacy, bias, and trust, and priorities for future research. This work is a conceptual and methodological contribution: it formalizes a common structure for prompt specification and comparison, but does not claim empirical validation of PICCO as an optimization method.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces PICCO, a five-part reference architecture (Persona, Instructions, Context, Constraints, Output) to systematically structure prompts for large language models, clarifying terminology around prompt design but not providing empirical performance gains.
AI.motivation: Prompt design strongly affects LLM performance, but existing guidance is fragmented, inconsistently defined, and lacks a unifying conceptual structure. The authors want to consolidate and formalize how prompts are described and constructed so practitioners can design, compare, and refine prompts more systematically.
AI.method: The authors conducted a multi-database literature search to identify 11 prior prompting frameworks, then performed a rigorous conceptual synthesis across them. From this they built a taxonomy distinguishing concepts such as prompt frameworks, prompt elements, prompt generation, prompting techniques, and prompt engineering, and derived a five-element reference architecture called PICCO.
AI.result: The study yields: (1) a taxonomy that clearly separates related but distinct notions in the prompting space, and (2) the PICCO framework, which decomposes any prompt into five elements—Persona, Instructions, Context, Constraints, and Output—each with a defined role, scope, and interaction with the others. The authors also catalog key prompting techniques, iterative engineering approaches (human and automated), and responsible use considerations related to security, privacy, bias, and trust.
AI.conclusion: PICCO provides a coherent, general structure for specifying and analyzing prompts and for comparing different prompting frameworks. It is a conceptual and methodological contribution rather than an empirically validated way to optimize LLM performance, and the authors highlight that future work should test and refine the framework and investigate its practical impact on outcomes.

title: TRACER: Trace-Based Adaptive Cost-Efficient Routing for LLM Classification
summary:
Every call to an LLM classification endpoint produces a labeled input-output pair already retained in production logs. These pairs constitute a free, growing training set: a lightweight surrogate trained on them can absorb a significant portion of future traffic at near-zero marginal inference cost. The open questions are when the surrogate is reliable enough to deploy, what it handles versus defers, and how that boundary evolves as data accumulates.
  We introduce TRACER (Trace-based Adaptive Cost-Efficient Routing), an open-source system that trains ML surrogates on an LLM's own production traces and governs deployment through a parity gate: the surrogate is activated only when its agreement with the LLM exceeds a user-specified threshold α. To make the routing boundary transparent, TRACER generates interpretability artifacts describing which input regions the surrogate handles, where it plateaus, and why it defers.
  On a 77-class intent benchmark with a Sonnet 4.6 teacher, TRACER achieves 83-100% surrogate coverage depending on the quality target α; on a 150-class benchmark, the surrogate fully replaces the teacher. On a natural language inference task, the parity gate correctly refuses deployment because the embedding representation cannot support reliable separation. The system is available as open-source software.

AI.tldr: TRACER uses logged LLM classification traces to train cheaper surrogate models and deploys them only when they match the LLM above a chosen parity threshold.
AI.motivation: LLM classification calls are expensive, yet production logs naturally accumulate labeled data (inputs plus LLM outputs). This creates an opportunity to train cheaper surrogate models that can handle a large share of future requests. However, it is unclear when such surrogates are reliable enough, which inputs they can safely handle, when they should defer back to the LLM, and how this routing boundary changes as more data is collected. The work aims to systematically exploit traces for cost reduction while preserving accuracy and providing transparency about routing decisions.
AI.method: The authors propose TRACER (Trace-based Adaptive Cost-Efficient Routing), an open-source system that (1) trains machine-learning surrogate models on production traces of an LLM used as a classifier, (2) evaluates the surrogate’s parity with the LLM on held-out traces, and (3) deploys the surrogate behind a parity gate that only routes queries to it when its agreement with the LLM exceeds a user-defined threshold α. TRACER also generates interpretability artifacts that characterize the surrogate’s coverage region, plateau behavior, and deferral regions, helping users understand where the surrogate is trustworthy and where it is not.
AI.result: On a 77-class intent classification benchmark with a Sonnet 4.6 LLM as teacher, TRACER’s surrogates can cover 83–100% of requests depending on the chosen parity threshold α. On a larger 150-class benchmark, the surrogate model successfully replaces the LLM fully. In contrast, on a natural language inference task, TRACER’s parity gate correctly identifies that the surrogate (based on embeddings) cannot reliably separate classes and thus refuses deployment, demonstrating the system’s safeguards.
AI.conclusion: Trace-based surrogate training can substantially offload LLM classification workloads at near-zero marginal inference cost when tasks admit a robust embedding-based representation. A parity-gating mechanism with an explicit quality target ensures that surrogates are only deployed where they closely match the LLM, and interpretability outputs clarify the routing boundary. TRACER shows that production traces can be safely and transparently exploited for cost-efficient routing, while also detecting when surrogate deployment is not viable, and is released as open-source software.

title: Chinese Language Is Not More Efficient Than English in Vibe Coding: A Preliminary Study on Token Cost and Problem-Solving Rate
summary: A claim has been circulating on social media and practitioner forums that Chinese prompts are more token-efficient than English for LLM coding tasks, potentially reducing costs by up to 40\%. This claim has influenced developers to consider switching to Chinese for ``vibe coding'' to save on API costs. In this paper, we conduct a rigorous empirical study using SWE-bench Lite, a benchmark of software engineering tasks, to evaluate whether this claim of Chinese token efficiency holds up to scrutiny. Our results reveal three key findings: First, the efficiency advantage of Chinese is not observed. Second, token cost varies by model architecture in ways that defy simple assumptions: while MiniMax-2.7 shows 1.28x higher token costs for Chinese, GLM-5 actually consumes fewer tokens with Chinese prompts. Third, and most importantly, we found that the success rate when prompting in Chinese is generally lower than in English across all models we tested. We also measure cost efficiency as expected cost per successful task -- jointly accounting for token consumption and task resolution rate. These findings should be interpreted as preliminary evidence rather than a definitive conclusion, given the limited number of models evaluated and the narrow set of benchmarks tested due to resource constraints; they indicate that language effects on token cost are model-dependent, and that practitioners should not expect cost savings or performance gains just by switching their prompt language to Chinese.

AI.tldr: The paper tests whether using Chinese prompts reduces token costs for LLM coding tasks and finds no general efficiency advantage, with Chinese often costing more and performing worse.
AI.motivation: A widespread belief in developer communities claims Chinese prompts are more token-efficient than English for coding with LLMs, potentially cutting API costs by up to 40%. This belief has practical impact, as developers consider switching languages to save money, but it lacks rigorous empirical validation.
AI.method: The authors run a controlled empirical study using SWE-bench Lite, a benchmark of software engineering tasks. They compare multiple LLMs on coding tasks using prompts in Chinese vs. English, and measure token counts, success rates, and a combined metric of cost efficiency (expected cost per successful task). They analyze how different model architectures respond to prompt language in terms of both token usage and task performance.
AI.result: They find: (1) Chinese prompts do not show a general token-efficiency advantage over English. (2) Token cost differences are model-dependent: for example, MiniMax-2.7 uses 1.28x more tokens with Chinese prompts, while GLM-5 uses fewer tokens with Chinese. (3) Success rates with Chinese prompts are generally lower than with English across all tested models. Overall cost efficiency (cost per successful task) does not favor Chinese prompts.
AI.conclusion: The popular claim that Chinese prompts are reliably more token-efficient and cheaper for LLM coding tasks is not supported by this study. Language effects on token cost and performance are model-dependent, and Chinese prompts often lead to lower success rates. Given the limited models and benchmarks tested, the findings are preliminary, but practitioners should not expect automatic cost savings or performance gains simply by switching from English to Chinese prompts.

title: MARS$^2$: Scaling Multi-Agent Tree Search via Reinforcement Learning for Code Generation
summary: Reinforcement learning (RL) paradigms have demonstrated strong performance on reasoning-intensive tasks such as code generation. However, limited trajectory diversity often leads to diminishing returns, which constrains the achievable performance ceiling. Search-enhanced RL alleviates this issue by introducing structured exploration, which remains constrained by the single-agent policy priors. Meanwhile, leveraging multiple interacting policies can acquire more diverse exploratory signals, but existing approaches are typically decoupled from structured search. We propose \textbf{MARS$^2$} (Multi-Agent Reinforced Tree-Search Scaling), a unified RL framework in which multiple independently-optimized agents collaborate within a shared tree-structured search environment. MARS$^2$ models the search tree as a learnable multi-agent interaction environment, enabling heterogeneous agents to collaboratively generate and refine candidate solutions within a shared search topology. To support effective learning, we introduce a path-level group advantage formulation based on tree-consistent reward shaping, which facilitates effective credit assignment across complex search trajectories. Experiments on code generation benchmarks show that MARS$^2$ consistently improves performance across diverse model combinations and training settings, demonstrating the effectiveness of coupling multi-agent collaboration with tree search for enhancing reinforcement learning. Our code is publicly available at https://github.com/TsinghuaC3I/MARTI.

AI.tldr: The paper presents MARS^2, a multi-agent reinforcement learning framework that integrates multiple independently trained agents into a shared tree-structured search environment to improve performance on reasoning-heavy tasks like code generation.
AI.motivation: Traditional RL for reasoning tasks, such as code generation, suffers from limited trajectory diversity, leading to diminishing performance gains. Search-enhanced RL improves exploration but is restricted by a single policy’s prior, while multi-agent methods provide diverse signals but are not tightly integrated with structured search. The authors aim to overcome these limitations by unifying multi-agent collaboration with tree-based search.
AI.method: The authors introduce MARS^2 (Multi-Agent Reinforced Tree-Search Scaling), which treats the search tree as a learnable multi-agent interaction environment. Multiple heterogeneous agents, each optimized independently, operate on a shared tree-structured search space to propose, refine, and evaluate candidate solutions. They design a path-level group advantage formulation with tree-consistent reward shaping to enable effective credit assignment along complex search trajectories, improving learning stability and efficiency across agents.
AI.result: On code generation benchmarks, MARS^2 consistently outperforms baselines across different model combinations and training configurations, indicating that combining multi-agent collaboration with tree search significantly enhances RL-based code generation performance.
AI.conclusion: Coupling multi-agent reinforcement learning with a shared tree-structured search environment yields more diverse and effective exploration, better credit assignment, and improved performance on reasoning-intensive tasks like code generation. MARS^2 offers a scalable and general framework for integrating multi-agent collaboration with search-enhanced RL, as evidenced by gains on standard code generation benchmarks.

title: Enhancing Mental Health Counseling Support in Bangladesh using Culturally-Grounded Knowledge
summary: Large language models (LLMs) show promise in generating supportive responses for mental health and counseling applications. However, their responses often lack cultural sensitivity, contextual grounding, and clinically appropriate guidance. This work addresses the gap of how to systematically incorporate domain-specific, clinically validated knowledge into LLMs to improve counseling quality. We utilize and compare two approaches, retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) and a knowledge graph (KG)-based method, designed to support para-counselors. Our KG is constructed manually and clinically validated, capturing causal relationships between stressors, interventions, and outcomes, with contributions from multidisciplinary people. We evaluated multiple LLMs in both settings using BERTScore F1 and SBERT cosine similarity, as well as human evaluation across five metrics, which is designed to directly measure the effectiveness of counseling beyond similarity at the surface level. The results show that KG-based approaches consistently improve contextual relevance, clinical appropriateness, and practical usability compared to RAG alone, demonstrating that structured, expert-validated knowledge plays a critical role in addressing LLMs limitations in counseling tasks.

AI.tldr: The paper compares retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) and a clinically validated knowledge graph (KG) to guide LLMs for mental-health counseling, finding KG-based methods yield more contextually relevant and clinically appropriate support.
AI.motivation: LLMs are increasingly used for mental health support, but their responses can be culturally insensitive, lack context, and fail to follow clinical best practices. There is a gap in how to inject rigorously validated, domain-specific counseling knowledge into LLMs so that their guidance is trustworthy and effective for para-counselors.
AI.method: The authors build a manually constructed, clinically validated knowledge graph encoding causal relations among stressors, interventions, and outcomes, contributed by multidisciplinary experts. They integrate this KG with LLMs and compare it against a standard RAG setup that retrieves domain texts. Multiple LLMs are evaluated under both conditions using automatic similarity metrics (BERTScore F1, SBERT cosine) and a human evaluation framework with five counseling-focused metrics that assess effectiveness beyond surface text similarity.
AI.result: Across evaluated LLMs, the KG-based setup consistently outperforms pure RAG on contextual relevance, clinical appropriateness, and practical usability of counseling responses, as confirmed by both automatic metrics and detailed human judgments.
AI.conclusion: Embedding structured, expert-validated counseling knowledge via a knowledge graph enables LLMs to produce higher-quality mental health counseling responses than RAG alone. Structured knowledge integration is key to mitigating current LLM limitations and improving safety and effectiveness in counseling support tools.

title: MEME-Fusion@CHiPSAL 2026: Multimodal Ablation Study of Hate Detection and Sentiment Analysis on Nepali Memes
summary: Hate speech detection in Devanagari-scripted social media memes presents compounded challenges: multimodal content structure, script-specific linguistic complexity, and extreme data scarcity in low-resource settings. This paper presents our system for the CHiPSAL 2026 shared task, addressing both Subtask A (binary hate speech detection) and Subtask B (three-class sentiment classification: positive, neutral, negative). We propose a hybrid cross-modal attention fusion architecture that combines CLIP (ViT-B/32) for visual encoding with BGE-M3 for multilingual text representation, connected through 4-head self-attention and a learnable gating network that dynamically weights modality contributions on a per-sample basis. Systematic evaluation across eight model configurations demonstrates that explicit cross-modal reasoning achieves a 5.9% F1-macro improvement over text-only baselines on Subtask A, while uncovering two unexpected but critical findings: English-centric vision models exhibit near-random performance on Devanagari script, and standard ensemble methods catastrophically degrade under data scarcity (N nearly equal to 850 per fold) due to correlated overfitting. The code can be accessed at https://github.com/Tri-Yantra-Technologies/MEME-Fusion/

AI.tldr: System for detecting hate speech and sentiment in Devanagari-scripted memes using cross-modal fusion of image and text encoders.
AI.motivation: Detecting hate speech in Devanagari memes is difficult due to multimodal memes, script-specific linguistic challenges, and very limited labeled data in low-resource settings.
AI.method: A hybrid cross-modal attention fusion model that uses CLIP (ViT-B/32) as the visual encoder and BGE-M3 for multilingual text, linked via 4-head self-attention and a learnable gating network that adaptively weights image vs. text per sample; evaluated across eight model variants for two subtasks in the CHiPSAL 2026 shared task.
AI.result: Cross-modal reasoning outperforms text-only models by 5.9% macro-F1 on binary hate speech detection; the experiments also show that English-centric vision models fail on Devanagari script and that typical ensemble methods perform very poorly under extreme data scarcity due to correlated overfitting.
AI.conclusion: Cross-modal attention fusion is effective for Devanagari meme hate speech detection under low-resource conditions, but using script-mismatched vision encoders and naive ensembling can be harmful; tailored cross-modal modeling and careful architecture choices are crucial.

title: Prompt Optimization Is a Coin Flip: Diagnosing When It Helps in Compound AI Systems
summary: Prompt optimization in compound AI systems is statistically indistinguishable from a coin flip: across 72 optimization runs on Claude Haiku (6 methods $\times$ 4 tasks $\times$ 3 repeats), 49% score below zero-shot; on Amazon Nova Lite, the failure rate is even higher. Yet on one task, all six methods improve over zero-shot by up to $+6.8$ points. What distinguishes success from failure? We investigate with 18,000 grid evaluations and 144 optimization runs, testing two assumptions behind end-to-end optimization tools like TextGrad and DSPy: (A) individual prompts are worth optimizing, and (B) agent prompts interact, requiring joint optimization. Interaction effects are never significant ($p > 0.52$, all $F < 1.0$), and optimization helps only when the task has exploitable output structure -- a format the model can produce but does not default to. We provide a two-stage diagnostic: an \$80 ANOVA pre-test for agent coupling, and a 10-minute headroom test that predicts whether optimization is worthwhile -- turning a coin flip into an informed decision.

AI.tldr: Prompt optimization in multi-agent AI systems often fails to beat zero-shot performance and behaves like a coin flip, unless the task has specific exploitable output structure.
AI.motivation: To understand why automated prompt optimization methods for compound AI systems (multi-agent or multi-prompt pipelines) so often fail to outperform simple zero-shot prompts, and to identify when optimization is actually worthwhile.
AI.method: The authors run extensive experiments: 72 prompt-optimization runs across 6 methods, 4 tasks, and 3 repetitions on Claude Haiku, plus experiments on Amazon Nova Lite. They add 18,000 grid evaluations and 144 optimization runs to test two assumptions of end-to-end tools like TextGrad and DSPy: (A) that individual prompts are worth optimizing, and (B) that prompts in an agent system interact and thus require joint optimization. They use ANOVA to test for interaction effects and design diagnostic tests (an ANOVA-based coupling test and a quick headroom test).
AI.result: They find that around half of optimization runs perform worse than zero-shot, with failure rates even higher on some models. Interaction effects between agent prompts are statistically insignificant (p > 0.52, all F < 1.0). Optimization only provides gains on tasks with exploitable output structure—formats the model can generate but does not default to. They design a two-stage diagnostic: a low-cost ANOVA pre-test for agent coupling and a fast headroom test that predicts whether optimization will be beneficial.
AI.conclusion: End-to-end prompt optimization for compound AI systems is generally unreliable and often no better than chance unless the task exhibits specific output structure that can be exploited. Agent-prompt coupling is not a meaningful factor in their studies. Practitioners should first run cheap diagnostics—a coupling ANOVA and a headroom test—before investing in large-scale optimization, turning an essentially random bet into a more informed decision about when optimization will pay off.

title: ReviewGrounder: Improving Review Substantiveness with Rubric-Guided, Tool-Integrated Agents
summary: The rapid rise in AI conference submissions has driven increasing exploration of large language models (LLMs) for peer review support. However, LLM-based reviewers often generate superficial, formulaic comments lacking substantive, evidence-grounded feedback. We attribute this to the underutilization of two key components of human reviewing: explicit rubrics and contextual grounding in existing work. To address this, we introduce REVIEWBENCH, a benchmark evaluating review text according to paper-specific rubrics derived from official guidelines, the paper's content, and human-written reviews. We further propose REVIEWGROUNDER, a rubric-guided, tool-integrated multi-agent framework that decomposes reviewing into drafting and grounding stages, enriching shallow drafts via targeted evidence consolidation. Experiments on REVIEWBENCH show that REVIEWGROUNDER, using a Phi-4-14B-based drafter and a GPT-OSS-120B-based grounding stage, consistently outperforms baselines with substantially stronger/larger backbones (e.g., GPT-4.1 and DeepSeek-R1-670B) in both alignment with human judgments and rubric-based review quality across 8 dimensions. The code is available \href{https://github.com/EigenTom/ReviewGrounder}{here}.

AI.tldr: They build a benchmark and a rubric-grounded multi-agent LLM system for peer review that outperforms larger models on review quality and alignment with humans.
AI.motivation: AI conference submissions are exploding, and people want to use LLMs to help with peer review. But current LLM-based reviews are often generic, shallow, and not well grounded in the paper or existing work, partly because they don’t use explicit review rubrics or rich context like humans do. There is a need for systematic evaluation and better methods that make LLM reviews more substantive and rubric-aligned.
AI.method: They (1) construct REVIEWBENCH, a benchmark where review texts are evaluated against paper-specific rubrics derived from official review guidelines, the paper’s own content, and human-written reviews; (2) propose REVIEWGROUNDER, a rubric-guided, tool-integrated multi-agent framework that splits reviewing into a drafting stage and a grounding stage. A drafter model first produces an initial, possibly shallow review. Then a separate grounding agent uses tools and the rubric to search, retrieve, and consolidate targeted evidence from the paper and related context to enrich and substantiate the draft. They implement REVIEWGROUNDER using a Phi-4-14B model as the drafter and a larger GPT-OSS-120B model for the grounding stage, and compare it to strong baselines, including GPT-4.1 and DeepSeek-R1-670B.
AI.result: On REVIEWBENCH, their framework consistently outperforms baselines, including much larger and more powerful single LLMs, in two main respects: (1) closer alignment with human review judgments, and (2) higher rubric-based review quality across 8 evaluation dimensions. This shows that structured, rubric- and evidence-grounded multi-agent setups can beat brute-force scaling in this review-assistance setting.
AI.conclusion: Systematically incorporating explicit rubrics and contextual grounding into LLM-based reviewing, via a multi-agent draft-and-ground architecture, leads to reviews that are more human-aligned and higher quality than those from larger monolithic models. REVIEWBENCH provides a dedicated benchmark for this setting, and REVIEWGROUNDER demonstrates that intelligent structure and tools can be more effective than just scaling model size for peer review assistance.

title: GDPR Auto-Formalization with AI Agents and Human Verification
summary: We study the overall process of automatic formalization of GDPR provisions using large language models, within a human-in-the-loop verification framework. Rather than aiming for full autonomy, we adopt a role-specialized workflow in which LLM-based AI components, operating in a multi-agent setting with iterative feedback, generate legal scenarios, formal rules, and atomic facts. This is coupled with independent verification modules which include human reviewers' assessment of representational, logical, and legal correctness. Using this approach, we construct a high-quality dataset to be used for GDPR auto-formalization, and analyze both successful and problematic cases. Our results show that structured verification and targeted human oversight are essential for reliable legal formalization, especially in the presence of legal nuance and context-sensitive reasoning.

AI.tldr: The paper develops and evaluates a human-in-the-loop, multi-agent LLM workflow to automatically formalize GDPR provisions into machine-readable legal rules and facts, showing that structured verification and human oversight are crucial for reliability.
AI.motivation: Legal texts like the GDPR are complex, nuanced, and context-sensitive, making automated formalization into logical rules difficult but valuable for compliance tools, automated reasoning, and legal analytics. Existing approaches either require heavy manual effort or treat LLMs as black-box autonomous agents, which risks errors in sensitive legal settings. The authors aim to understand how LLMs can be systematically integrated with human review and verification to produce reliable formalizations of GDPR provisions.
AI.method: They design a role-specialized, multi-agent workflow where different LLM-based components generate (1) legal scenarios, (2) formal rules, and (3) atomic facts, with iterative feedback between agents. This workflow is embedded in a human-in-the-loop framework: independent verification modules and human reviewers assess representational fidelity to the GDPR text, logical consistency, and legal correctness. Using this setup, they automatically produce and then curate a dataset of formalized GDPR rules and related artifacts, and analyze both the successfully formalized cases and the failure modes.
AI.result: The workflow yields a high-quality dataset for GDPR auto-formalization and exposes where and why LLMs succeed or fail at legal formalization. The study finds that purely autonomous LLM formalization is unreliable, especially where legal nuance and context-sensitive interpretation are required. Structured verification steps, separation of roles among AI agents, and targeted human oversight significantly improve the reliability and quality of the resulting formal rules and scenarios.
AI.conclusion: Reliable automatic formalization of GDPR provisions is feasible only when LLMs are embedded in a carefully structured, human-in-the-loop verification process. Role-specialized LLM agents can effectively generate scenarios, rules, and facts, but independent verification and human legal review are indispensable to handle nuance, avoid subtle errors, and produce datasets suitable for downstream legal reasoning and compliance applications.

title: EuropeMedQA Study Protocol: A Multilingual, Multimodal Medical Examination Dataset for Language Model Evaluation
summary: While Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated high proficiency on English-centric medical examinations, their performance often declines when faced with non-English languages and multimodal diagnostic tasks. This study protocol describes the development of EuropeMedQA, the first comprehensive, multilingual, and multimodal medical examination dataset sourced from official regulatory exams in Italy, France, Spain, and Portugal. Following FAIR data principles and SPIRIT-AI guidelines, we describe a rigorous curation process and an automated translation pipeline for comparative analysis. We evaluate contemporary multimodal LLMs using a zero-shot, strictly constrained prompting strategy to assess cross-lingual transfer and visual reasoning. EuropeMedQA aims to provide a contamination-resistant benchmark that reflects the complexity of European clinical practices and fosters the development of more generalizable medical AI.

AI.tldr: EuropeMedQA is a new multilingual, multimodal medical exam benchmark built from official European regulatory exams to evaluate and improve LLMs’ cross-lingual and visual diagnostic abilities.
AI.motivation: Current LLMs perform strongly on English-only, text-based medical exams but show degraded performance on non-English and multimodal diagnostic tasks. Existing benchmarks are predominantly English-centric and may be contaminated by training data. There is a need for a contamination-resistant, realistic benchmark that captures the linguistic and clinical diversity of European medical practice and can systematically test multilingual and multimodal reasoning capabilities of medical AI systems.
AI.method: The authors design a dataset, EuropeMedQA, by collecting questions from official regulatory medical examinations in Italy, France, Spain, and Portugal. They apply a rigorous curation workflow guided by FAIR data principles and SPIRIT-AI guidelines to ensure transparency and reproducibility. An automated translation pipeline is developed to create aligned multilingual versions of questions and allow cross-lingual comparison. They then evaluate state-of-the-art multimodal LLMs in a zero-shot setting, using tightly constrained prompts, to measure language transfer and visual reasoning performance on this benchmark.
AI.result: As a study protocol/description paper, the abstract focuses on the dataset construction and evaluation framework rather than detailed numeric results. It establishes that EuropeMedQA has been created as the first comprehensive, multilingual, and multimodal exam dataset from multiple European countries, and that contemporary multimodal LLMs can be systematically evaluated on it under standardized, zero-shot conditions. It also claims that the benchmark is designed to be resistant to data contamination from web training corpora.
AI.conclusion: EuropeMedQA provides a new, realistic benchmark reflecting European clinical complexity, enabling robust assessment of multilingual and multimodal capabilities of LLMs in medicine. By adhering to FAIR and SPIRIT-AI principles and using official exam sources, it aims to support the development and comparison of more generalizable, clinically relevant medical AI systems, especially for non-English and multimodal diagnostic tasks.

title: El Agente Forjador: Task-Driven Agent Generation for Quantum Simulation
summary: AI for science promises to accelerate the discovery process. The advent of large language models (LLMs) and agentic workflows enables the expediting of a growing range of scientific tasks. However, most of the current generation of agentic systems depend on static, hand-curated toolsets that hinder adaptation to new domains and evolving libraries. We present El Agente Forjador, a multi-agent framework in which universal coding agents autonomously forge, validate, and reuse computational tools through a four-stage workflow of tool analysis, tool generation, task execution, and iterative solution evaluation. Evaluated across 24 tasks spanning quantum chemistry and quantum dynamics on five coding agent setups, we compare three operating modes: zero-shot generation of tools per task, reuse of a curriculum-built toolset, and direct problem-solving with the coding agents as the baseline. We find that our tool generation and reuse framework consistently improves accuracy over the baseline. We also show that reusing a toolset built by a stronger coding agent can reduce API cost and substantially raises the solution quality for weaker coding agents. Case studies further demonstrate that tools forged for different domains can be combined to solve hybrid tasks. Taken together, these results show that LLM-based agents can use their scientific knowledge and coding capabilities to autonomously build reusable scientific tools, pointing toward a paradigm in which agent capabilities are defined by the tasks they are designed to solve rather than by explicitly engineered implementations.

AI.tldr: A multi-agent LLM framework that autonomously creates, validates, and reuses code-based scientific tools, improving performance and efficiency on quantum science tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing AI-for-science agent systems rely on fixed, manually curated toolsets that are hard to adapt to new scientific domains or evolving software libraries. This rigidity limits scalability, cross-domain generalization, and the ability of agents to leverage their coding capabilities to build up their own reusable scientific tools.
AI.method: The authors propose El Agente Forjador, a multi-agent framework where universal coding agents autonomously forge tools via a four-stage workflow: (1) tool analysis, where tasks and existing tools are examined; (2) tool generation, where new code tools are written; (3) task execution, where these tools are applied to solve scientific problems; and (4) iterative solution evaluation, where performance feedback informs refinement. They evaluate three modes—zero-shot per-task tool generation, reuse of a curriculum-built toolset, and baseline direct problem solving without tools—across 24 quantum chemistry and quantum dynamics tasks using five different coding-agent setups.
AI.result: Across the 24 quantum-science tasks, the tool-generation and tool-reuse modes consistently outperform the baseline direct problem-solving mode in accuracy. Reusing a toolset that was originally forged by a stronger coding agent significantly improves solution quality and reduces API costs when used by weaker agents. Case studies show that tools forged in different domains can be composed to tackle hybrid problems that span multiple subfields.
AI.conclusion: LLM-based coding agents can autonomously construct, validate, and reuse scientific software tools that materially improve task performance and efficiency over direct problem solving. This supports a paradigm shift in which an agent’s capabilities are determined less by hand-engineered tools and more by the tasks it has been trained or tasked to solve, enabling adaptive, extensible AI-for-science workflows across domains.

title: Tracking the Temporal Dynamics of News Coverage of Catastrophic and Violent Events
summary: The modern news cycle has been fundamentally reshaped by the rapid exchange of information online. As a result, media framing shifts dynamically as new information, political responses, and social reactions emerge. Understanding how these narratives form, propagate, and evolve is essential for interpreting public discourse during moments of crisis. In this study, we examine the temporal and semantic dynamics of reporting for violent and catastrophic events using a large-scale corpus of 126,602 news articles collected from online publishers. We quantify narrative change through publication volume, semantic drift, semantic dispersion, and term relevance. Our results show that sudden events of impact exhibit structured and predictable news-cycle patterns characterized by rapid surges in coverage, early semantic drift, and gradual declines toward the baseline. In addition, our results indicate the terms that are driving the temporal patterns.

AI.tldr: They analyze how news narratives about extreme events change over time, using large-scale text metrics.
AI.motivation: Online news cycles evolve quickly as new information and reactions appear, but we lack quantitative understanding of how media narratives about crises form and change over time.
AI.method: They compile a corpus of 126,602 online news articles about violent and catastrophic events and measure narrative change using publication volume, semantic drift, semantic dispersion, and term relevance over time.
AI.result: They find that sudden high-impact events follow consistent patterns: a sharp spike in coverage, early and notable shifts in how the events are described, increasing diversity of language, and then a gradual return to baseline, with specific key terms driving these temporal patterns.
AI.conclusion: News coverage of sudden crises is not random but follows predictable temporal and semantic dynamics that can be quantified, and particular terms play a central role in structuring how narratives evolve over a news cycle.

title: CoDaS: AI Co-Data-Scientist for Biomarker Discovery via Wearable Sensors
summary: Scientific discovery in digital health requires converting continuous physiological signals from wearable devices into clinically actionable biomarkers. We introduce CoDaS (AI Co-Data-Scientist), a multi-agent system that structures biomarker discovery as an iterative process combining hypothesis generation, statistical analysis, adversarial validation, and literature-grounded reasoning with human oversight using large-scale wearable datasets. Across three cohorts totaling 9,279 participant-observations, CoDaS identified 41 candidate digital biomarkers for mental health and 25 for metabolic outcomes, each subjected to an internal validation battery spanning replication, stability, robustness, and discriminative power. Across two independent depression cohorts, CoDaS surfaced circadian instability-related features in both datasets, reflected in sleep duration variability (DWB, ρ= 0.252, p < 0.001) and sleep onset variability (GLOBEM, ρ= 0.126, p < 0.001). In a metabolic cohort, CoDaS derived a cardiovascular fitness index (steps/resting heart rate; ρ= -0.374, p < 0.001), and recovered established clinical associations, including the hepatic function ratio (AST/ALT; ρ= -0.375, p < 0.001), a known correlate of insulin resistance. Incorporating CoDaS-derived features alongside demographic variables led to modest but consistent improvements in predictive performance, with cross-validated ΔR^2 increases of 0.040 for depression and 0.021 for insulin resistance. These findings suggest that CoDaS enables systematic and traceable hypothesis generation and prioritization for biomarker discovery from large-scale wearable data.

AI.tldr: The paper presents CoDaS, a multi-agent AI system that systematically discovers and validates digital biomarkers from large-scale wearable data, showing modest but consistent predictive gains for mental and metabolic health outcomes.
AI.motivation: There is a gap between raw continuous physiological signals collected by wearables and clinically useful, validated digital biomarkers. Existing approaches to biomarker discovery are often ad hoc, opaque, and not easily reproducible or systematically validated across large datasets. The authors aim to build an AI-driven framework that structures this discovery process, adds rigorous internal validation, and keeps the process traceable and amenable to human oversight.
AI.method: The authors design CoDaS (AI Co-Data-Scientist), a multi-agent system that frames biomarker discovery as an iterative pipeline. The pipeline integrates several capabilities: hypothesis generation from wearable signals, statistical association testing with clinical outcomes, adversarial validation to guard against spurious findings or dataset artifacts, and literature-grounded reasoning to contextualize and prioritize candidates. CoDaS operates on large-scale wearable datasets from three cohorts (9,279 participant-observations total) and performs an internal validation battery for each candidate biomarker, assessing replication across cohorts, temporal stability, robustness to perturbations, and discriminative performance. Human experts oversee and guide the process.
AI.result: Using three cohorts, CoDaS discovered 41 candidate digital biomarkers related to mental health and 25 related to metabolic outcomes. In two independent depression cohorts, it consistently identified circadian instability features, specifically variability in sleep duration (DWB, Spearman ρ = 0.252, p < 0.001) and sleep onset variability (GLOBEM, ρ = 0.126, p < 0.001). In the metabolic cohort, CoDaS proposed a cardiovascular fitness index defined as steps/resting heart rate (ρ = -0.374, p < 0.001) and also recovered known clinical markers such as the hepatic function ratio AST/ALT (ρ = -0.375, p < 0.001), which is linked to insulin resistance. Including CoDaS-derived features with demographic covariates yielded cross-validated predictive performance improvements: ΔR^2 of 0.040 for depression and 0.021 for insulin resistance.
AI.conclusion: CoDaS operationalizes biomarker discovery from wearable data as a systematic, multi-agent AI process with built-in validation and human oversight. It can generate, validate, and prioritize digital biomarker candidates that not only rediscover known associations but also yield modest, consistent gains in prediction for mental and metabolic health outcomes. The framework enhances traceability and reproducibility in digital biomarker discovery and suggests a scalable approach for turning raw wearable signals into clinically relevant insights.

title: LLM Predictive Scoring and Validation: Inferring Experience Ratings from Unstructured Text
summary: We tasked GPT-4.1 to read what baseball fans wrote about their game-day experience and predict the overall experience rating each fan gave on a 0-10 survey scale. The model received only the text of a single open-ended response. These AI predictions were compared with the actual experience ratings captured by the survey instrument across approximately 10,000 fan responses from five Major League Baseball teams. In total two-thirds of predicted ratings fell within one point of self-reported fan ratings (67% within +/-1, 36% exact match), and the predicted measurement was near-deterministic across three independent scoring runs (87% exact agreement, 99.9% within +/-1). Predicted ratings aligned most strongly with the overall experience rating (r = 0.82) rather than with any specific aspect of the game-day experience such as parking, concessions, staff, etc. However, predictions were systematically lower than self-reported ratings by approximately one point, and this gap was not driven by any single aspect. Rather, our analysis shows that self-reported ratings capture the fan's verdict, an overall evaluative judgment that integrates the entire experience. While predicted ratings quantify the impact of salient moments characterized as memorable, emotionally intense, unusual, or actionable. Each measure contains information the other misses. These baseline results establish that a simple, unoptimized prompt can directionally predict how fans rate their experience from the text a fan wrote and that a gap between the two numbers can be interpreted as a construct difference worth preserving rather than an error to eliminate.

AI.tldr: The paper evaluates how well GPT-4.1 can infer baseball fans’ 0–10 overall experience ratings from a single open-ended comment and compares these AI-predicted ratings to survey self-reports.
AI.motivation: Traditional satisfaction surveys use numeric scales plus many attribute questions, but open-text comments often capture richer, more nuanced feedback. Organizations want to know whether large language models can reliably infer overall satisfaction scores from this text alone, potentially simplifying surveys and extracting more value from verbatims.
AI.method: Using roughly 10,000 open-ended responses from fans of five Major League Baseball teams, the authors prompted GPT-4.1 (with a simple, non-optimized prompt) to assign an overall 0–10 rating to each fan’s game-day experience based only on the text. They ran three independent scoring passes to assess stability, then compared AI scores to self-reported overall ratings and to ratings of specific aspects (parking, concessions, staff, etc.) using accuracy metrics and correlations.
AI.result: Two-thirds of GPT-4.1’s predicted ratings were within one point of the fans’ own ratings (67% within ±1; 36% exact match). Across three runs, the model’s predictions were highly consistent (87% exact agreement; 99.9% within ±1 across runs). Predicted ratings correlated strongly with the self-reported overall rating (r = 0.82) and less with specific aspect ratings. However, AI predictions were systematically about one point lower than self-reported ratings, a gap not explained by any single aspect of the experience.
AI.conclusion: GPT-4.1 can reliably and stably approximate fans’ overall experience ratings from a single open-ended comment using a simple prompt, but it tends to score experiences about one point lower than self-reports. The authors argue this difference reflects a construct distinction: self-reported ratings represent a holistic evaluative verdict on the entire experience, while AI-predicted ratings are more driven by salient, memorable, emotionally intense, or actionable moments. Thus, the gap between AI and human ratings may be informative and worth preserving, rather than a prediction error to be eliminated.

title: A Parallel Approach to Counting Exact Covers Based on Decomposability Property
summary: The exact cover problem is a classical NP-hard problem with broad applications in the area of AI. Algorithm DXZ is a method to count exact covers representing by zero-suppressed binary decision diagrams (ZBDDs). In this paper, we propose a zero-suppressed variant of decision decomposable negation normal form (in short, decision-ZDNNF), which is strictly more succinct than ZBDDs. We then design a novel parallel algorithm, namely DXD, which constructs a decision-ZDNNF representing the set of all exact covers. Furthermore, we improve DXD by dynamically updating connected components. The experimental results demonstrate that the improved DXD algorithm outperforms all of state-of-the-art methods.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a more succinct representation (decision-ZDNNF) for counting exact covers and a new parallel algorithm (DXD) that outperforms existing methods.
AI.motivation: Counting exact covers is NP-hard but important in AI applications. Existing methods using ZBDDs (like algorithm DXZ) can be limited by representation size and efficiency, especially on large instances. There is a need for a more compact representation and faster, scalable counting algorithms.
AI.method: The authors define a zero-suppressed variant of decision decomposable negation normal form, called decision-ZDNNF, and prove it is strictly more succinct than ZBDDs. They then design a parallel construction algorithm, DXD, that builds a decision-ZDNNF capturing all exact covers of a given instance. They further optimize DXD by dynamically updating connected components of the problem during construction to reduce redundant work and improve parallel performance.
AI.result: The improved DXD algorithm, which leverages decision-ZDNNF and dynamic connected-component updates, is experimentally evaluated against existing state-of-the-art methods for counting exact covers. The experiments show that DXD consistently runs faster and scales better, outperforming all compared algorithms.
AI.conclusion: Decision-ZDNNF is a more compact knowledge compilation target than ZBDDs for representing exact covers, and the proposed parallel DXD algorithm effectively exploits this representation. The dynamic connected-component optimization further boosts efficiency, leading to state-of-the-art performance on benchmark instances of the exact cover counting problem.

title: Purging the Gray Zone: Latent-Geometric Denoising for Precise Knowledge Boundary Awareness
summary: Large language models (LLMs) often exhibit hallucinations due to their inability to accurately perceive their own knowledge boundaries. Existing abstention fine-tuning methods typically partition datasets directly based on response accuracy, causing models to suffer from severe label noise near the decision boundaries and consequently exhibit high rates of abstentions or hallucinations. This paper adopts a latent space representation perspective, revealing a "gray zone" near the decision hyperplane where internal belief ambiguity constitutes the core performance bottleneck. Based on this insight, we propose the **GeoDe** (**Geo**metric **De**noising) framework for abstention fine-tuning. This method constructs a truth hyperplane using linear probes and performs "geometric denoising" by employing geometric distance as a confidence signal for abstention decisions. This approach filters out ambiguous boundary samples while retaining high-fidelity signals for fine-tuning. Experiments across multiple models (Llama3, Qwen3) and benchmark datasets (TriviaQA, NQ, SciQ, SimpleQA) demonstrate that GeoDe significantly enhances model truthfulness and demonstrates strong generalization in out-of-distribution (OOD) scenarios. Code is available at https://github.com/Notbesidemoon/GeoDe.

AI.tldr: GeoDe is a geometric denoising framework for abstention fine-tuning that uses latent-space distances to filter ambiguous training samples, improving LLM truthfulness and OOD generalization.
AI.motivation: LLMs hallucinate because they cannot reliably identify when they do not know an answer. Existing abstention fine-tuning methods label data purely by correctness of final outputs, which creates heavy label noise near decision boundaries—samples whose correctness is ambiguous or unstable. This leads to too many unnecessary abstentions or persistent hallucinations, limiting reliability. The authors aim to better understand and exploit the internal belief structure of LLMs to learn when to abstain more accurately.
AI.method: They view the model in latent space and identify a gray zone around a decision hyperplane where the model’s internal beliefs are ambiguous. Using linear probes, they estimate a truth hyperplane that separates truthful from hallucinated responses in this representation space. They then define geometric distance to this hyperplane as a confidence metric. During abstention fine-tuning, they perform geometric denoising: boundary-near (low-distance) samples, which are likely label-noisy or belief-ambiguous, are filtered out, while far-from-boundary (high-distance) samples are kept as high-fidelity training signals for learning abstention behavior. This framework, called GeoDe, is applied on top of existing LLMs without changing their core architectures.
AI.result: Across multiple LLMs (Llama3, Qwen3) and QA benchmarks (TriviaQA, NQ, SciQ, SimpleQA), GeoDe reduces hallucination rates and improves abstention calibration versus standard abstention fine-tuning baselines. It yields higher truthfulness metrics and better trade-offs between answering and abstaining, and the learned abstention behavior generalizes well to out-of-distribution datasets beyond those used for training.
AI.conclusion: Ambiguity in LLM internal belief states concentrates in a latent gray zone near a decision hyperplane, and naive accuracy-based dataset partitioning fails to handle this, injecting label noise into abstention training. By explicitly modeling this geometry and using distance to a learned truth hyperplane as a confidence signal, GeoDe can filter noisy boundary examples and train more reliable abstention behavior. This geometric denoising approach substantially improves truthfulness and OOD robustness, suggesting latent-space geometry is a powerful lever for controlling hallucinations in LLMs.

title: Learning to Draw ASCII Improves Spatial Reasoning in Language Models
summary: When faced with complex spatial problems, humans naturally sketch layouts to organize their thinking, and the act of drawing further sharpens their understanding. In this work, we ask whether a similar principle holds for Large Language Models (LLMs): can learning to construct explicit visual layouts from spatial descriptions instill genuine spatial understanding? We introduce Text2Space, a dataset that pairs natural language descriptions with ground-truth ASCII grid layouts and spatial QA pairs, enabling us to separate failures in constructing spatial representations from failures in reasoning over them. We adopt ASCII because it is human-readable, operates entirely within the token space of language models, and encodes spatial relations in a structurally verifiable form. Our evaluation reveals a pronounced "Read-Write Asymmetry": LLMs interpret ASCII representations effectively but struggle to produce them from text, and these construction errors propagate to incorrect answers downstream. To address this limitation, we train models on layout construction (Text$\rightarrow$ASCII) and find that it significantly improves spatial reasoning from text alone, even without producing any ASCII at inference time. Combining construction with comprehension training further amplifies these gains. Crucially, these improvements transfer to three external spatial reasoning benchmarks, demonstrating that, much as sketching sharpens human spatial thinking, learning to construct explicit layouts instills spatial understanding that generalizes beyond the training format.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that training LLMs to explicitly construct ASCII spatial layouts from text improves their spatial reasoning and generalizes to external benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Humans often sketch when solving spatial problems, and this externalization sharpens their spatial reasoning. Current LLMs can read spatial layouts (like ASCII grids) but it is unclear whether teaching them to construct such layouts from language improves their underlying spatial understanding rather than just format handling.
AI.method: The authors introduce Text2Space, a dataset of natural language spatial descriptions paired with ground-truth ASCII grid layouts and spatial QA pairs. They evaluate LLMs on both reading (ASCII→answers) and writing (Text→ASCII) to expose a "Read-Write Asymmetry." They then train models specifically on layout construction (Text→ASCII) and on combined construction plus comprehension tasks, and assess performance both on Text2Space QA and on three external spatial reasoning benchmarks.
AI.result: LLMs are good at interpreting given ASCII layouts but poor at generating correct layouts from text, and these generation errors lead to wrong answers in downstream spatial QA. Training on layout construction substantially boosts spatial reasoning from text alone, even when no ASCII is produced at inference. Joint training on both construction and comprehension yields even larger gains. These benefits transfer to multiple external spatial reasoning benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Explicitly training LLMs to construct structural spatial representations (ASCII layouts) from language instills more robust spatial understanding. Like human sketching, this construction process improves reasoning and generalizes beyond the specific representation, indicating that building internalized layout skills is key to better spatial capabilities in LLMs.

title: Faithfulness Serum: Mitigating the Faithfulness Gap in Textual Explanations of LLM Decisions via Attribution Guidance
comment: 24 pages, multiple figures (e.g., at least 6 main figures), includes experiments across several benchmarks (MMLU, CommonsenseQA, SciQ, ARC, OpenBookQA); code available on GitHub
summary: Large language models (LLMs) achieve strong performance and have revolutionized NLP, but their lack of explainability keeps them treated as black boxes, limiting their use in domains that demand transparency and trust. A promising direction to address this issue is post-hoc text-based explanations, which aim to explain model decisions in natural language. Prior work has focused on generating convincing rationales that appear to be subjectively faithful, but it remains unclear whether these explanations are epistemically faithful, whether they reflect the internal evidence the model actually relied on for its decision. In this paper, we first assess the epistemic faithfulness of LLM-generated explanations via counterfactuals and show that they are often unfaithful. We then introduce a training-free method that enhances faithfulness by guiding explanation generation through attention-level interventions, informed by token-level heatmaps extracted via a faithful attribution method. This method significantly improves epistemic faithfulness across multiple models, benchmarks, and prompts.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how faithful large language model (LLM) natural-language explanations are to the models’ actual decision-making, finds they are often unfaithful, and proposes a training-free, attention-guided method to generate more epistemically faithful explanations.
AI.motivation: LLMs are powerful but are treated as black boxes because their decisions are hard to explain. Many current text-based explanations optimize for sounding plausible to humans (subjective faithfulness) rather than truly reflecting the internal evidence the model used (epistemic faithfulness). This gap threatens trust and safe deployment in domains that require transparent reasoning, so the authors want to rigorously test and improve the epistemic faithfulness of LLM explanations.
AI.method: 1) Empirically evaluate epistemic faithfulness of LLM-generated explanations using counterfactual analysis: test whether changing or removing explanation-mentioned evidence actually changes the model’s prediction. 2) When they find unfaithfulness, they propose a training-free explanation-generation procedure: (a) compute faithful token-level attributions (heatmaps) for the model’s decision using an attribution method; (b) intervene at the attention level during explanation generation to steer the model to focus on these high-attribution tokens; (c) generate explanations conditioned on these guided internal states. They test this across multiple LLMs, benchmarks, and prompt styles.
AI.result: The counterfactual evaluation shows that standard LLM explanations frequently do not track the true evidence behind the model’s decisions. Using their attention-guided, attribution-informed procedure yields explanations whose content is much more sensitive to the actual evidence the model used, i.e., they are more epistemically faithful. This improvement is consistent across several different LLMs, datasets, and prompt configurations.
AI.conclusion: Natural-language explanations from LLMs are often not epistemically faithful by default, even if they sound convincing. However, explanations can be made substantially more faithful without retraining the model, by using faithful attribution methods to identify important tokens and then guiding the model’s attention during explanation generation. This offers a practical path toward more trustworthy, transparent use of LLMs in sensitive applications.

title: Targeted Exploration via Unified Entropy Control for Reinforcement Learning
comment: Accepted for publication in Findings of the 64th Annual Meeting of the Association for Computational Linguistics (ACL 2026)
summary: Recent advances in reinforcement learning (RL) have improved the reasoning capabilities of large language models (LLMs) and vision-language models (VLMs). However, the widely used Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) consistently suffers from entropy collapse, causing the policy to converge prematurely and lose diversity. Existing exploration methods introduce additional bias or variance during exploration, making it difficult to maintain optimization stability. We propose Unified Entropy Control for Reinforcement Learning (UEC-RL), a framework that provides targeted mechanisms for exploration and stabilization. UEC-RL activates more exploration on difficult prompts to search for potential and valuable reasoning trajectories. In parallel, a stabilizer prevents entropy from growing uncontrollably, thereby keeping training stable as the model consolidates reliable behaviors. Together, these components expand the search space when needed while maintaining robust optimization throughout training. Experiments on both LLM and VLM reasoning tasks show consistent gains over RL baselines on both Pass@1 and Pass@$k$. On Geometry3K, UEC-RL achieves a 37.9\% relative improvement over GRPO, indicating that it sustains effective exploration without compromising convergence and underscoring UEC-RL as a key for scaling RL-based reasoning in large models. Our code is available at https://github.com/597358816/UEC-RL.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes UEC-RL, a unified entropy control framework for reinforcement learning that improves exploration and stability when training LLMs and VLMs for reasoning tasks, outperforming GRPO and other RL baselines.
AI.motivation: While RL has improved reasoning in LLMs and VLMs, the commonly used GRPO algorithm suffers from entropy collapse, leading to premature convergence and lack of diversity in generated reasoning trajectories. Existing exploration techniques introduce extra bias or variance, destabilizing optimization. There is a need for a method that can enhance exploration where necessary while keeping learning stable and avoiding entropy collapse.
AI.method: The authors introduce UEC-RL (Unified Entropy Control for Reinforcement Learning), which decouples and coordinates exploration and stabilization. It adaptively increases exploration on difficult prompts to search broader, potentially better reasoning trajectories, while a separate stabilizer explicitly prevents entropy from growing without bound. This dual mechanism dynamically expands the search space when beneficial and constrains entropy growth to preserve optimization stability throughout training. The approach is applied to both large language and vision-language models on reasoning tasks, using GRPO-like policy optimization as the baseline.
AI.result: Across multiple LLM and VLM reasoning benchmarks, UEC-RL yields consistent improvements over standard RL baselines in Pass@1 and Pass@k metrics. On the Geometry3K benchmark in particular, the method achieves a 37.9% relative improvement over GRPO, demonstrating that it maintains effective exploration without sacrificing convergence quality.
AI.conclusion: UEC-RL effectively addresses entropy collapse in GRPO by providing unified, adaptive entropy control that balances exploration and stability. It enables sustained, high-quality exploration during RL training of large models while preserving robust convergence, making it a promising foundation for scaling RL-based reasoning in large language and vision-language models.

title: Shuffle the Context: RoPE-Perturbed Self-Distillation for Long-Context Adaptation
summary:
Large language models (LLMs) increasingly operate in settings that require reliable long-context understanding, such as retrieval-augmented generation and multi-document reasoning. A common strategy is to fine-tune pretrained short-context models at the target sequence length. However, we find that standard long-context adaptation can remain brittle: model accuracy depends strongly on the absolute placement of relevant evidence, exhibiting high positional variance even when controlling for task format and difficulty.
  We propose RoPE-Perturbed Self-Distillation, a training regularizer that improves positional robustness. The core idea is to form alternative "views" of the same training sequence by perturbing its RoPE indices -- effectively moving parts of the context to different positions -- and to train the model to produce consistent predictions across views via self-distillation. This encourages reliance on semantic signals instead of brittle position dependencies. Experiments on long-context adaptation of Llama-3-8B and Qwen-3-4B demonstrate consistent gains on long-context benchmarks, including up to 12.04% improvement on RULER-64K for Llama-3-8B and 2.71% on RULER-256K for Qwen-3-4B after SFT, alongside improved length extrapolation beyond the training context window.

AI.tldr: They improve long-context robustness of LLMs by perturbing RoPE positions and using self-distillation to make predictions invariant to token placement, yielding sizable gains on long-context benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Standard long-context fine-tuning of short-context LLMs is brittle: model accuracy varies a lot depending on where in the long input the relevant evidence appears, even when tasks are otherwise equivalent. This positional variance is harmful for applications like retrieval-augmented generation and multi-document reasoning that depend on reliable use of information anywhere in a long context. The authors want a way to make models less sensitive to absolute position and more reliant on semantics.
AI.method: They introduce RoPE-Perturbed Self-Distillation. During training, they create alternative views of the same sequence by perturbing RoPE (rotary positional embedding) indices, effectively relocating parts of the context to different positions without changing the underlying text. The model is trained so that its predictions remain consistent across the original and perturbed views using self-distillation, encouraging invariance to position. This is applied on top of standard long-context supervised fine-tuning of existing models like Llama-3-8B and Qwen-3-4B.
