AI.result: On long-context benchmarks, including RULER at 64K and 256K context lengths, the method yields consistent accuracy improvements over standard long-context adaptation: up to 12.04 percentage points on RULER-64K for Llama-3-8B and 2.71 percentage points on RULER-256K for Qwen-3-4B after supervised fine-tuning. The models also show better length extrapolation beyond their training context windows.
AI.conclusion: Perturbing RoPE indices and using self-distillation as a regularizer significantly improves positional robustness of long-context-adapted LLMs. By enforcing prediction consistency across different positional arrangements of the same content, models rely more on semantic information and less on brittle absolute position cues, leading to better and more reliable performance at long sequence lengths.

title: AgentGA: Evolving Code Solutions in Agent-Seed Space
summary: We present AgentGA, a framework that evolves autonomous code-generation runs by optimizing the agent seed: the task prompt plus optional parent archives that initialize a fresh workspace. The outer loop searches over these reusable starting conditions rather than editing code directly. Each generation launches a fresh autonomous run from a reset workspace, while selected parent archives provide inherited artifacts that descendants can inspect and reuse. AgentGA couples a population-level genetic algorithm with long-horizon agents; selection uses deterministic 1:1 elite tournaments and operator allocation is adapted online with a modified Hedge controller. We instantiate the approach for tabular AutoML on the 16-competition Weco-Kaggle Lite benchmark. On the 10 benchmark runs reported here, AgentGA averages 74.52% Exceeds % of Human versus 54.15% for AIDE. Across 1135 parent-child comparisons, descendants given parent archives outperform runs started from scratch, indicating that inherited artifacts improve later autonomous runs. These findings support agent-seed optimization as a practical design point for autonomous code-search systems.

AI.tldr: AgentGA is a framework that uses genetic algorithms to evolve the starting conditions (prompts + saved artifacts) of autonomous code-generation agents, improving performance on AutoML tasks.
AI.motivation: Autonomous code-generation and AutoML agents often start each run from scratch, ignoring useful artifacts produced in previous runs. Existing systems focus on directly editing code or single-run optimization, missing the opportunity to optimize reusable starting conditions (prompts, initial files, prior solutions) that could guide future autonomous runs and systematically reuse past work.
AI.method: AgentGA introduces agent-seed optimization: instead of mutating code directly, a genetic algorithm operates over agent seeds, each consisting of a task prompt plus optional parent archives (collections of artifacts from previous runs). In each generation, a population of seeds is evaluated by launching fresh autonomous agent runs in reset workspaces initialized using the seed’s prompt and inherited archives. A population-level GA with deterministic 1:1 elite tournaments handles selection, and operator allocation (e.g., different mutation/crossover strategies over seeds/archives) is dynamically tuned using a modified Hedge controller. The framework is instantiated for tabular AutoML on the Weco-Kaggle Lite benchmark of 16 competitions, where seeds correspond to different initial instructions and inherited code/data artifacts given to an autonomous coding agent.
AI.result: On 10 benchmark runs of the 16-competition Weco-Kaggle Lite suite, AgentGA achieves an average of 74.52% “Exceeds % of Human” compared to 54.15% for the baseline AIDE system, indicating substantially better performance relative to human reference scores. In 1135 parent–child comparisons, runs whose seeds include parent archives systematically outperform runs initiated from scratch, demonstrating that inheriting artifacts from prior runs yields measurable gains in downstream autonomous performance.
AI.conclusion: Optimizing agent seeds—prompts plus selected inherited artifacts—via a genetic algorithm is an effective and practical strategy for improving autonomous code-generation systems. Rather than editing code directly, evolving reusable starting conditions allows long-horizon agents to better exploit prior work, leading to higher benchmark performance and clear benefits from artifact inheritance. This supports agent-seed optimization as a promising design point for future autonomous code-search and AutoML frameworks.

title: When PCOS Meets Eating Disorders: An Explainable AI Approach to Detecting the Hidden Triple Burden
summary: Women with polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) face substantially elevated risks of body image distress, disordered eating, and metabolic challenges, yet existing natural language processing approaches for detecting these conditions lack transparency and cannot identify co-occurring presentations. We developed small, open-source language models to automatically detect this triple burden in social media posts with grounded explainability. We collected 1,000 PCOS-related posts from six subreddits, with two trained annotators labeling posts using guidelines operationalizing Lee et al. (2017) clinical framework. Three models (Gemma-2-2B, Qwen3-1.7B, DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-1.5B) were fine-tuned using Low-Rank Adaptation to generate structured explanations with textual evidence. The best model achieved 75.3 percent exact match accuracy on 150 held-out posts, with robust comorbidity detection and strong explainability. Performance declined with diagnostic complexity, indicating their best use is for screening rather than autonomous diagnosis.

AI.tldr: The paper develops small, explainable language models to detect co-occurring body image distress, disordered eating, and metabolic challenges in PCOS-related social media posts, showing good screening performance but limited diagnostic complexity handling.
AI.motivation: Women with PCOS have high, intertwined risks of body image distress, disordered eating, and metabolic issues, but current NLP tools for identifying these problems in text are opaque and typically handle conditions in isolation. There is a need for transparent, explainable models that can detect these comorbid presentations together, especially in social media where people discuss symptoms and distress informally.
AI.method: The authors collected 1,000 PCOS-related posts from six subreddits and had two trained annotators label them using guidelines based on Lee et al. (2017) clinical framework. They fine-tuned three small open-source language models (Gemma-2-2B, Qwen3-1.7B, DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-1.5B) with Low-Rank Adaptation to produce structured, grounded explanations that include textual evidence for predicted labels capturing body image distress, disordered eating, and metabolic challenges and their co-occurrence.
AI.result: Among the three models, the best fine-tuned model reached 75.3% exact match accuracy on a 150-post held-out test set. It was particularly effective at identifying comorbid conditions and produced strong, grounded explanations for its predictions, although performance decreased as diagnostic complexity increased.
AI.conclusion: Small, open-source LMs fine-tuned with LoRA can serve as explainable screening tools for identifying co-occurring body image, eating, and metabolic issues in PCOS-related social media posts. Their declining performance on more complex diagnostic patterns suggests they should augment, not replace, clinical judgment, and are better suited for screening and research surveillance than for autonomous diagnosis.

title: Rethinking Patient Education as Multi-turn Multi-modal Interaction
comment: Equal contribution for the first two authors
summary: Most medical multimodal benchmarks focus on static tasks such as image question answering, report generation, and plain-language rewriting. Patient education is more demanding: systems must identify relevant evidence across images, show patients where to look, explain findings in accessible language, and handle confusion or distress. Yet most patient education work remains text-only, even though combined image-and-text explanations may better support understanding. We introduce MedImageEdu, a benchmark for multi-turn, evidence-grounded radiology patient education. Each case provides a radiology report with report text and case images. A DoctorAgent interacts with a PatientAgent, conditioned on a hidden profile that captures factors such as education level, health literacy, and personality. When a patient question would benefit from visual support, the DoctorAgent can issue drawing instructions grounded in the report, case images, and the current question to a benchmark-provided drawing tool. The tool returns image(s), after which the DoctorAgent produces a final multimodal response consisting of the image(s) and a grounded plain-language explanation. MedImageEdu contains 150 cases from three sources and evaluates both the consultation process and the final multimodal response along five dimensions: Consultation, Safety and Scope, Language Quality, Drawing Quality, and Image-Text Response Quality. Across representative open- and closed-source vision-language model agents, we find three consistent gaps: fluent language often outpaces faithful visual grounding, safety is the weakest dimension across disease categories, and emotionally tense interactions are harder than low education or low health literacy. MedImageEdu provides a controlled testbed for assessing whether multimodal agents can teach from evidence rather than merely answer from text.

AI.tldr: MedImageEdu is a benchmark for evaluating multimodal AI agents that educate radiology patients through multi-turn, image-grounded conversations.
AI.motivation: Existing medical multimodal benchmarks mostly cover static tasks like image QA or report generation and do not simulate realistic patient education, which requires multi-turn dialogue, emotional sensitivity, and visual explanations. Moreover, most patient education research is text-only, despite the importance of image-and-text explanations for radiology findings.
AI.method: The authors build MedImageEdu, a benchmark with 150 radiology cases that include reports and images. They simulate consultations between a DoctorAgent and a PatientAgent, where the patient is governed by a hidden profile (education, health literacy, personality). The DoctorAgent can invoke a drawing tool to generate visual aids grounded in the report and images, and must produce final multimodal explanations. They define five evaluation dimensions: Consultation, Safety and Scope, Language Quality, Drawing Quality, and Image-Text Response Quality, and test various open- and closed-source vision-language agents.
AI.result: Experiments with different vision-language model agents show three robust shortcomings: (1) models can produce fluent language but often fail to ground explanations faithfully in the visual evidence; (2) safety behaviors are weakest across disease types; and (3) emotionally tense consultations are more challenging for models than those involving low education or low health literacy alone.
AI.conclusion: MedImageEdu offers a controlled environment to systematically evaluate whether multimodal agents can perform evidence-based patient education in radiology, moving beyond text-only answers toward grounded, safe, and empathetic multimodal teaching. The observed performance gaps highlight the need for improved visual grounding, safety mechanisms, and handling of emotional complexity in medical AI assistants.

title: APEX-MEM: Agentic Semi-Structured Memory with Temporal Reasoning for Long-Term Conversational AI
summary: Large language models still struggle with reliable long-term conversational memory: simply enlarging context windows or applying naive retrieval often introduces noise and destabilizes responses. We present APEX-MEM, a conversational memory system that combines three key innovations: (1) a property graph which uses domain-agnostic ontology to structure conversations as temporally grounded events in an entity-centric framework, (2) append-only storage that preserves the full temporal evolution of information, and (3) a multi-tool retrieval agent that understands and resolves conflicting or evolving information at query time, producing a compact and contextually relevant memory summary. This retrieval-time resolution preserves the full interaction history while suppressing irrelevant details. APEX-MEM achieves 88.88% accuracy on LOCOMO's Question Answering task and 86.2% on LongMemEval, outperforming state-of-the-art session-aware approaches and demonstrating that structured property graphs enable more temporally coherent long-term conversational reasoning.

AI.tldr: APEX-MEM is a structured conversational memory system for LLMs that uses a property graph, append-only storage, and a multi-tool retrieval agent to maintain temporally coherent, compact, and accurate long-term dialogue context, achieving state-of-the-art accuracy on long-context QA benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing large language models struggle with reliable long-term conversational memory. Simply enlarging context windows or using naive retrieval introduces noise, destabilizes responses, and fails to respect temporal evolution or conflicts in information. The paper aims to create a memory system that can maintain temporally coherent, entity-centric, and conflict-aware long-term conversational context for better reasoning and question answering over extended interactions.
AI.method: The paper proposes APEX-MEM, a conversational memory system with three main components: (1) a property graph based on a domain-agnostic ontology that structures conversations as temporally grounded events centered around entities; (2) an append-only storage mechanism that records the full temporal evolution of information without overwriting past states; and (3) a multi-tool retrieval agent that, at query time, navigates the property graph, interprets and resolves conflicting or evolving information, and synthesizes a compact, contextually relevant memory summary for the language model. The key idea is to perform conflict resolution and relevance filtering at retrieval time, rather than pruning the stored history.
AI.result: APEX-MEM achieves 88.88% accuracy on LOCOMO's Question Answering task and 86.2% accuracy on LongMemEval. These results outperform state-of-the-art session-aware baselines on long-term conversational reasoning and QA tasks, indicating that the proposed structured property graph and retrieval-time resolution approach provide more reliable and temporally coherent memory for LLMs.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that representing conversational memory as a structured, temporally grounded property graph, coupled with append-only storage and a retrieval-time multi-tool agent for resolving conflicts and summarizing context, significantly improves long-term conversational reasoning in LLMs. APEX-MEM's strong performance on long-context benchmarks demonstrates that such structured memory architectures are a promising direction for building more stable, temporally coherent, and accurate long-term conversational AI systems.

title: Acceptance Dynamics Across Cognitive Domains in Speculative Decoding
summary: Speculative decoding accelerates large language model (LLM) inference. It uses a small draft model to propose a tree of future tokens. A larger target model then verifies these tokens in a single batched forward pass. Despite the growing body of work on speculative methods, the degree to which the cognitive characteristics of a task affect acceptance probability remains largely unexplored. We present an empirical study of tree-based speculative decoding acceptance dynamics. Our study spans four well-established NLP benchmark domains: code generation, mathematical reasoning, logical reasoning, and open-ended chat. For this, we use TinyLlama-1.1B as the draft model against Llama-2-7B-Chat-GPTQ as the target. Over 99,768 speculative nodes collected from 200 prompts, we derive per-domain acceptance rates, expected accepted lengths, depth-acceptance profiles, and entropy-acceptance correlations. We find that task type is a stronger predictor of acceptance than tree depth. Furthermore, only the chat domain consistently yields an expected accepted length exceeding 1.0 token per step. We also show that the entropy-acceptance correlation is consistently negative but weak across all domains (rho in [-0.20, -0.15]). Counterintuitively, chat produces the highest entropy yet the highest acceptance rate. We attribute this divergence to the lexical predictability of RLHF-aligned register. These findings have direct implications for domain-aware speculation budgets and draft-model selection strategies. Index Terms--speculative decoding, large language model inference, tree attention, draft model, acceptance probability, LLM efficiency

AI.tldr: The paper empirically studies how different NLP task types affect the token acceptance behavior in tree-based speculative decoding for LLMs, showing that task domain is more predictive of acceptance than tree depth and that chat tasks yield the most efficient speculation despite higher entropy.
AI.motivation: While speculative decoding is known to speed up LLM inference, it is unclear how cognitive/task characteristics influence the probability that draft tokens are accepted by the target model. Understanding this can guide better configuration of speculative decoders (e.g., budgets, draft model choice) for different application domains.
AI.method: Use TinyLlama-1.1B as a draft model and Llama-2-7B-Chat-GPTQ as a target model to perform tree-based speculative decoding across four benchmark domains: code generation, mathematical reasoning, logical reasoning, and open-ended chat. Collect 99,768 speculative nodes from 200 prompts and compute statistics including per-domain acceptance rates, expected accepted token lengths per step, depth-acceptance profiles, and correlations between token entropy and acceptance.
AI.result: Task domain strongly predicts token acceptance behavior, more than tree depth. Only the chat domain achieves an expected accepted length greater than 1 token per step. Across all domains, the correlation between model entropy and acceptance is negative but weak (Spearman rho between -0.20 and -0.15). Surprisingly, the chat domain shows both the highest entropy and the highest acceptance rate, contrary to naive expectations based on entropy alone.
AI.conclusion: Cognitive/task characteristics significantly shape speculative decoding dynamics. Domain type matters more than tree depth for acceptance, and RLHF-aligned chat language, despite being high-entropy, is lexically predictable enough to support efficient speculation. These insights suggest practitioners should tune speculation budgets and choose draft models with domain awareness, rather than relying on domain-agnostic configurations.

title: The Cost of Language: Centroid Erasure Exposes and Exploits Modal Competition in Multimodal Language Models
summary: Multimodal language models systematically underperform on visual perception tasks, yet the structure underlying this failure remains poorly understood. We propose centroid replacement, collapsing each token to its nearest K-means centroid, as a controlled probe for modal dependence. Across seven models spanning three architecture families, erasing text centroid structure costs 4$\times$ more accuracy than erasing visual centroid structure, exposing a universal imbalance where language representations overshadow vision even on tasks that demand visual reasoning. We exploit this asymmetry through text centroid contrastive decoding, recovering up to +16.9% accuracy on individual tasks by contrastively decoding against a text-centroid-erased reference. This intervention varies meaningfully with training approaches: standard fine-tuned models show larger gains (+5.6% on average) than preference-optimized models (+1.5% on average). Our findings suggest that modal competition is structurally localized, correctable at inference time without retraining, and quantifiable as a diagnostic signal to guide future multimodal training.

AI.tldr: The paper studies why multimodal language models rely too much on text and not enough on vision, and introduces centroid-based probing and decoding methods to diagnose and mitigate this imbalance without retraining.
AI.motivation: Multimodal language models often perform poorly on visual perception and visual reasoning tasks, but it is unclear how and where the imbalance between language and vision arises inside the models. The authors want a principled, architecture-agnostic way to probe how much each modality contributes to decisions, and to see whether this imbalance can be leveraged or corrected at inference time. They also want to understand how different training strategies (fine-tuning vs. preference optimization) affect this modal dependence.
AI.method: They introduce "centroid replacement": using K-means to cluster token embeddings and then replacing each token’s representation with the nearest cluster centroid. By selectively erasing structure in either the text or visual tokens (while keeping the other modality intact), they can measure how much each modality contributes to performance. They run this across seven models from three architecture families. Building on the observed asymmetry, they propose text centroid contrastive decoding: decoding answers while contrasting against a reference run in which the text centroids have been erased, to suppress over-reliance on language patterns and emphasize visual information.
AI.result: When they erase the centroid structure of text tokens, model accuracy drops about four times more than when they erase visual centroid structure, across all tested models. This reveals a consistent, cross-architecture bias where language dominates over vision even on visually demanding tasks. Using text centroid contrastive decoding, they obtain substantial accuracy improvements on specific benchmarks, with gains up to +16.9% on some tasks. On average, standard fine-tuned models benefit more (+5.6% accuracy) than preference-optimized models (+1.5%), indicating training method modulates the degree of modal imbalance and how correctable it is via this technique.
AI.conclusion: Multimodal models exhibit a structural, quantifiable imbalance where textual representations overshadow visual ones. This imbalance is localized enough that a simple centroid-based intervention at inference time can both diagnose and partially correct it, improving performance without retraining. The magnitude of the effect depends on training regime, suggesting that future multimodal training should explicitly account for modal competition and may use centroid-based diagnostics as a guide for better balancing language and vision.

title: DR$^{3}$-Eval: Towards Realistic and Reproducible Deep Research Evaluation
summary: Deep Research Agents (DRAs) aim to solve complex, long-horizon research tasks involving planning, retrieval, multimodal understanding, and report generation, yet their evaluation remains challenging due to dynamic web environments and ambiguous task definitions. We propose DR$^{3}$-Eval, a realistic and reproducible benchmark for evaluating deep research agents on multimodal, multi-file report generation. DR$^{3}$-Eval is constructed from authentic user-provided materials and paired with a per-task static research sandbox corpus that simulates open-web complexity while remaining fully verifiable, containing supportive documents, distractors, and noise. Moreover, we introduce a multi-dimensional evaluation framework measuring Information Recall, Factual Accuracy, Citation Coverage, Instruction Following, and Depth Quality, and validate its alignment with human judgments. Experiments with our developed multi-agent system DR$^{3}$-Agent based on multiple state-of-the-art language models demonstrate that DR$^{3}$-Eval is highly challenging and reveals critical failure modes in retrieval robustness and hallucination control. Our code and data are publicly available.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces DR^3-Eval, a realistic, reproducible benchmark and evaluation framework for testing deep research agents on complex multimodal, multi-file report generation tasks.
AI.motivation: Evaluating deep research agents is difficult because real-world web environments are dynamic, tasks are loosely defined, and existing benchmarks often lack realism, reproducibility, and clear, multi-faceted quality measures. The authors seek a way to systematically and verifiably assess how well such agents handle complex, long-horizon research and reporting tasks.
AI.method: They build DR^3-Eval, a benchmark composed of authentic, user-provided materials and, for each task, a static research sandbox corpus that mimics open-web complexity while remaining fully controlled and verifiable. Each sandbox includes relevant documents, distractors, and noise. They also design a multi-dimensional evaluation framework with metrics for Information Recall, Factual Accuracy, Citation Coverage, Instruction Following, and Depth Quality, and validate these metrics against human judgments. Finally, they implement a multi-agent deep research system, DR^3-Agent, using several state-of-the-art language models and run experiments on DR^3-Eval.
AI.result: Experiments show that DR^3-Eval is challenging even for strong multi-agent systems and uncovers important weaknesses, especially in retrieval robustness and hallucination control. The multi-dimensional metrics correlate well with human evaluations, indicating the framework is a meaningful measure of agent performance. The benchmark, along with its code and data, is released publicly.
AI.conclusion: DR^3-Eval provides a realistic, reproducible, and fine-grained benchmark for evaluating deep research agents on complex multimodal report generation tasks. It offers a controlled yet challenging environment that reveals key failure modes, particularly in retrieval and hallucination mitigation, and supplies validated metrics aligned with human judgment, positioning it as a useful platform for future research and system comparison.

title: BiCon-Gate: Consistency-Gated De-colloquialisation for Dialogue Fact-Checking
summary: Automated fact-checking in dialogue involves multi-turn conversations where colloquial language is frequent yet understudied. To address this gap, we propose a conservative rewrite candidate for each response claim via staged de-colloquialisation, combining lightweight surface normalisation with scoped in-claim coreference resolution. We then introduce BiCon-Gate, a semantics-aware consistency gate that selects the rewrite candidate only when it is semantically supported by the dialogue context, otherwise falling back to the original claim. This gated selection stabilises downstream fact-checking and yields gains in both evidence retrieval and fact verification. On the DialFact benchmark, our approach improves retrieval and verification, with particularly strong gains on SUPPORTS, and outperforms competitive baselines, including a decoder-based one-shot LLM rewrite that attempts to perform all de-colloquialisation steps in a single pass.

AI.tldr: They improve automated fact-checking in dialogues by conservatively rewriting colloquial claims and using a semantic gate to decide when to apply the rewrite, boosting retrieval and verification on a dialogue fact-checking benchmark.
AI.motivation: Existing automated fact-checking methods struggle with multi-turn dialogues where colloquial, context-dependent expressions are common. Colloquial claims can hurt both evidence retrieval and factual verification because they are noisy, underspecified, or rely heavily on dialogue context. Prior work has not sufficiently addressed how to systematically normalise such colloquial language without distorting meaning.
AI.method: The paper introduces a two-part method. First, they generate a conservative rewrite candidate for each response claim using staged de-colloquialisation: (1) lightweight surface normalisation to clean up informal phrasing, and (2) scoped, in-claim coreference resolution to make implicit references explicit, while trying to preserve original semantics. Second, they propose BiCon-Gate, a semantics-aware consistency gate that decides whether to use the rewritten claim or fall back to the original. The gate examines support from the dialogue context to ensure that the rewrite is semantically consistent with the conversation before passing it to downstream fact-checking modules.
AI.result: On the DialFact benchmark, their approach improves both evidence retrieval quality and final fact verification accuracy, with especially strong performance gains on SUPPORTS-labelled instances. Their method outperforms several strong baselines, including a decoder-based one-shot large language model that attempts to perform all de-colloquialisation operations in a single rewrite step.
AI.conclusion: Carefully staged, conservative de-colloquialisation combined with a semantic consistency gate can make dialogue claims more suitable for automated fact-checking without introducing harmful meaning shifts. This leads to more stable and accurate evidence retrieval and verification than using either raw colloquial text or aggressive one-shot LLM rewrites.

title: M2-PALE: A Framework for Explaining Multi-Agent MCTS--Minimax Hybrids via Process Mining and LLMs
summary: Monte-Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) is a fundamental sampling-based search algorithm widely used for online planning in sequential decision-making domains. Despite its success in driving recent advances in artificial intelligence, understanding the behavior of MCTS agents remains a challenge for both developers and users. This difficulty stems from the complex search trees produced through the simulation of numerous future states and their intricate relationships. A known weakness of standard MCTS is its reliance on highly selective tree construction, which may lead to the omission of crucial moves and a vulnerability to tactical traps. To resolve this, we incorporate shallow, full-width Minimax search into the rollout phase of multi-agent MCTS to enhance strategic depth. Furthermore, to demystify the resulting decision-making logic, we introduce \textsf{M2-PALE} (MCTS--Minimax Process-Aided Linguistic Explanations). This framework employs process mining techniques, specifically the Alpha Miner, iDHM, and Inductive Miner algorithms, to extract underlying behavioral workflows from agent execution traces. These process models are then synthesized by LLMs to generate human-readable causal and distal explanations. We demonstrate the efficacy of our approach in a small-scale checkers environment, establishing a scalable foundation for interpreting hybrid agents in increasingly complex strategic domains.

AI.tldr: Proposes a hybrid MCTS+Minimax agent plus an explanation framework (M2-PALE) that uses process mining and LLMs to turn agent traces into human-readable causal explanations, demonstrated in checkers.
AI.motivation: Standard MCTS, while powerful, is hard to interpret and can miss critical tactical moves due to highly selective tree construction, making agents both opaque and vulnerable to traps. There is a need both to improve MCTS’s tactical robustness and to make its decision-making process understandable to humans.
AI.method: 1) Modify multi-agent MCTS by adding shallow, full-width Minimax search in the rollout phase to increase strategic depth and reduce missed tactical moves. 2) Log agent execution traces and apply process mining algorithms (Alpha Miner, iDHM, Inductive Miner) to infer process models that represent typical behavioral workflows of the hybrid agent. 3) Use large language models to synthesize these process models into natural-language explanations describing causal and distal (longer-term) reasons for decisions. 4) Evaluate the approach in a constrained checkers environment.
AI.result: The hybrid MCTS+Minimax agent operates effectively in a small-scale checkers setting, and the process mining plus LLM pipeline successfully extracts coherent behavioral workflows and translates them into understandable explanations of the agent’s decision logic.
AI.conclusion: Integrating shallow Minimax search into MCTS rollouts can mitigate certain weaknesses of standard MCTS, while the M2-PALE framework provides a scalable way to interpret hybrid search-based agents. The checkers case study suggests this combination can both strengthen tactical performance and yield human-readable, causal explanations, forming a basis for application to more complex strategic domains.

title: Generating Concept Lexicalizations via Dictionary-Based Cross-Lingual Sense Projection
comment: To be published in the proceedings of Canadian AI 2026
summary: We study the task of automatically expanding WordNet-style lexical resources to new languages through sense generation. We generate senses by associating target-language lemmas with existing lexical concepts via semantic projection. Given a sense-tagged English corpus and its translation, our method projects English synsets onto aligned target-language tokens and assigns the corresponding lemmas to those synsets. To generate these alignments and ensure their quality, we augment a pre-trained base aligner with a bilingual dictionary, which is also used to filter out incorrect sense projections. We evaluate the method on multiple languages, comparing it to prior methods, as well as dictionary-based and large language model baselines. Results show that the proposed project-and-filter strategy improves precision while remaining interpretable and requiring few external resources. We plan to make our code, documentation, and generated sense inventories accessible.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a projection-based method to automatically add word senses in new languages to WordNet-style lexical resources using parallel corpora and bilingual dictionaries.
AI.motivation: Existing WordNet-style lexical resources are extensive for English but limited or missing for many other languages. Manually constructing sense inventories is costly and slow, and previous automatic approaches either depend heavily on resources or sacrifice interpretability and precision. There is a need for a method that can reliably expand sense inventories to new languages with minimal additional resources and in an interpretable way.
AI.method: The authors take a sense-tagged English corpus and its translation into a target language. Using a pre-trained word aligner, they align English tokens to target-language tokens. English synsets (WordNet concepts) attached to the English tokens are then projected to the aligned target-language tokens, and the corresponding lemmas in the target language are associated with these synsets, effectively generating new senses. To improve the quality of alignments, they augment a base aligner with a bilingual dictionary; the same dictionary is used to filter out incorrect sense projections. The resulting method is a project-and-filter pipeline that uses semantic projection plus dictionary-based quality control.
AI.result: Across multiple target languages, the proposed method is evaluated against prior automatic expansion methods, against a purely dictionary-based approach, and against baselines using large language models. The experiments show that their project-and-filter strategy yields higher precision in the generated senses than competing methods, while remaining resource-light and interpretable.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that semantic projection from a sense-tagged English corpus, combined with dictionary-augmented alignment and filtering, is an effective and efficient way to expand WordNet-style sense inventories to new languages. The method improves precision over existing approaches, uses relatively few external resources, and maintains interpretability. The authors intend to release their code, documentation, and generated sense inventories to support further research and practical use.

title: CAMO: An Agentic Framework for Automated Causal Discovery from Micro Behaviors to Macro Emergence in LLM Agent Simulations
summary: LLM-empowered agent simulations are increasingly used to study social emergence, yet the micro-to-macro causal mechanisms behind macro outcomes often remain unclear. This is challenging because emergence arises from intertwined agent interactions and meso-level feedback and nonlinearity, making generative mechanisms hard to disentangle. To this end, we introduce \textbf{\textsc{CAMO}}, an automated \textbf{Ca}usal discovery framework from \textbf{M}icr\textbf{o} behaviors to \textbf{M}acr\textbf{o} Emergence in LLM agent simulations. \textsc{CAMO} converts mechanistic hypotheses into computable factors grounded in simulation records and learns a compact causal representation centered on an emergent target $Y$. \textsc{CAMO} outputs a computable Markov boundary and a minimal upstream explanatory subgraph, yielding interpretable causal chains and actionable intervention levers. It also uses simulator-internal counterfactual probing to orient ambiguous edges and revise hypotheses when evidence contradicts the current view. Experiments across four emergent settings demonstrate the promise of \textsc{CAMO}.

AI.tldr: CAMO is a causal discovery framework that links micro-level behaviors of LLM agents to macro-level emergent outcomes, producing interpretable causal structures and intervention levers.
AI.motivation: In LLM-based agent simulations, researchers increasingly study emergent social or collective phenomena. However, while these simulations can generate complex macro-level patterns, the precise causal pathways from individual agent behaviors and interactions (micro level) to aggregate outcomes (macro level) are usually opaque. This opacity stems from intertwined interactions, meso-level feedback loops, and nonlinear dynamics that make it hard to isolate which mechanisms actually generate a given emergent pattern. The paper aims to provide a principled way to uncover and explain these micro-to-macro causal mechanisms, rather than treating emergence as a black box.
AI.method: The authors propose CAMO, an automated causal discovery framework tailored to LLM agent simulations. First, they express mechanistic hypotheses (about how emergence happens) as computable factors that can be measured from simulation logs. CAMO then learns a compact causal representation around a chosen emergent target variable Y, identifying its Markov boundary and constructing a minimal upstream explanatory subgraph that traces causal chains leading to Y. The framework includes procedures to orient ambiguous causal edges, using simulator-internal counterfactual interventions and probing to test alternative directions. When empirical evidence conflicts with initial hypotheses, CAMO can revise the hypothesized structure based on these counterfactual experiments.
AI.result: Across four different experimental settings involving emergent phenomena in LLM agent simulations, CAMO successfully discovers causal structures linking micro-level agent behaviors to macro-level outcomes. It yields interpretable causal chains and identifies actionable intervention points in the system. The results show that CAMO can both clarify which factors are genuinely causal for the emergent outcome and correct or refine initial mechanistic hypotheses when they are mis-specified.
AI.conclusion: CAMO demonstrates that it is feasible to move beyond purely descriptive analysis of LLM-based emergent simulations toward explicit, testable causal explanations of how micro-level processes generate macro-level outcomes. By converting hypotheses into computable factors, learning a causal representation focused on an emergent target, and using counterfactual probing to orient edges and revise structures, CAMO offers interpretable causal chains and practical levers for intervention. This framework can make studies of emergence in LLM agent societies more transparent, rigorous, and actionable.

title: The Autocorrelation Blind Spot: Why 42% of Turn-Level Findings in LLM Conversation Analysis May Be Spurious
comment: 14 pages, 3 figures, 5 tables, 1 algorithm. Code and synthetic demonstration data: https://github.com/ferdinandschessl-boop/autocorrelation-correction
summary: Turn-level metrics are widely used to evaluate properties of multi-turn human-LLM conversations, from safety and sycophancy to dialogue quality. However, consecutive turns within a conversation are not statistically independent -- a fact that virtually all current evaluation pipelines fail to correct for in their statistical inference. We systematically characterize the autocorrelation structure of 66 turn-level metrics across 202 multi-turn conversations (11,639 turn pairs, 5 German-speaking users, 4 LLM platforms) and demonstrate that naive pooled analysis produces severely inflated significance estimates: 42% of associations that appear significant under standard pooled testing fail to survive cluster-robust correction. The inflation varies substantially across categories rather than scaling linearly with autocorrelation: three memoryless families (embedding velocity, directional, differential) aggregate to 14%, while the seven non-memoryless families (thermo-cycle, frame distance, lexical/structural, rolling windows, cumulative, interaction, timestamp) aggregate to 33%, with individual category rates ranging from 0% to 100% depending on per-family effect size. We present a two-stage correction framework combining Chelton (1983) effective degrees of freedom with conversation-level block bootstrap, and validate it on a pre-registered hold-out split where cluster-robust metrics replicate at 57% versus 30% for pooled-only metrics. We provide concrete design principles, a publication checklist, and open-source code for the correction pipeline. A survey of ~30 recent papers at major NLP and AI venues that compute turn-level statistics in LLM evaluations finds that only 4 address temporal dependence at all, and 26 do not correct for it.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that common ways of evaluating multi-turn human-LLM conversations using turn-level metrics ignore temporal dependence, leading to inflated statistical significance, and proposes a correction framework.
AI.motivation: Researchers widely use turn-level metrics to study safety, sycophancy, and dialogue quality in multi-turn conversations with LLMs, but these metrics assume each turn is independent. In reality, consecutive turns are autocorrelated, so standard pooled analyses may report spurious significant effects, threatening the validity of many recent evaluation results.
AI.method: The authors empirically analyze the autocorrelation structure of 66 turn-level metrics across 202 multi-turn conversations involving 5 German-speaking users and 4 LLM platforms (11,639 turn pairs). They compare naive pooled significance testing with cluster-robust methods, categorize metrics into memoryless vs. non-memoryless families, and quantify how often significance disappears after correction. They then propose a two-stage correction framework that combines Chelton (1983) effective degrees of freedom adjustment with conversation-level block bootstrapping, and validate this framework on a pre-registered hold-out split.
AI.result: They find strong temporal dependence in many metrics and show that naive pooled analysis substantially inflates significance: 42% of associations that appear significant with standard pooled tests fail under cluster-robust correction. Inflation differs by metric family: memoryless families show lower inflation (14% overall) while non-memoryless families show higher inflation (33% overall, with some categories up to 100% loss of significance). Their proposed correction framework produces more reliable results, with cluster-robust metrics replicating at 57% on a hold-out set versus 30% for metrics that relied only on pooled analysis.
AI.conclusion: Ignoring temporal dependence in turn-level metrics for LLM evaluations leads to overly optimistic and often spurious significance claims. Autocorrelation patterns vary widely across metric types, making simple linear adjustments inadequate. The authors recommend and provide a concrete two-stage correction framework, practical design principles, and a checklist for future work, and they show that most recent papers in major NLP and AI venues do not handle temporal dependence properly, underscoring the need for methodological reform.

title: SynHAT: A Two-stage Coarse-to-Fine Diffusion Framework for Synthesizing Human Activity Traces
summary: Human activity traces (HATs) are critical for many applications, including human mobility modeling and point-of-interest (POI) recommendation. However, growing privacy concerns have severely limited access to authentic large-scale HAT datasets. Recent advances in generative AI provide new opportunities to synthesize realistic and privacy-preserving HATs for such applications. Yet two major challenges remain: (i) HATs are highly irregular and dynamic, with long and varying time intervals, making it difficult to capture their complex spatio-temporal dependencies and underlying distributions; and (ii) generative models are often computationally expensive, making long-term, fine-grained HAT synthesis inefficient. To address these challenges, we propose SynHAT, a computationally efficient coarse-to-fine HAT synthesis framework built on a novel spatio-temporal denoising diffusion model. In Stage 1, we develop Coarse-HADiff, which models the overall spatio-temporal dependencies of coarse-grained latent spatio-temporal traces. It incorporates a novel Latent Spatio-Temporal U-Net with dual Drift-Jitter branches to jointly model smooth spatial transitions and temporal variations during denoising. In Stage 2, we introduce a three-step pipeline consisting of Behavior Pattern Extraction, Fine-HADiff, which shares the same architecture as Coarse-HADiff, and Semantic Alignment to generate fine-grained latent spatio-temporal traces from the Stage 1 outputs. We extensively evaluate SynHAT in terms of data fidelity, utility, privacy, robustness, and scalability. Experiments on real-world HAT datasets from four cities across three countries show that SynHAT substantially outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, achieving 52% and 33% improvements on spatial and temporal metrics, respectively.

AI.tldr: SynHAT proposes a two-stage, coarse-to-fine generative framework using a novel spatio-temporal diffusion model to efficiently synthesize realistic, privacy-preserving human activity traces that better match real-world mobility data than prior methods.
AI.motivation: Access to large-scale, authentic human activity traces is restricted due to privacy concerns, yet such data are essential for tasks like human mobility modeling and POI recommendation. Existing generative methods struggle with the irregular, long-horizon, and dynamic nature of human activity traces and are often too computationally heavy for long-term, fine-grained synthesis. There is a need for a generative approach that can capture complex spatio-temporal dependencies while remaining efficient and privacy-preserving.
AI.method: The paper introduces SynHAT, a two-stage coarse-to-fine synthesis framework built on a spatio-temporal denoising diffusion model. Stage 1 (Coarse-HADiff) learns overall spatio-temporal dependencies on coarse-grained latent traces using a Latent Spatio-Temporal U-Net with dual Drift-Jitter branches to separately handle smooth spatial transitions and temporal variations during denoising. Stage 2 refines these outputs via a three-step pipeline: (1) Behavior Pattern Extraction from coarse traces, (2) Fine-HADiff, architecturally similar to Coarse-HADiff, to generate fine-grained latent traces, and (3) Semantic Alignment to map latent outputs back to semantically meaningful spatio-temporal trajectories. The framework is evaluated on multiple real-world HAT datasets.
AI.result: On datasets from four cities across three countries, SynHAT is shown to generate synthetic HATs that better match real data compared to state-of-the-art baselines. Quantitatively, it achieves up to 52% improvement on spatial metrics and 33% improvement on temporal metrics, while being computationally efficient. Additional evaluations demonstrate advantages in data utility, privacy preservation, robustness, and scalability.
AI.conclusion: SynHAT effectively addresses the dual challenges of modeling complex, irregular human activity traces and maintaining computational efficiency for long-term, fine-grained synthesis. Its coarse-to-fine, diffusion-based design with specialized spatio-temporal modeling components yields synthetic data that are both realistic and privacy-preserving, significantly outperforming existing generative approaches on multiple benchmarks.

title: HWE-Bench: Benchmarking LLM Agents on Real-World Hardware Bug Repair Tasks
summary: Existing benchmarks for hardware design primarily evaluate Large Language Models (LLMs) on isolated, component-level tasks such as generating HDL modules from specifications, leaving repository-scale evaluation unaddressed. We introduce HWE-Bench, the first large-scale, repository-level benchmark for evaluating LLM agents on real-world hardware bug repair tasks. HWE-Bench comprises 417 task instances derived from real historical bug-fix pull requests across six major open-source projects spanning both Verilog/SystemVerilog and Chisel, covering RISC-V cores, SoCs, and security roots-of-trust. Each task is grounded in a fully containerized environment where the agent must resolve a real bug report, with correctness validated through the project's native simulation and regression flows. The benchmark is built through a largely automated pipeline that enables efficient expansion to new repositories. We evaluate seven LLMs with four agent frameworks and find that the best agent resolves 70.7% of tasks overall, with performance exceeding 90% on smaller cores but dropping below 65% on complex SoC-level projects. We observe larger performance gaps across models than commonly reported on software benchmarks, and difficulty is driven by project scope and bug-type distribution rather than code size alone. Our failure analysis traces agent failures to three stages of the debugging process: fault localization, hardware-semantic reasoning, and cross-artifact coordination across RTL, configuration, and verification components, providing concrete directions for developing more capable hardware-aware agents.

AI.tldr: Introduces HWE-Bench, a large, repository-level benchmark to evaluate LLM agents on real-world hardware bug repair tasks.
AI.motivation: Current hardware-design LLM benchmarks focus on narrow, component-level tasks (e.g., generating HDL modules) and do not capture the real-world complexity of debugging and repairing hardware repositories. There is a need for a realistic, repository-scale benchmark that reflects how LLM agents perform on authentic hardware bug-fix workflows.
AI.method: The authors construct HWE-Bench from 417 real historical bug-fix pull requests across six major open-source hardware projects (RISC-V cores, SoCs, and security roots-of-trust) implemented in Verilog/SystemVerilog and Chisel. Each benchmark instance is set up in a containerized environment mirroring the original project, requiring an LLM agent to resolve a real bug report with correctness checked via the project’s native simulation and regression flows. The benchmark is created through a largely automated pipeline to allow easy extension to additional repositories. They then evaluate seven LLMs combined with four agent frameworks on these tasks.
AI.result: The best-performing LLM agent solves 70.7% of all benchmark tasks, surpassing 90% success on smaller core designs but falling below 65% on more complex SoC-level projects. Performance differences between models are larger than typically seen on software benchmarks. Analysis shows that task difficulty correlates more with project scope and the distribution of bug types than with raw code size.
AI.conclusion: HWE-Bench exposes significant challenges for current LLM agents on realistic hardware bug-repair tasks and reveals larger model performance gaps than standard software benchmarks. The main failure modes cluster around fault localization, hardware-specific semantic reasoning, and coordinating changes across multiple artifacts (RTL, configuration, verification). These insights highlight concrete research directions for building more capable, hardware-aware LLM agents and provide a scalable benchmark for tracking progress.

title: Hierarchical vs. Flat Iteration in Shared-Weight Transformers
summary: We present an empirical study of whether hierarchically structured, shared-weight recurrence can match the representational quality of independent-layer stacking in a Transformer-based language model. HRM-LM replaces L independent Transformer layers with a two-speed recurrent pair: a Fast module operating at every step for local refinement, and a Slow module operating every T steps for global compression. This recurrent hierarchy is unrolled for M = N x T steps with shared parameters. The central and most robust finding, supported by a parameter-matched Universal Transformer ablation (UniTF, 1.2B) across five independent runs, is a sharp empirical gap between the two approaches.

AI.tldr: Empirical comparison of hierarchical recurrent Transformer vs standard stacked layers, finding a clear performance gap.
AI.motivation: Investigate whether reusing parameters via hierarchical, shared-weight recurrence can achieve the same representational quality as conventional independently parameterized Transformer layers, potentially yielding more parameter-efficient language models.
AI.method: Propose HRM-LM, which replaces L independent Transformer layers with a hierarchical recurrent architecture consisting of a Fast module (runs every step for local refinement) and a Slow module (runs every T steps for global compression), unrolled for M = N x T steps with shared parameters. Compare it empirically, including against a parameter-matched Universal Transformer baseline (UniTF, 1.2B), using multiple (five) independent runs.
AI.result: Experiments reveal a strong and consistent empirical performance gap between HRM-LM’s hierarchical shared-weight recurrence and standard stacking, favoring the latter; the Universal Transformer ablation confirms this gap under parameter-matched conditions across five runs.
AI.conclusion: Hierarchical shared-weight recurrence, as instantiated by HRM-LM, does not match the representational quality of independent-layer stacking in Transformer language models under the tested conditions, indicating limitations of this form of parameter sharing for high-quality language modeling.

title: SGA-MCTS: Decoupling Planning from Execution via Training-Free Atomic Experience Retrieval
summary: LLM-powered systems require complex multi-step decision-making abilities to solve real-world tasks, yet current planning approaches face a trade-off between the high latency of inference-time search and the limited generalization of supervised fine-tuning. To address this limitation, we introduce \textbf{SGA-MCTS}, a framework that casts LLM planning as non-parametric retrieval. Offline, we leverage Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) to explore the solution space and distill high-fidelity trajectories into State-Goal-Action (SGA) atoms. These atoms are de-lexicalized primitives that abstract concrete entities into symbolic slots, preserving reusable causal logic while discarding domain-specific noise. Online, a retrieval-augmented agent employs a hybrid symbolic-semantic mechanism to fetch relevant SGAs and re-ground them into the current context as soft reasoning hints. Empirical results on complex benchmarks demonstrate that this paradigm enables frozen, open-weights models to match the performance of SOTA systems (e.g., GPT-5) without task-specific fine-tuning. By effectively amortizing the heavy computational cost of search, SGA-MCTS achieves System 2 reasoning depth at System 1 inference speeds, rendering autonomous planning both scalable and real-time feasible.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes SGA-MCTS, a framework that uses offline Monte Carlo Tree Search to extract reusable symbolic State-Goal-Action patterns and online retrieval to guide LLM reasoning, achieving strong planning performance with frozen models and low latency.
AI.motivation: LLMs struggle with real-world tasks that require multi-step planning because inference-time search is slow and expensive, while supervised fine-tuning often fails to generalize across tasks. The authors want a way to get deep, high-quality planning behavior from open-weight, frozen models without paying high online compute costs or relying on brittle task-specific training.
AI.method: Offline, the method runs Monte Carlo Tree Search over tasks to discover high-quality solution trajectories. These trajectories are converted into de-lexicalized State-Goal-Action (SGA) atoms, which abstract away concrete entities into symbolic slots, keeping general causal structure. These SGA atoms are stored as a non-parametric memory. Online, when solving a new task, a retrieval-augmented agent uses a hybrid symbolic-semantic retrieval mechanism to find relevant SGAs and then re-ground them into the current context, feeding them to the LLM as soft reasoning hints to steer its planning without changing its weights.
AI.result: On complex planning benchmarks, the approach allows frozen, open-weights LLMs to reach performance comparable to state-of-the-art closed models such as GPT-5, despite not using task-specific fine-tuning and incurring only light online computation. The experiments show that amortizing MCTS search offline via SGA retrieval significantly narrows the gap between inexpensive models and top-tier systems in multi-step decision-making tasks.
AI.conclusion: Casting LLM planning as non-parametric retrieval over abstracted State-Goal-Action atoms enables deep, search-like reasoning at low inference-time cost. By separating heavy offline search from lightweight online retrieval and grounding, SGA-MCTS makes scalable, real-time autonomous planning feasible for frozen open models, effectively achieving System 2-level reasoning quality with System 1-level speed.

title: MARCA: A Checklist-Based Benchmark for Multilingual Web Search
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used as sources of information, yet their reliability depends on the ability to search the web, select relevant evidence, and synthesize complete answers. While recent benchmarks evaluate web-browsing and agentic tool use, multilingual settings, and Portuguese in particular, remain underexplored. We present \textsc{MARCA}, a bilingual (English and Portuguese) benchmark for evaluating LLMs on web-based information seeking. \textsc{MARCA} consists of 52 manually authored multi-entity questions, paired with manually validated checklist-style rubrics that explicitly measure answer completeness and correctness. We evaluate 14 models under two interaction settings: a Basic framework with direct web search and scraping, and an Orchestrator framework that enables task decomposition via delegated subagents. To capture stochasticity, each question is executed multiple times and performance is reported with run-level uncertainty. Across models, we observe large performance differences, find that orchestration often improves coverage, and identify substantial variability in how models transfer from English to Portuguese. The benchmark is available at https://github.com/maritaca-ai/MARCA

AI.tldr: Introduces MARCA, a bilingual (English/Portuguese) benchmark to evaluate how well LLMs perform web-based information seeking, with checklist rubrics and uncertainty-aware evaluation.
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks for LLM web-browsing and tool use rarely cover multilingual scenarios, and Portuguese in particular is underexplored. There is a need to rigorously measure LLMs’ ability to search, select evidence, and provide complete, correct answers across languages.
AI.method: The authors build MARCA, a benchmark of 52 manually crafted, multi-entity questions in English and Portuguese. Each question is paired with a manually validated checklist-style rubric that breaks down the required answer components. They test 14 LLMs in two web-interaction setups: (1) a Basic framework with straightforward web search and page scraping, and (2) an Orchestrator framework with subagent-based task decomposition. Each question is run multiple times per model to quantify stochastic variability, and performance metrics include answer completeness and correctness with run-level uncertainty.
AI.result: They observe large differences in performance across models, show that the orchestrated agent framework frequently yields better coverage of required answer components than the basic setup, and find notable variation in how well models’ web-seeking capabilities transfer from English to Portuguese.
AI.conclusion: MARCA provides a bilingual, rubric-based benchmark and evaluation protocol for LLM web information seeking, revealing model gaps, the benefits of orchestration, and uneven cross-lingual transfer. The benchmark can be used to systematically compare and improve LLMs’ web-browsing behavior in both English and Portuguese.

title: Layered Mutability: Continuity and Governance in Persistent Self-Modifying Agents
comment: 17 pages, 2 figures, 3 tables. self-modifying agents; AI governance; identity drift; persistent memory; runtime adaptation; model editing Primary: cs.AI Cross-list: cs.LG, cs.CY
summary: Persistent language-model agents increasingly combine tool use, tiered memory, reflective prompting, and runtime adaptation. In such systems, behavior is shaped not only by current prompts but by mutable internal conditions that influence future action. This paper introduces layered mutability, a framework for reasoning about that process across five layers: pretraining, post-training alignment, self-narrative, memory, and weight-level adaptation. The central claim is that governance difficulty rises when mutation is rapid, downstream coupling is strong, reversibility is weak, and observability is low, creating a systematic mismatch between the layers that most affect behavior and the layers humans can most easily inspect. I formalize this intuition with simple drift, governance-load, and hysteresis quantities, connect the framework to recent work on temporal identity in language-model agents, and report a preliminary ratchet experiment in which reverting an agent's visible self-description after memory accumulation fails to restore baseline behavior. In that experiment, the estimated identity hysteresis ratio is 0.68. The main implication is that the salient failure mode for persistent self-modifying agents is not abrupt misalignment but compositional drift: locally reasonable updates that accumulate into a behavioral trajectory that was never explicitly authorized.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes how different forms of change in persistent language-model agents make them harder to govern, proposing a five-layer framework and showing that behavior can drift in ways that are hard to reverse or observe.
AI.motivation: Persistent language-model agents now use tools, memory, and self-modification, so their behavior depends on evolving internal states, not just prompts. This creates governance and safety challenges because the most behaviorally important changes may be the hardest to monitor or reverse. The paper aims to clarify and formalize where and why governance becomes difficult as agents change over time.
AI.method: The author proposes a conceptual framework called layered mutability, dividing sources of change into five layers: pretraining, post-training alignment, self-narrative (how the agent describes itself), memory (accumulated interaction history), and weight-level adaptation. They introduce simple quantitative notions—drift, governance load, and hysteresis—to capture how changes accumulate and how hard they are to control. They then run a preliminary experiment: an agent accumulates memories that shape its behavior; later, the visible self-description is reverted to baseline to test whether the original behavior returns, and they estimate an identity hysteresis ratio from this setup.
AI.result: The framework identifies conditions—rapid mutation, strong coupling to future behavior, weak reversibility, and poor observability—under which governance becomes particularly hard, often in layers that are less visible to humans. In the experiment, resetting the agent’s self-description after memory accumulation does not fully restore earlier behavior, showing path dependence and hysteresis. The measured identity hysteresis ratio is 0.68, indicating substantial behavioral inertia from past states despite surface-level reset attempts.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that for persistent, self-modifying language-model agents, the primary risk is not sudden catastrophic misalignment, but gradual compositional drift: a sequence of seemingly reasonable updates leading to a behavioral profile that was never explicitly approved. Because the most influential changes can be less observable and less reversible, governance approaches must account for layered mutability, path dependence, and hysteresis rather than focusing only on static alignment at deployment time.

title: Filling in the Mechanisms: How do LMs Learn Filler-Gap Dependencies under Developmental Constraints?
comment: To be published in the 64th Annual Meeting of the Association for Computational Linguistics
summary: For humans, filler-gap dependencies require a shared representation across different syntactic constructions. Although causal analyses suggest this may also be true for LLMs (Boguraev et al., 2025), it is still unclear if such a representation also exists for language models trained on developmentally feasible quantities of data. We applied Distributed Alignment Search (DAS, Geiger et al. (2024)) to LMs trained on varying amounts of data from the BabyLM challenge (Warstadt et al., 2023), to evaluate whether representations of filler-gap dependencies transfer between wh-questions and topicalization, which greatly vary in terms of their input frequency. Our results suggest shared, yet item-sensitive mechanisms may develop with limited training data. More importantly, LMs still require far more data than humans to learn comparable generalizations, highlighting the need for language-specific biases in models of language acquisition.

AI.tldr: The paper tests whether small data-trained language models develop shared representations for filler-gap dependencies across constructions, finding partial transfer but much weaker data efficiency than humans, implying a need for stronger linguistic inductive biases.
AI.motivation: Humans can generalize filler-gap dependencies (e.g., wh-movement) across very different constructions with relatively scant input. Prior work with large LLMs suggests they also use shared internal mechanisms, but we do not know whether such mechanisms still emerge when models are trained on human-plausible (developmentally feasible) data sizes. This matters for modeling language acquisition and for understanding whether current LMs’ success depends on massive data rather than human-like inductive biases.
AI.method: The authors use Distributed Alignment Search (DAS), a technique for aligning and comparing internal neural representations, on a set of language models trained on different-size subsets of the BabyLM corpus. They focus on filler-gap dependencies and test whether the representations that support such dependencies in wh-questions also transfer to topicalization structures, which differ dramatically in frequency in the training data. By varying data size, they probe when and how shared mechanisms emerge.
AI.result: They find evidence that the models do develop shared internal mechanisms for filler-gap dependencies across wh-questions and topicalization, but these mechanisms are item-sensitive and not fully abstract. The emergence and robustness of such shared representations depends strongly on the amount of training data: only with relatively large (for a child) data quantities do the models approach human-like generalization patterns.
AI.conclusion: Even with sophisticated analysis tools like DAS, LMs trained on developmentally feasible data show only partial, fragile sharing of filler-gap mechanisms across constructions. The models need substantially more data than human learners to achieve roughly comparable generalizations, supporting the view that successful language acquisition in humans depends on language-specific inductive biases that current models largely lack. This suggests future acquisition models should build in stronger structural biases rather than relying solely on scale.

title: The Agentification of Scientific Research: A Physicist's Perspective
summary: This article argues that the most important significance of the AI revolution, especially the rise of large language models, lies not simply in automation, but in a fundamental change in how complex information and human know-how are carried, replicated, and shared. From this perspective, AI for Science is especially important because it may transform not only the efficiency of research, but also the structure of scientific collaboration, discovery, publishing, and evaluation. The article outlines a gradual path from AI as a research tool to AI as a scientific collaborator, and discusses how AI is likely to fundamentally reshape scientific publication. It also argues that continuous learning and diversity of ideas are essential if AI is to play a meaningful role in original scientific discovery.

AI.tldr: The paper discusses how the AI revolution, driven by large language models, will transform the way scientific knowledge is created, shared, and evaluated, moving AI from a mere tool to an active collaborator in science.
AI.motivation: To explain why the core impact of modern AI is not just automation of tasks but a deep shift in how complex information and human expertise are represented and transmitted, and to explore what this means for the future of scientific research and communication.
AI.method: Conceptual and argumentative analysis: the authors outline a progressive trajectory for AI in science (from tool to collaborator), examine implications for collaboration, discovery, publication, and evaluation, and reason about conditions—such as continuous learning and intellectual diversity—needed for AI to contribute to original discovery.
AI.result: The paper proposes a framework describing stages in the integration of AI into scientific practice, identifies likely transformations in research workflows and publication systems, and highlights key requirements (like ongoing learning and diversity in training and use) for AI systems to move beyond mere automation toward genuine scientific contribution.
AI.conclusion: AI, particularly large language models, will increasingly mediate how scientific knowledge and know-how are encoded, shared, and expanded. If designed and used with continuous learning and diversity of ideas, AI systems can evolve from tools into meaningful scientific collaborators and catalysts for reshaping how science is conducted and published.

title: Psychological Steering of Large Language Models
summary: Large language models (LLMs) emulate a consistent human-like behavior that can be shaped through activation-level interventions. This paradigm is converging on additive residual-stream injections, which rely on injection-strength sweeps to approximate optimal intervention settings. However, existing methods restrict the search space and sweep in uncalibrated activation-space units, potentially missing optimal intervention conditions. Thus, we introduce a psychological steering framework that performs unbounded, fluency-constrained sweeps in semantically calibrated units. Our method derives and calibrates residual-stream injections using psychological artifacts, and we use the IPIP-NEO-120, which measures the OCEAN personality model, to compare six injection methods. We find that mean-difference (MD) injections outperform Personality Prompting (P$^2$), an established baseline for OCEAN steering, in open-ended generation in 11 of 14 LLMs, with gains of 3.6\% to 16.4\%, overturning prior reports favoring prompting and positioning representation engineering as a new frontier in open-ended psychological steering. Further, we find that a hybrid of P$^2$ and MD injections outperforms both methods in 13 of 14 LLMs, with gains over P$^2$ ranging from 5.6\% to 21.9\% and from 3.3\% to 26.7\% over MD injections. Finally, we show that MD injections align with the Linear Representation Hypothesis and provide reliable, approximately linear control knobs for psychological steering. Nevertheless, they also induce OCEAN trait covariance patterns that depart from the Big Two model, suggesting a gap between learned representations and human psychology.

AI.tldr: They propose a new way to steer LLM personalities by directly editing internal activations instead of just prompting, showing this works better for controlling OCEAN traits.
AI.motivation: Existing activation injection methods for controlling LLM behavior use restricted, poorly calibrated search spaces and often underperform prompt-based personality steering, risking suboptimal or misleading conclusions about how best to control model traits.
AI.method: They design a psychological steering framework that constructs residual-stream activation injections from psychologically grounded artifacts (IPIP-NEO-120 OCEAN questionnaire), calibrates them into semantically meaningful units, and performs unbounded, fluency-constrained sweeps. They compare six injection strategies, especially mean-difference (MD) vectors, against a strong prompting baseline (P^2) across 14 LLMs in open-ended generation, analyzing performance, linearity, and induced trait covariance.
AI.result: Mean-difference injections outperform the Personality Prompting baseline in most models (11/14) with 3.6–16.4% gains; a hybrid of MD injections with P^2 beats both in 13/14 models with 5.6–21.9% gains over P^2 and 3.3–26.7% over MD alone. MD injections behave approximately linearly and reliably as control knobs for OCEAN traits but yield trait covariance structures that diverge from the human “Big Two” personality model.
AI.conclusion: Representation-level interventions in the residual stream, particularly mean-difference injections, provide a more powerful and controllable method for psychological steering of LLMs than prompt-based methods, supporting linear representation hypotheses but also revealing systematic mismatches between LLM-internal personality structure and human personality models.

title: Personalized and Context-Aware Transformer Models for Predicting Post-Intervention Physiological Responses from Wearable Sensor Data
summary: Consumer wearables enable continuous measurement of physiological data related to stress and recovery, but turning these streams into actionable, personalized stress-management recommendations remains a challenge. In practice, users often do not know how a given intervention, defined as an activity intended to reduce stress, will affect heart rate (HR), heart rate variability (HRV), or inter-beat intervals (BBI) over the next 15 to 120 minutes. We present a framework that predicts post-intervention trajectories and the direction of change for these physiological indicators across time windows. Our methodology combines a Transformer model for multi-horizon trajectories of percent change relative to a pre-intervention baseline, direction-of-change calls (positive, negative, or neutral) at each horizon, and an empirical study using wearable sensor data overlaid with user-tagged events and interventions. This proof of concept shows that personalized post-intervention prediction is feasible. We encourage future integration into stress-management tools for personalized intervention recommendations tailored to each person's day following further validation in larger studies and, where applicable, appropriate regulatory review.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a machine-learning framework, based on Transformers and wearable sensor data, to predict how stress-reduction interventions will change heart-rate-related metrics over the next 15–120 minutes.
AI.motivation: Although consumer wearables collect rich, continuous physiological data related to stress and recovery, users still lack clear, personalized guidance on how specific stress-reduction activities will impact their short-term physiology. Existing tools typically summarize past data or give generic advice rather than forecasting individual responses to interventions in the near future. The authors aim to fill this gap by creating a system that can forecast personalized trajectories of key stress-related metrics after a given intervention, enabling more actionable stress-management recommendations.
AI.method: The authors develop a framework that: (1) uses wearable sensor data to obtain heart rate (HR), heart rate variability (HRV), and inter-beat interval (BBI) measurements; (2) aligns these data with user-tagged events and interventions meant to reduce stress; (3) defines a pre-intervention baseline and then models the percent change from that baseline over multiple future time horizons (15 to 120 minutes) after an intervention; and (4) employs a Transformer-based model to generate multi-horizon trajectory predictions plus categorical direction-of-change predictions (positive, negative, neutral) for each metric at each horizon. They conduct an empirical proof-of-concept study using this pipeline.
AI.result: The framework successfully produces individualized predictions of how HR, HRV, and BBI evolve over the 15–120 minutes after stress-reduction interventions. The results indicate that the model can capture user-specific patterns and provide feasible forecasts of both the magnitude (percent change) and direction of physiological changes post-intervention, demonstrating that personalized post-intervention prediction is technically achievable with wearable data and Transformers.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that it is feasible to use consumer-wearable data and Transformer models to predict personalized short-term physiological responses to stress-management interventions. While framed as a proof of concept with limited sample size, the work suggests that such models could be integrated into stress-management tools to recommend interventions tailored to how an individual’s physiology is likely to respond across the rest of their day. The authors emphasize the need for larger validation studies and appropriate regulatory review before clinical or commercial deployment.

title: CobwebTM: Probabilistic Concept Formation for Lifelong and Hierarchical Topic Modeling
summary: Topic modeling seeks to uncover latent semantic structure in text corpora with minimal supervision.
