Neural approaches achieve strong performance but require extensive tuning and struggle with lifelong learning due to catastrophic forgetting and fixed capacity, while classical probabilistic models lack flexibility and adaptability to streaming data. We introduce \textsc{CobwebTM}, a low-parameter lifelong hierarchical topic model based on incremental probabilistic concept formation. By adapting the Cobweb algorithm to continuous document embeddings, \textsc{CobwebTM} constructs semantic hierarchies online, enabling unsupervised topic discovery, dynamic topic creation, and hierarchical organization without predefining the number of topics. Across diverse datasets, \textsc{CobwebTM} achieves strong topic coherence, stable topics over time, and high-quality hierarchies, demonstrating that incremental symbolic concept formation combined with pretrained representations is an efficient approach to topic modeling.

AI.tldr: CobwebTM is an online, low-parameter hierarchical topic model that incrementally builds topic hierarchies from document embeddings, avoiding catastrophic forgetting and fixed topic numbers while maintaining coherent and stable topics over time.
AI.motivation: Existing neural topic models perform well but require heavy tuning, have fixed capacity, and suffer from catastrophic forgetting, making them ill-suited for lifelong/streaming settings. Classical probabilistic topic models, while more interpretable, are rigid and struggle with flexible adaptation to streaming data and changing topic structure. There is a need for a topic model that is incremental, can grow its capacity, organizes topics hierarchically, and leverages modern text representations without extensive supervision or hyperparameter tuning.
AI.method: The paper adapts the classical Cobweb incremental concept formation algorithm to modern continuous document embeddings, creating CobwebTM. Documents are first encoded as embeddings (e.g., via pretrained language models). CobwebTM then incrementally assigns each new document to nodes in a hierarchical tree, splitting, merging, or creating new nodes based on probabilistic criteria to maximize category utility. This online process builds and maintains a semantic hierarchy of topics without requiring a pre-specified number of topics. The model is low-parameter and designed for lifelong learning over streams of documents.
AI.result: Across multiple and diverse text datasets, CobwebTM produces topics with strong coherence, maintains topic stability over time in streaming scenarios, and yields meaningful hierarchical structures. Empirical comparisons suggest that it is competitive with or better than baselines in coherence and hierarchy quality, while also supporting online, dynamic topic creation.
AI.conclusion: Incremental symbolic concept formation, when combined with pretrained continuous document representations, provides an effective, efficient, and flexible approach to topic modeling. CobwebTM demonstrates that such an approach can support lifelong, online topic discovery and hierarchical organization without predefining topic numbers, while avoiding catastrophic forgetting and maintaining coherent and stable topics over time.

title: Disentangle-then-Refine: LLM-Guided Decoupling and Structure-Aware Refinement for Graph Contrastive Learning
summary: Conventional Graph Contrastive Learning (GCL) on Text-Attributed Graphs (TAGs) relies on blind stochastic augmentations, inadvertently entangling task-relevant signals with noise. We propose SDM-SCR, a robust framework anchored in Approximate Orthogonal Decomposition. First, the Semantic Decoupling Module (SDM) leverages the instruction-following capability of Large Language Models (LLMs) to actively parse raw attributes into asymmetric, task-oriented signal and noise views. This shifts the paradigm from random perturbation to semantic-aware disentanglement. Subsequently, Semantic Consistency Regularization (SCR) exploits the spectral observation that semantic signals are topologically smooth while residual noise is high-frequency. SCR functions as a selective spectral filter, enforcing consistency only on the signal subspace to eliminate LLM hallucinations without over-smoothing. This ``Disentangle-then-Refine'' mechanism ensures rigorous signal purification. Extensive experiments demonstrate that SDM-SCR achieves SOTA performance in accuracy and efficiency.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes SDM-SCR, a graph contrastive learning framework for text-attributed graphs that uses LLM-based semantic disentanglement and spectral regularization to better separate task-relevant signal from noise, achieving state-of-the-art performance.
AI.motivation: Existing Graph Contrastive Learning methods for text-attributed graphs rely on random, stochastic data augmentations. These "blind" augmentations can corrupt or mix task-relevant semantic information with noise, hurting representation quality and downstream performance. There is a need for a more principled, semantic-aware way to distinguish useful signal from irrelevant or noisy content, especially given the rich textual attributes and the availability of powerful LLMs.
AI.method: The authors introduce SDM-SCR, built on Approximate Orthogonal Decomposition. (1) Semantic Decoupling Module (SDM): uses the instruction-following ability of large language models to parse raw textual node attributes into two asymmetric views: a task-oriented signal view and a noise view. This replaces random perturbations with semantically guided disentanglement. (2) Semantic Consistency Regularization (SCR): based on the spectral insight that meaningful semantic signals tend to be smooth over the graph topology, while noise is high-frequency. SCR acts as a selective spectral filter that enforces consistency only in the signal subspace, which both removes LLM hallucinations and avoids the over-smoothing effect common in GNNs. Together these steps implement a "Disentangle-then-Refine" process for graph representations.
AI.result: Through extensive experiments on text-attributed graph benchmarks, SDM-SCR attains state-of-the-art performance in both accuracy and efficiency compared with existing GCL baselines. The framework more effectively preserves task-relevant semantics and better suppresses noise, indicating superior representation quality.
AI.conclusion: By combining LLM-driven semantic disentanglement with topology-aware spectral regularization, SDM-SCR addresses the limitations of random augmentations in graph contrastive learning on text-attributed graphs. The method provides a principled way to separate and refine signal versus noise, leading to robust, high-quality graph representations and SOTA empirical results.

title: PeerPrism: Peer Evaluation Expertise vs Review-writing AI
summary:
Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly used in scientific peer review, assisting with drafting, rewriting, expansion, and refinement. However, existing peer-review LLM detection methods largely treat authorship as a binary problem-human vs. AI-without accounting for the hybrid nature of modern review workflows. In practice, evaluative ideas and surface realization may originate from different sources, creating a spectrum of human-AI collaboration.
  In this work, we introduce PeerPrism, a large-scale benchmark of 20,690 peer reviews explicitly designed to disentangle idea provenance from text provenance. We construct controlled generation regimes spanning fully human, fully synthetic, and multiple hybrid transformations. This design enables systematic evaluation of whether detectors identify the origin of the surface text or the origin of the evaluative reasoning. We benchmark state-of-the-art LLM text detection methods on PeerPrism. While several methods achieve high accuracy on the standard binary task (human vs. fully synthetic), their predictions diverge sharply under hybrid regimes. In particular, when ideas originate from humans but the surface text is AI-generated, detectors frequently disagree and produce contradictory classifications. Accompanied by stylometric and semantic analyses, our results show that current detection methods conflate surface realization with intellectual contribution.
  Overall, we demonstrate that LLM detection in peer review cannot be reduced to a binary attribution problem. Instead, authorship must be modeled as a multidimensional construct spanning semantic reasoning and stylistic realization. PeerPrism is the first benchmark evaluating human-AI collaboration in these settings. We release all code, data, prompts, and evaluation scripts to facilitate reproducible research at https://github.com/Reviewerly-Inc/PeerPrism.

AI.tldr: PeerPrism is a benchmark of 20,690 peer reviews designed to distinguish between the origins of ideas and the text in human–AI co-written reviews, showing that current LLM detectors conflate style with intellectual contribution and fail in hybrid settings.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM detection methods in peer review assume a simple binary distinction between human- and AI-written text, which is misaligned with real-world workflows where humans and AI may collaborate, with ideas and surface text coming from different sources. There is a need to understand and evaluate how detectors perform in these hybrid, collaborative regimes.
AI.method: The authors build PeerPrism, a large-scale benchmark consisting of 20,690 peer reviews generated under controlled regimes: fully human, fully synthetic, and several hybrid transformations where idea provenance and text provenance are systematically varied. They then evaluate state-of-the-art LLM-based text detection methods on these regimes, and conduct stylometric and semantic analyses to examine what aspects of authorship the detectors are actually capturing.
AI.result: Detection methods that perform well on the standard binary task (human vs. fully synthetic) show sharply degraded and inconsistent behavior on hybrid regimes. When human ideas are expressed via AI-generated surface text, detectors disagree widely and often provide contradictory labels, indicating that they are primarily sensitive to stylistic markers of AI generation rather than the underlying evaluative reasoning or intellectual contribution.
AI.conclusion: LLM authorship detection in peer review cannot be treated as a simple binary attribution of entire reviews to either humans or AI. Authorship is inherently multidimensional, involving both semantic reasoning and stylistic realization. PeerPrism is introduced as the first benchmark explicitly targeting this human–AI collaboration space, and the authors release all associated resources to support further, more nuanced research on detection methods.

title: CoTEvol: Self-Evolving Chain-of-Thoughts for Data Synthesis in Mathematical Reasoning
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) exhibit strong mathematical reasoning when trained on high-quality Chain-of-Thought (CoT) that articulates intermediate steps, yet costly CoT curation hinders further progress. While existing remedies such as distillation from stronger LLMs and self-synthesis based on test-time search alleviate this issue, they often suffer from diminishing returns or high computing overhead.In this work, we propose CoTEvol, a genetic evolutionary framework that casts CoT generation as a population-based search over reasoning trajectories.Candidate trajectories are iteratively evolved through reflective global crossover at the trajectory level and local mutation guided by uncertainty at the step level, enabling holistic recombination and fine-grained refinement. Lightweight, task-aware fitness functions are designed to guide the evolutionary process toward accurate and diverse reasoning. Empirically, CoTEvol improves correct-CoT synthesis success by over 30% and enhances structural diversity, with markedly improved efficiency. LLMs trained on these evolutionary CoT data achieve an average gain of 6.6% across eight math benchmarks, outperforming previous distillation and self-synthesis approaches. These results underscore the promise of evolutionary CoT synthesis as a scalable and effective method for mathematical reasoning tasks.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces CoTEvol, a genetic evolutionary framework that automatically generates diverse and accurate Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning traces for training LLMs, significantly boosting math reasoning performance with lower cost than existing methods.
AI.motivation: High-quality Chain-of-Thought data greatly improves LLM mathematical reasoning, but manually curating or generating such data is expensive and existing automatic methods (distillation from stronger models, self-synthesis with search) either plateau in benefit or require heavy computation. The authors want a scalable, efficient way to synthesize large amounts of correct and diverse CoT traces.
AI.method: They cast CoT generation as a population-based genetic search over reasoning trajectories. Starting from candidate CoTs, the system iteratively applies: (1) trajectory-level crossover that reflectively recombines segments from different CoTs to create new global reasoning paths; (2) step-level mutation that locally edits uncertain or low-confidence steps to improve correctness; and (3) lightweight, task-aware fitness functions that evaluate accuracy and structural diversity and select better candidates. This evolutionary loop produces improved CoTs over generations with modest compute.
AI.result: The proposed CoTEvol framework increases the success rate of synthesizing correct CoTs by more than 30% compared with baselines, while also generating more structurally diverse reasoning traces and doing so more efficiently. When LLMs are trained on the CoT data produced by CoTEvol, they achieve an average performance improvement of 6.6% on eight different math benchmarks, surpassing prior distillation and self-synthesis approaches.
AI.conclusion: Evolutionary search over reasoning trajectories is an effective and scalable strategy to automatically synthesize high-quality CoT data for mathematical reasoning. By combining crossover, mutation, and task-aware fitness, CoTEvol can produce accurate, diverse, and efficient CoTs that translate into substantial performance gains for LLMs, indicating a promising direction for future CoT generation and reasoning enhancement techniques.

title: Mechanistic Decoding of Cognitive Constructs in LLMs
comment: This work has been submitted to the IEEE for possible publication
summary: While Large Language Models (LLMs) demonstrate increasingly sophisticated affective capabilities, the internal mechanisms by which they process complex emotions remain unclear. Existing interpretability approaches often treat models as black boxes or focus on coarse-grained basic emotions, leaving the cognitive structure of more complex affective states underexplored. To bridge this gap, we propose a Cognitive Reverse-Engineering framework based on Representation Engineering (RepE) to analyze social-comparison jealousy. By combining appraisal theory with subspace orthogonalization, regression-based weighting, and bidirectional causal steering, we isolate and quantify two psychological antecedents of jealousy, Superiority of Comparison Person and Domain Self-Definitional Relevance, and examine their causal effects on model judgments. Experiments on eight LLMs from the Llama, Qwen, and Gemma families suggest that models natively encode jealousy as a structured linear combination of these constituent factors. Their internal representations are broadly consistent with the human psychological construct, treating Superiority as the foundational trigger and Relevance as the ultimate intensity multiplier. Our framework also demonstrates that toxic emotional states can be mechanically detected and surgically suppressed, suggesting a possible route toward representational monitoring and intervention for AI safety in multi-agent environments.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a framework to reverse‑engineer how LLMs represent a complex emotion—social-comparison jealousy—showing it emerges from linearly combined psychological factors inside the models.
AI.motivation: Although LLMs exhibit sophisticated emotional behavior, we lack clarity about how they internally represent complex emotions, especially beyond basic categories like happiness or anger. Current interpretability work often treats models as black boxes or only inspects coarse-grained affect, missing the finer cognitive structure that psychology attributes to emotions such as jealousy. The authors aim to connect cognitive emotion theories with mechanistic interpretability, using jealousy as a case study, to see whether LLMs encode emotions in terms of human-like psychological components and to enable safer control over harmful emotional states in AI agents.
AI.method: The authors propose a Cognitive Reverse-Engineering framework built on Representation Engineering (RepE). They focus on social-comparison jealousy and draw on appraisal theory to identify two antecedent factors: (1) Superiority of the Comparison Person and (2) Domain Self-Definitional Relevance. They construct and orthogonalize representation subspaces corresponding to these factors, use regression-based weighting to quantify their contributions, and apply bidirectional causal steering to manipulate these components in hidden activations. Through these steps, they test how independently steering each factor changes the model’s jealousy-related judgments and outputs across eight different LLMs (Llama, Qwen, and Gemma families).
AI.result: Across all tested models, the authors find linear directions in representation space corresponding to the two psychological antecedents of jealousy. Manipulating these directions causally affects jealousy-related assessments in the expected ways: increases along the Superiority dimension reliably trigger jealousy, while modulation along the Relevance dimension scales its intensity. Regression analyses show that jealousy representations can be approximately reconstructed as a structured linear combination of these antecedents. The internal structure discovered in the models aligns well with human cognitive theories, and toxic or harmful jealousy states can be mechanically dampened or suppressed without broadly degrading other capabilities.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that LLMs do not merely simulate jealousy superficially but encode it as a compositional, human-like cognitive structure built from identifiable psychological components. Their proposed Cognitive Reverse-Engineering + RepE framework can isolate, quantify, and causally manipulate these components, revealing that Superiority is a primary trigger and Relevance an intensity multiplier for jealousy within the models. This offers a principled approach for mechanistically detecting and selectively suppressing toxic emotional states, suggesting a practical pathway toward representational monitoring and targeted intervention for AI safety in multi-agent and socially rich settings.

title: MirrorBench: Evaluating Self-centric Intelligence in MLLMs by Introducing a Mirror
summary: Recent progress in Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) has demonstrated remarkable advances in perception and reasoning, suggesting their potential for embodied intelligence. While recent studies have evaluated embodied MLLMs in interactive settings, current benchmarks mainly target capabilities to perceive, understand, and interact with external objects, lacking a systematic evaluation of self-centric intelligence. To address this, we introduce MirrorBench, a simulation-based benchmark inspired by the classical Mirror Self-Recognition (MSR) test in psychology. MirrorBench extends this paradigm to embodied MLLMs through a tiered framework of progressively challenging tasks, assessing agents from basic visual perception to high-level self-representation. Experiments on leading MLLMs show that even at the lowest level, their performance remains substantially inferior to human performance, revealing fundamental limitations in self-referential understanding. Our study bridges psychological paradigms and embodied intelligence, offering a principled framework for evaluating the emergence of general intelligence in large models. Project page: https://fflahm.github.io/mirror-bench-page/.

AI.tldr: MirrorBench is a simulation-based benchmark to evaluate self-centric intelligence in embodied multimodal large language models (MLLMs) via tasks inspired by the psychological mirror self-recognition test.
AI.motivation: Existing embodied MLLM benchmarks focus largely on perceiving and interacting with external objects but lack systematic evaluation of self-centric abilities such as self-perception and self-representation. The authors want to understand whether and how embodied MLLMs develop self-referential understanding, a key ingredient of more general intelligence, and to provide a principled way to measure it.
AI.method: They design MirrorBench, a simulation-based benchmark modeled after the classical Mirror Self-Recognition (MSR) test in psychology. MirrorBench consists of a tiered set of tasks of increasing difficulty, ranging from basic visual perception of an agent’s own body or reflection to more abstract reasoning about its own state and representation. Embodied MLLMs are deployed as agents in a simulated environment and evaluated on their performance across these tiers.
AI.result: When state-of-the-art MLLMs are evaluated on MirrorBench, their performance is far below human level, even on the simplest tasks, indicating that they struggle with self-centric perception and self-referential reasoning. This empirical gap highlights that current embodied MLLMs do not yet possess robust self-understanding capabilities.
AI.conclusion: MirrorBench provides a structured, psychology-inspired framework to evaluate self-centric and self-referential abilities in embodied MLLMs. The observed performance gaps show fundamental limitations in current models’ self-understanding, and the benchmark is proposed as a principled tool for tracking the emergence of more general intelligence in future large models, helping to connect cognitive science paradigms with embodied AI research.

title: NLP needs Diversity outside of 'Diversity'
summary: This position paper argues that recent progress with diversity in NLP is disproportionately concentrated on a small number of areas surrounding fairness. We further argue that this is the result of a number of incentives, biases, and barriers which come together to disenfranchise marginalized researchers in non-fairness fields, or to move them into fairness-related fields. We substantiate our claims with an investigation into the demographics of NLP researchers by subfield, using our research to support a number of recommendations for ensuring that all areas within NLP can become more inclusive and equitable. In particular, we highlight the importance of breaking down feedback loops that reinforce disparities, and the need to address geographical and linguistic barriers that hinder participation in NLP research.

AI.tldr: The paper argues that diversity efforts in NLP are overly focused on fairness, marginalizing other subfields and researchers, and offers demographic analysis and recommendations to make all NLP areas more inclusive.
AI.motivation: To challenge the assumption that diversity in NLP is being addressed evenly across the field and to show that current efforts, largely centered on fairness, unintentionally sideline marginalized researchers working in other areas.
AI.method: The authors present a position argument, then conduct an empirical investigation of the demographics of NLP researchers by subfield, examining who participates where and how factors like incentives, institutional biases, and structural barriers shape this distribution.
AI.result: They find that diversity-related attention and resources cluster heavily around fairness-related research, while other NLP subfields show lower participation from marginalized researchers, influenced by feedback loops, institutional incentives, and geographical and linguistic barriers.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that to achieve genuine equity across NLP, the community must break feedback loops that concentrate diversity work into fairness areas, and must actively reduce geographical and linguistic barriers, adopting policies and practices that support inclusive participation in all subfields.

title: CogEvolution: A Human-like Generative Educational Agent to Simulate Student's Cognitive Evolution
summary: Generative Agents, owing to their precise modeling and simulation capabilities of human behavior, have become a pivotal tool in the field of Artificial Intelligence in Education (AIEd) for uncovering complex cognitive processes of learners. However, existing educational agents predominantly rely on static personas to simulate student learning behaviors, neglecting the decisive role of deep cognitive capabilities in learning outcomes during practice interactions. Furthermore, they struggle to characterize the dynamic fluidity of knowledge internalization, transfer, and cognitive state transitions. To overcome this bottleneck, this paper proposes a human-like educational agent capable of simulating student cognitive evolution: CogEvolution. Specifically, we first construct a cognitive depth perceptron based on the Interactive, Constructive, Active, Passive (ICAP) taxonomy from cognitive psychology, achieving precise quantification of learner cognitive engagement. Subsequently, we propose a memory retrieval method based on Item Response Theory (IRT) to simulate the connection and assimilation of new and prior knowledge. Finally, we design a dynamic cognitive update mechanism based on evolutionary algorithms to simulate the real-time integration of student learning behaviors and cognitive evolution processes. Comprehensive evaluations demonstrate that CogEvolution not only significantly outperforms baseline models in behavioral fidelity and learning curve fitting but also uniquely reproduces plausible and robust cognitive evolutionary paths consistent with educational psychology expectations, providing a novel paradigm for constructing highly interpretable educational agents.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes CogEvolution, a human-like educational generative agent that models and simulates students’ dynamic cognitive evolution, achieving higher behavioral fidelity, better learning-curve fitting, and psychologically plausible cognitive trajectories.
AI.motivation: Existing generative educational agents mostly use static personas and cannot capture how learners’ cognition dynamically deepens, how knowledge is internalized and transferred, or how cognitive states transition during practice. This limits their ability to reveal complex cognitive processes and accurately simulate learning outcomes, despite generative agents becoming a key tool in AIEd.
AI.method: 1) Define a cognitive depth perceptron grounded in the ICAP (Interactive, Constructive, Active, Passive) taxonomy to quantitatively measure learners’ cognitive engagement; 2) Propose a memory retrieval mechanism using Item Response Theory (IRT) to model how new information connects with and assimilates prior knowledge; 3) Design a dynamic cognitive update mechanism via evolutionary algorithms to integrate observed learning behaviors with evolving cognitive states in real time within the agent.
AI.result: Experiments show that CogEvolution more closely matches real student behaviors than baseline agents, fits students’ learning curves more accurately, and is uniquely able to generate cognitively plausible, stable evolutionary paths that align with expectations from educational psychology.
AI.conclusion: CogEvolution demonstrates that embedding cognitive psychology (ICAP), psychometrics (IRT), and evolutionary computation into generative agents yields more interpretable, human-like educational agents capable of simulating learners’ cognitive evolution. This offers a new, psychologically grounded paradigm for future AIEd agent design.

title: CausalDetox: Causal Head Selection and Intervention for Language Model Detoxification
summary: Large language models (LLMs) frequently generate toxic content, posing significant risks for safe deployment. Current mitigation strategies often degrade generation quality or require costly human annotation. We propose CAUSALDETOX, a framework that identifies and intervenes on the specific attention heads causally responsible for toxic generation. Using the Probability of Necessity and Sufficiency (PNS), we isolate a minimal set of heads that are necessary and sufficient for toxicity. We utilize these components via two complementary strategies: (1) Local Inference-Time Intervention, which constructs dynamic, input-specific steering vectors for context-aware detoxification, and (2) PNS-Guided Fine-Tuning, which permanently unlearns toxic representations. We also introduce PARATOX, a novel benchmark of aligned toxic/non-toxic sentence pairs enabling controlled counterfactual evaluation. Experiments on ToxiGen, ImplicitHate, and ParaDetox show that CAUSALDETOX achieves up to 5.34% greater toxicity reduction compared to baselines while preserving linguistic fluency, and offers a 7x speedup in head selection.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces CAUSALDETOX, a causal-analysis-based framework that pinpoints and intervenes on specific attention heads responsible for toxic outputs in LLMs, reducing toxicity with minimal quality loss and less computation.
AI.motivation: Large language models can produce toxic text, which is dangerous for real-world use. Existing detoxification methods often hurt generation quality, require many human-labeled examples, or are computationally expensive. The authors are motivated to find a more targeted, causally grounded way to suppress toxicity by intervening only on the model components that actually cause toxic behavior, thereby improving safety while preserving fluency and efficiency.
AI.method: The authors introduce CAUSALDETOX, which uses causal inference—specifically, the Probability of Necessity and Sufficiency (PNS)—to identify a minimal subset of attention heads that are both necessary and sufficient for generating toxicity. After finding these heads, they propose two strategies: (1) Local Inference-Time Intervention, which creates input-specific steering vectors to adjust these heads’ activations during generation for context-aware detoxification, and (2) PNS-Guided Fine-Tuning, which fine-tunes the model so that these heads unlearn toxic representations more permanently. They also design PARATOX, a benchmark comprising aligned toxic/non-toxic paraphrase pairs, enabling counterfactual-style evaluation of interventions.
AI.result: On several toxicity-related benchmarks (ToxiGen, ImplicitHate, ParaDetox), CAUSALDETOX outperforms baseline detoxification methods by reducing toxicity scores by up to 5.34% more while maintaining linguistic quality. Additionally, their causal head-selection procedure is about 7 times faster than previous methods that search for influential heads more exhaustively or heuristically.
AI.conclusion: Targeted, causally motivated interventions on specific attention heads can effectively reduce toxic generations in LLMs without substantially harming fluency. The PNS-based framework enables efficient identification of toxicity-related components, and both inference-time steering and PNS-guided fine-tuning offer practical, complementary ways to detoxify models. The PARATOX benchmark further supports accurate evaluation of causal detoxification methods using aligned toxic and non-toxic counterfactuals.

title: Sequence Search: Automated Sequence Design using Neural Architecture Search
summary: Developing an MR sequence is challenging and remains largely constrained by human intuition. Recently, AI-driven approaches have been proposed; however, most require an initial sequence for parameter optimization or extensive training datasets, limiting their general applicability. In this study, we propose "Sequence Search," an automated sequence design framework based on neural architecture search. The method takes tissue properties, imaging parameters, and design objectives as inputs and generates pulse sequences satisfying the design objectives, without requiring prior knowledge of conventional sequence structures. Sequence Search iteratively generates candidate sequences through neural architecture search and optimizes them via a differentiable Bloch simulator and objective-specific loss functions using gradient-based learning. The framework successfully replicated conventional spin-echo, T2-weighted spin-echo, and inversion recovery sequences. Less intuitive solutions were also discovered, such as three-RF spin-echo-like sequences with reduced RF energy and refocusing phases deviating from the conventional Hahn-echo. This work establishes a generalizable framework for automated MR sequence design, highlighting the potential to explore configurations beyond conventional designs based on human intuition.

AI.tldr: They introduce an AI-based framework (“Sequence Search”) that automatically designs MR pulse sequences from basic inputs, reproducing standard sequences and finding new, less intuitive but efficient ones.
AI.motivation: Designing MRI pulse sequences is complex, traditionally driven by expert intuition and trial-and-error. Existing AI approaches still need either a starting sequence or large training data, limiting how broadly they can be applied and how far they can deviate from known designs. The authors want a general, automated way to discover pulse sequences directly from imaging goals and tissue properties, without relying on prior human-designed sequence templates.
AI.method: They formulate MR sequence design as a neural architecture search (NAS) problem. The framework takes tissue properties, scan parameters, and desired imaging objectives as input, and uses NAS to iteratively propose candidate pulse sequence “architectures.” Each candidate is evaluated and optimized with a differentiable Bloch simulator, enabling gradient-based learning with objective-specific loss functions. This pipeline jointly searches over sequence structure and optimizes its continuous parameters without needing prior conventional sequence structures as templates.
AI.result: The system was able to automatically discover MR pulse sequences that effectively reproduce known clinical sequences such as spin-echo, T2-weighted spin-echo, and inversion recovery. Additionally, it found unconventional but effective solutions, including three-RF spin-echo-like sequences that deliver similar contrast while reducing RF energy and using nonstandard refocusing phases that differ from the classic Hahn-echo configuration.
AI.conclusion: The proposed “Sequence Search” framework demonstrates that neural architecture search combined with differentiable MR physics can automatically generate both standard and novel MR pulse sequences from high-level imaging goals. This suggests MR sequence design can be made more generalizable and less reliant on human intuition, enabling exploration of new sequence configurations that may be more efficient or better tailored to specific objectives than conventional designs.

title: Retrieve, Then Classify: Corpus-Grounded Automation of Clinical Value Set Authoring
summary: Clinical value set authoring -- the task of identifying all codes in a standardized vocabulary that define a clinical concept -- is a recurring bottleneck in clinical quality measurement and phenotyping. A natural approach is to prompt a large language model (LLM) to generate the required codes directly, but structured clinical vocabularies are large, version-controlled, and not reliably memorized during pretraining. We propose Retrieval-Augmented Set Completion (RASC): retrieve the $K$ most similar existing value sets from a curated corpus to form a candidate pool, then apply a classifier to each candidate code. Theoretically, retrieve-and-select can reduce statistical complexity by shrinking the effective output space from the full vocabulary to a much smaller retrieved candidate pool. We demonstrate the utility of RASC on 11,803 publicly available VSAC value sets, constructing the first large-scale benchmark for this task. A cross-encoder fine-tuned on SAPBert achieves AUROC~0.852 and value-set-level F1~0.298, outperforming a simpler three-layer Multilayer Perceptron (AUROC~0.799, F1~0.250) and both reduce the number of irrelevant candidates per true positive from 12.3 (retrieval-only) to approximately 3.2 and 4.4 respectively. Zero-shot GPT-4o achieves value-set-level F1~0.105, with 48.6\% of returned codes absent from VSAC entirely. This performance gap widens with increasing value set size, consistent with RASC's theoretical advantage. We observe similar performance gains across two other classifier model types, namely a cross-encoder initialized from pre-trained SAPBert and a LightGBM model, demonstrating that RASC's benefits extend beyond a single model class. The code to download and create the benchmark dataset, as well as the model training code is available at: \href{https://github.com/mukhes3/RASC}{https://github.com/mukhes3/RASC}.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Retrieval-Augmented Set Completion (RASC), a method that improves automatic construction of clinical value sets by first retrieving similar existing value sets and then classifying candidate codes, outperforming direct LLM generation and simpler models.
AI.motivation: Authoring clinical value sets (selecting all codes that define a clinical concept in standardized vocabularies) is labor-intensive and a bottleneck for tasks like clinical quality measurement and phenotyping. Directly asking LLMs to output codes performs poorly because vocabularies are huge, versioned, and not well memorized. The authors want a scalable, accurate way to automate or accelerate this process while dealing with the large output space of possible codes.
AI.method: They propose Retrieval-Augmented Set Completion (RASC). First, for a target concept, they retrieve the K most similar value sets from a large curated corpus (VSAC) based on semantic similarity. These retrieved sets form a candidate pool of codes. Then they treat inclusion of each candidate code as a classification problem, training models (e.g., a SAPBert-based cross-encoder and a LightGBM model) to decide whether each candidate belongs in the new value set. This reduces the effective search space from the full vocabulary to the retrieved candidate pool. They also construct a large-scale benchmark using 11,803 VSAC value sets and compare against baselines, including a simple MLP and zero-shot GPT-4o code generation.
AI.result: On the 11,803 VSAC value sets, the SAPBert-based cross-encoder with RASC achieves AUROC 0.852 and value-set-level F1 0.298, while a simpler 3-layer MLP achieves AUROC 0.799 and F1 0.250. Both RASC models greatly cut down irrelevant candidates per true positive, from 12.3 with retrieval-only to about 3.2 (cross-encoder) and 4.4 (MLP). Zero-shot GPT-4o performs much worse with value-set-level F1 0.105 and nearly half (48.6%) of its returned codes not appearing in VSAC. Performance benefits of RASC become more pronounced for larger value sets and are replicated with multiple classifier architectures, including a LightGBM model.
AI.conclusion: RASC, a retrieve-then-classify framework, is an effective and theoretically motivated approach to clinical value set authoring, substantially outperforming direct LLM generation and simpler baselines, especially as value set size grows. By shrinking the effective output space to a retrieved candidate pool, RASC improves both accuracy and precision in code selection across several classifier types. The authors release code and data to support further research and benchmarking in automated value set construction.

title: A Comparative Study of CNN Optimization Methods for Edge AI: Exploring the Role of Early Exits
summary: Deploying deep neural networks on edge devices requires balancing accuracy, latency, and resource constraints under realistic execution conditions. To fit models within these constraints, two broad strategies have emerged: static compression techniques such as pruning and quantization, which permanently reduce model size, and dynamic approaches such as early-exit mechanisms, which adapt computational cost at runtime. While both families are widely studied in isolation, they are rarely compared under identical conditions on physical hardware. This paper presents a unified deployment-oriented comparison of static compression and dynamic early-exit mechanisms, evaluated on real edge devices using ONNX based inference pipelines. Our results show that static and dynamic techniques offer fundamentally different trade-offs for edge deployment. While pruning and quantization deliver consistent memory footprint reduction, early-exit mechanisms enable input-adaptive computation savings that static methods cannot match. Their combination proves highly effective, simultaneously reducing inference latency and memory usage with minimal accuracy loss, expanding what is achievable at the edge.

AI.tldr: The paper compares static compression (pruning, quantization) and dynamic early-exit mechanisms for deploying deep neural networks on real edge devices, showing they provide complementary trade-offs and work best in combination.
AI.motivation: Edge devices have strict accuracy, latency, and resource constraints, making it challenging to deploy deep neural networks. Existing works typically study static compression or dynamic early-exit methods separately and often in simulation rather than on real hardware, leaving a gap in understanding their relative benefits and how they interact under realistic deployment conditions.
AI.method: The authors build ONNX-based inference pipelines and deploy various statically compressed models (via pruning and quantization) and dynamically adaptive models (via early-exit mechanisms) on real edge devices. They then empirically measure and compare accuracy, latency, and memory usage under unified, deployment-oriented conditions, and also evaluate combinations of static and dynamic methods.
AI.result: Static compression techniques reliably shrink the memory footprint and model size but offer fixed computational cost per input. Dynamic early-exit methods introduce input-dependent computation, saving compute and latency on many inputs in ways static methods cannot. When combined, they simultaneously reduce inference latency and memory usage while keeping accuracy degradation small.
AI.conclusion: Static and dynamic techniques address different aspects of the edge deployment challenge: pruning and quantization give predictable, consistent resource savings, while early-exit mechanisms enable flexible, input-adaptive computation. A hybrid approach that combines both yields superior edge deployment trade-offs, expanding the feasible operating region for neural networks on constrained devices.

title: StoryCoder: Narrative Reformulation for Structured Reasoning in LLM Code Generation
summary: Effective code generation requires both model capability and a problem representation that carefully structures how models reason and plan. Existing approaches augment reasoning steps or inject specific structure into how models think, but leave scattered problem conditions unchanged. Inspired by the way humans organize fragmented information into coherent explanations, we propose StoryCoder, a narrative reformulation framework that transforms code generation questions into coherent natural language narratives, providing richer contextual structure than simple rephrasings. Each narrative consists of three components: a task overview, constraints, and example test cases, guided by the selected algorithm and genre. Experiments across 11 models on HumanEval, LiveCodeBench, and CodeForces demonstrate consistent improvements, with an average gain of 18.7% in zero-shot pass@10. Beyond accuracy, our analyses reveal that narrative reformulation guides models toward correct algorithmic strategies, reduces implementation errors, and induces a more modular code structure. The analyses further show that these benefits depend on narrative coherence and genre alignment, suggesting that structured problem representation is important for code generation regardless of model scale or architecture. Our code is available at https://github.com/gu-ni/StoryCoder.

AI.tldr: StoryCoder improves code generation by reformulating coding problems into coherent narrative descriptions with task overview, constraints, and test-like examples, boosting performance across many models and benchmarks.
AI.motivation: While large models can generate code, their performance depends heavily on how problems are presented. Existing methods add reasoning steps or partial structure but leave problem statements fragmented, which can hinder systematic planning and algorithm selection. The authors aim to design a more coherent problem representation that better guides model reasoning, akin to how humans turn scattered requirements into a unified mental model.
AI.method: They introduce StoryCoder, a narrative reformulation framework that automatically rewrites code-generation questions into structured natural language narratives. Each narrative has three parts: (1) a high-level task overview, (2) explicit constraints, and (3) illustrative example test cases. Narratives are further guided by a chosen algorithm and a narrative ‘genre’ to shape the explanation style. They evaluate StoryCoder across 11 different models on three benchmarks (HumanEval, LiveCodeBench, CodeForces), and perform quantitative and qualitative analyses of accuracy, algorithm choice, error patterns, and code structure. They also study how narrative coherence and alignment of genre with the task affect results.
AI.result: Across all 11 models and three benchmarks, StoryCoder yields consistent gains, with an average improvement of 18.7 percentage points in zero-shot pass@10 compared with baseline problem statements. Qualitative analyses indicate that models using narrative reformulations are more likely to pick correct algorithms, make fewer implementation mistakes, and produce more modular, well-structured code. The benefits are not tied to a particular model size or architecture but depend on how coherent and well-aligned the narrative is with the underlying algorithmic task.
AI.conclusion: Narrative-based, structured reformulation of coding problems substantially improves LLM code generation, beyond what can be achieved by simply adding reasoning steps. Organizing tasks into coherent stories with clear overviews, constraints, and examples helps models choose better algorithms and implement them more reliably. The effectiveness of this approach hinges on narrative coherence and genre-task alignment, suggesting that careful problem representation is a key lever for improving code generation performance across diverse models.

title: Diffusion Crossover: Defining Evolutionary Recombination in Diffusion Models via Noise Sequence Interpolation
summary: Interactive Evolutionary Computation (IEC) provides a powerful framework for optimizing subjective criteria such as human preferences and aesthetics, yet it suffers from a fundamental limitation: in high-dimensional generative representations, defining crossover in a semantically consistent manner is difficult, often leading to a mutation-dominated search. In this work, we explicitly define crossover in diffusion models. We propose Diffusion crossover, which formulates evolutionary recombination as step-wise interpolation of noise sequences in the reverse process of Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Models (DDPMs). By applying spherical linear interpolation (Slerp) to the noise sequences associated with selected parent images, the proposed method generates offspring that inherit characteristics from both parents while preserving the geometric structure of the diffusion process. Furthermore, controlling the time-step range of interpolation enables a principled trade-off between diversity (exploration) and convergence (exploitation). Experimental results using PCA analysis and perceptual similarity metrics (LPIPS) demonstrate that Diffusion crossover produces perceptually smooth and semantically consistent transitions between parent images. Qualitative interactive evolution experiments further confirm that the proposed method effectively supports human-in-the-loop image exploration. These findings suggest a new perspective: diffusion models are not only powerful generators, but also structured evolutionary search spaces in which recombination can be explicitly defined and controlled.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Diffusion crossover, a method to perform explicit, semantically meaningful crossover within diffusion models for interactive evolutionary image generation.
AI.motivation: Interactive Evolutionary Computation is good for optimizing subjective criteria (e.g., aesthetics, user preference) but struggles with high-dimensional generative representations: defining a meaningful crossover operator is hard, so evolution ends up relying mostly on mutation, which limits efficient search and combination of desirable traits from multiple parents.
AI.method: The authors work with Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Models and define crossover as an operation over their noise sequences during the reverse diffusion process. They perform step-wise spherical linear interpolation (Slerp) between the noise sequences of selected parent images at chosen timesteps. By controlling which timestep range is interpolated, they manage how much the offspring inherits from each parent and tune the trade-off between exploration (diverse offspring) and exploitation (closer to parents). They then evaluate results quantitatively via PCA trajectory analysis and LPIPS perceptual similarity, and qualitatively via interactive evolution sessions with human users.
AI.result: Diffusion crossover yields offspring images that form smooth, perceptually meaningful transitions between parents and preserve semantic content from both. PCA analysis shows structured transitions in the latent/noise space, and LPIPS scores validate perceptual smoothness and consistency. In user-facing interactive evolution experiments, participants can effectively explore image spaces by recombining parents using the proposed operator.
AI.conclusion: Diffusion models can be treated not only as generative models but also as structured search spaces where evolutionary operators like crossover can be rigorously defined. The proposed Diffusion crossover provides a principled, controllable recombination mechanism that addresses the traditional weakness of IEC in high-dimensional spaces, improving human-in-the-loop image exploration and suggesting broader opportunities for evolutionary search within diffusion frameworks.

title: Pushing the Boundaries of Multiple Choice Evaluation to One Hundred Options
summary: Multiple choice evaluation is widely used for benchmarking large language models, yet near ceiling accuracy in low option settings can be sustained by shortcut strategies that obscure true competence. Therefore, we propose a massive option evaluation protocol that scales the candidate set to one hundred options and sharply reduces the impact of chance performance. We apply this framework to a Korean orthography error detection task where models must pick the single incorrect sentence from a large candidate set. With fixed targets and repeated resampling and shuffling, we obtain stable estimates while separating content driven failures from positional artifacts. Across experiments, results indicate that strong performance in low option settings can overstate model competence. This apparent advantage often weakens under dense interference at high $N$, revealing gaps that conventional benchmarks tend to obscure. We identify two failure modes, semantic confusion and position bias toward early options under uncertainty. To isolate the effect of context length, we run padding controlled and length matched tests, which suggest that the main bottleneck is candidate ranking rather than context length. Together, these findings support massive option evaluation as a general framework for stress testing model reliability under extreme distractor density, beyond what low option benchmarks can reveal.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes and validates a ‘massive option’ multiple‑choice evaluation protocol (100 options) that exposes hidden weaknesses in language models that look strong on standard low-option benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Conventional multiple choice benchmarks often have few answer options, so high accuracy can be inflated by guessing, shortcuts, or positional biases. As models approach ceiling performance, these tests fail to distinguish true understanding from superficial strategies, making it hard to diagnose weaknesses and overstate real-world reliability.
AI.method: The authors design an evaluation protocol that scales multiple-choice candidate sets up to 100 options. They instantiate it on a Korean orthography error detection task where the model must choose the single incorrect sentence among many. They fix targets, repeatedly resample and shuffle the other options, and then measure accuracy and choice distributions to separate content-related errors from positional artifacts. They also conduct experiments to control for context length by padding and length-matching, and analyze failure patterns like semantic confusion and position bias.
AI.result: Under the 100-option setting, models that perform strongly in standard low-option settings show substantially degraded performance. The dense presence of distractor options reveals gaps in competence that standard benchmarks miss. Two clear failure modes emerge: (1) semantic confusion, where the model struggles to distinguish fine-grained differences between many similar candidates, and (2) a bias toward selecting early options when uncertain. Context-length-controlled experiments indicate that these degradations are mainly due to difficulties in ranking many candidates, not simply to long input sequences.
AI.conclusion: Massive option evaluation with large candidate sets is an effective stress test for language model reliability. It reduces the influence of chance, uncovers hidden weaknesses such as semantic confusion and positional bias, and shows that high scores on low-option benchmarks can exaggerate true competence. The framework is proposed as a general methodology for more rigorous, high-interference evaluation of language models beyond conventional multiple-choice tests.

title: The LLM Fallacy: Misattribution in AI-Assisted Cognitive Workflows
summary: The rapid integration of large language models (LLMs) into everyday workflows has transformed how individuals perform cognitive tasks such as writing, programming, analysis, and multilingual communication. While prior research has focused on model reliability, hallucination, and user trust calibration, less attention has been given to how LLM usage reshapes users' perceptions of their own capabilities. This paper introduces the LLM fallacy, a cognitive attribution error in which individuals misinterpret LLM-assisted outputs as evidence of their own independent competence, producing a systematic divergence between perceived and actual capability. We argue that the opacity, fluency, and low-friction interaction patterns of LLMs obscure the boundary between human and machine contribution, leading users to infer competence from outputs rather than from the processes that generate them. We situate the LLM fallacy within existing literature on automation bias, cognitive offloading, and human--AI collaboration, while distinguishing it as a form of attributional distortion specific to AI-mediated workflows. We propose a conceptual framework of its underlying mechanisms and a typology of manifestations across computational, linguistic, analytical, and creative domains. Finally, we examine implications for education, hiring, and AI literacy, and outline directions for empirical validation. We also provide a transparent account of human--AI collaborative methodology. This work establishes a foundation for understanding how generative AI systems not only augment cognitive performance but also reshape self-perception and perceived expertise.

AI.tldr: The paper defines the “LLM fallacy,” where people mistakenly see LLM-assisted work as proof of their own skill, leading to an inflated sense of personal capability.
AI.motivation: LLMs are widely used for complex cognitive tasks, but existing work mainly studies reliability, hallucinations, and trust. Much less is known about how using LLMs changes users’ beliefs about their own abilities. The authors want to explain and theorize this misperception because it has important consequences for learning, assessment, and expertise development.
AI.method: The paper is primarily conceptual and theoretical. It introduces and defines the LLM fallacy as a specific attribution error, positions it within related literatures (automation bias, cognitive offloading, human–AI collaboration), proposes a conceptual framework of mechanisms, and offers a typology of how the fallacy appears in different task domains. It also describes the human–AI collaborative process used to write the paper and suggests future empirical research directions.
AI.result: The authors articulate a clear definition of the LLM fallacy, differentiate it from related concepts, and develop a structured framework and typology of its manifestations in computational, linguistic, analytical, and creative tasks. They also derive implications for areas such as education, hiring, and AI literacy and specify avenues for empirical validation.
AI.conclusion: LLMs do more than boost performance; they reshape how people perceive their own expertise. Because the fluency and opacity of LLMs blur human vs. machine contributions, users may systematically over-attribute successful outputs to themselves. Recognizing and studying the LLM fallacy is therefore essential for designing educational practices, evaluation processes, and AI literacy interventions that keep self-assessment aligned with actual capability.

title: Fact4ac at the Financial Misinformation Detection Challenge Task: Reference-Free Financial Misinformation Detection via Fine-Tuning and Few-Shot Prompting of Large Language Models
summary: The proliferation of financial misinformation poses a severe threat to market stability and investor trust, misleading market behavior and creating critical information asymmetry. Detecting such misleading narratives is inherently challenging, particularly in real-world scenarios where external evidence or supplementary references for cross-verification are strictly unavailable. This paper presents our winning methodology for the "Reference-Free Financial Misinformation Detection" shared task. Built upon the recently proposed RFC-BENCH framework (Jiang et al. 2026), this task challenges models to determine the veracity of financial claims by relying solely on internal semantic understanding and contextual consistency, rather than external fact-checking. To address this formidable evaluation setup, we propose a comprehensive framework that capitalizes on the reasoning capabilities of state-of-the-art Large Language Models (LLMs). Our approach systematically integrates in-context learning, specifically zero-shot and few-shot prompting strategies, with Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT) via Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) to optimally align the models with the subtle linguistic cues of financial manipulation. Our proposed system demonstrated superior efficacy, successfully securing the first-place ranking on both official leaderboards. Specifically, we achieved an accuracy of 95.4% on the public test set and 96.3% on the private test set, highlighting the robustness of our method and contributing to the acceleration of context-aware misinformation detection in financial Natural Language Processing. Our models (14B and 32B) are available at https://huggingface.co/KaiNKaiho.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a winning system for detecting financial misinformation without using external references, relying only on the text itself.
AI.motivation: Financial misinformation endangers market stability and investor trust, yet most detection methods depend on external evidence, which is often unavailable in real-world, real-time settings. The authors aim to build a system that can judge the truthfulness of financial claims solely from their internal semantics and context, as required by the RFC-BENCH benchmark and the associated shared task.
AI.method: They design a framework centered on large language models, combining in-context learning (zero-shot and few-shot prompting) with parameter-efficient fine-tuning using LoRA (a PEFT method). The models are trained and prompted to capture subtle linguistic indicators of financial manipulation and to reason about semantic coherence and contextual consistency without external fact-checking. They build systems based on 14B and 32B parameter models and align them specifically to the RFC-BENCH shared-task setting.
AI.result: Their approach achieves first place on both public and private leaderboards of the "Reference-Free Financial Misinformation Detection" shared task, with 95.4% accuracy on the public test set and 96.3% on the private test set, outperforming competing systems.
AI.conclusion: A carefully designed combination of LLM-based reasoning, in-context prompting, and LoRA-based PEFT can robustly detect financial misinformation without any external references, reaching state-of-the-art performance on RFC-BENCH. This supports the feasibility and effectiveness of context-aware, reference-free misinformation detection in financial NLP, and the authors release their 14B and 32B models for the community.

title: Beyond Literal Summarization: Redefining Hallucination for Medical SOAP Note Evaluation
summary:
Evaluating large language models (LLMs) for clinical documentation tasks such as SOAP note generation remains challenging. Unlike standard summarization, these tasks require clinical abstraction, normalization of colloquial language, and medically grounded inference. However, prevailing evaluation methods including automated metrics and LLM as judge frameworks rely on lexical faithfulness, often labeling any information not explicitly present in the transcript as hallucination.
  We show that such approaches systematically misclassify clinically valid outputs as errors, inflating hallucination rates and distorting model assessment. Our analysis reveals that many flagged hallucinations correspond to legitimate clinical transformations, including synonym mapping, abstraction of examination findings, diagnostic inference, and guideline consistent care planning.
  By aligning evaluation criteria with clinical reasoning through calibrated prompting and retrieval grounded in medical ontologies we observe a significant shift in outcomes. Under a lexical evaluation regime, the mean hallucination rate is 35%, heavily penalizing valid reasoning. With inference aware evaluation, this drops to 9%, with remaining cases reflecting genuine safety concerns. These findings suggest that current evaluation practices over penalize valid clinical reasoning and may measure artifacts of evaluation design rather than true errors, underscoring the need for clinically informed evaluation in high context domains like medicine.

AI.tldr: The paper argues that standard automatic hallucination metrics for clinical LLM note-generation are misleading because they treat clinically reasonable inferences and abstractions as errors, and proposes an inference-aware, clinically grounded evaluation that dramatically lowers apparent hallucination rates.
AI.motivation: Current ways of evaluating LLMs on clinical documentation, especially SOAP note generation, mostly check lexical overlap with source transcripts and label any additional or transformed content as hallucination. But clinical documentation inherently requires abstraction, normalization of lay language, and medically justified inference. This mismatch likely misrepresents model performance and safety, so there is a need to understand and fix the bias in existing evaluation practices.
AI.method: The authors analyze hallucinations detected by prevailing evaluation approaches such as automatic metrics and LLM-as-a-judge frameworks when applied to clinical SOAP-note style generation from transcripts. They categorize flagged hallucinations and identify which are actually valid clinical transformations (e.g., synonym mapping, abstraction of findings, diagnostic inference, guideline-consistent planning). They then design an inference-aware evaluation protocol by calibrating prompts and incorporating retrieval grounded in medical ontologies to better reflect clinical reasoning rather than surface lexical faithfulness, and compare hallucination rates under both regimes.
AI.result: Under standard lexical evaluation, the mean hallucination rate is measured at 35%, with many instances being clinically reasonable inferences or abstractions. With the proposed inference-aware evaluation that aligns criteria with clinical reasoning and uses ontology-grounded retrieval plus calibrated prompting, the measured hallucination rate drops to 9%, and the remaining hallucinations better correspond to real safety-relevant errors rather than benign transformations.
AI.conclusion: Lexical-overlap-based hallucination assessments substantially overestimate error rates for clinical LLMs by misclassifying valid clinical reasoning and abstraction as hallucinations. Inference-aware, clinically grounded evaluation methods yield more accurate, lower hallucination estimates and highlight genuinely unsafe behavior. Evaluation protocols in high-context domains like medicine should therefore be redesigned to incorporate clinical reasoning and domain ontologies instead of relying primarily on lexical faithfulness.

title: CURaTE: Continual Unlearning in Real Time with Ensured Preservation of LLM Knowledge
summary: The inability to filter out in advance all potentially problematic data from the pre-training of large language models has given rise to the need for methods for unlearning specific pieces of knowledge after training. Existing techniques overlook the need for continuous and immediate action, causing them to suffer from degraded utility as updates accumulate and protracted exposure of sensitive information. To address these issues, we propose Continual Unlearning in Real Time with Ensured Preservation of LLM Knowledge (CURaTE). Our method begins by training a sentence embedding model on a dataset designed to enable the formation of sharp decision boundaries for determining whether a given input prompt corresponds to any stored forget requests. The similarity of a given input to the forget requests is then used to determine whether to answer or return a refusal response. We show that even with such a simple approach, not only does CURaTE achieve more effective forgetting than existing methods, but by avoiding modification of the language model parameters, it also maintains near perfect knowledge preservation over any number of updates and is the only method capable of continual unlearning in real-time.

AI.tldr: CURaTE is a continual, real-time unlearning framework for LLMs that uses a separate sentence-embedding gate to block responses matching stored forget-requests, achieving strong forgetting without modifying LLM parameters.
AI.motivation: LLMs inevitably memorize some problematic or sensitive data during pre-training, and it is infeasible to perfectly filter such data in advance. Existing unlearning methods typically rely on retraining or fine-tuning the model, which is slow, cannot react immediately to new deletion requests, degrades model utility as more unlearning steps accumulate, and can still leave windows where sensitive information is exposed. There is a need for an approach that can perform targeted unlearning continuously and in real time, while preserving the rest of the model’s knowledge and functionality.
AI.method: The authors propose CURaTE, a framework that decouples unlearning from the LLM’s parameters. They first train a sentence embedding model on a curated dataset built to create sharp decision boundaries between prompts that should be forgotten and those that are allowed. For any incoming user prompt, CURaTE encodes it and compares its embedding to the set of stored embeddings corresponding to forget requests (e.g., sensitive facts, specific texts). If the similarity exceeds a learned threshold, the system refuses to answer; otherwise, it forwards the prompt to the base LLM, which remains unchanged. New forget requests are incorporated by embedding and storing them, enabling continual updates without re-training the LLM.
AI.result: Experiments show that CURaTE forgets targeted information more effectively than prior unlearning approaches while preserving the LLM’s performance on non-targeted tasks. Because the LLM parameters are never modified, CURaTE avoids cumulative performance degradation observed in retraining-based unlearning as more forget requests are added. The method supports immediate addition of new forget items and robustly blocks responses related to them, demonstrating real-time continual unlearning behavior.
AI.conclusion: CURaTE demonstrates that a simple, embedding-based gating mechanism can provide strong, continual unlearning capabilities for LLMs without any parameter updates to the base model. This design yields better forgetting, near-perfect preservation of general knowledge, and scalability to an arbitrary number of forget requests, addressing practical needs for rapid, repeated content removal after deployment. The work suggests that future unlearning systems may benefit from similar modular, non-invasive architectures rather than repeated fine-tuning of the core LLM.

title: Intermediate Layers Encode Optimal Biological Representations in Single-Cell Foundation Models
comment: 9 pages, 2 figures, 4 tables. Accepted at the LMRL (Learning Meaningful Representations of Life) Workshop at ICLR 2026
summary: Current single-cell foundation model benchmarks universally extract final layer embeddings, assuming these represent optimal feature spaces. We systematically evaluate layer-wise representations from scFoundation (100M parameters) and Tahoe-X1 (1.3B parameters) across trajectory inference and perturbation response prediction. Our analysis reveals that optimal layers are task-dependent (trajectory peaks at 60% depth, 31% above final layers) and context-dependent (perturbation optima shift 0-96% across T cell activation states). Notably, first-layer embeddings outperform all deeper layers in quiescent cells, challenging assumptions about hierarchical feature abstraction. These findings demonstrate that "where" to extract features matters as much as "what" the model learns, necessitating systematic layer evaluation tailored to biological task and cellular context rather than defaulting to final-layer embeddings.

AI.tldr: They show that in single-cell foundation models, the best layer to extract embeddings is not always the final layer and strongly depends on task and cellular context.
AI.motivation: Most benchmarks for single-cell foundation models rely on embeddings from the final layer, assuming it provides the most informative representation. However, this assumption has not been rigorously tested across tasks or cellular states, and may hide performance gains available from intermediate layers.
AI.method: They systematically probe representations from all layers of two large single-cell foundation models (scFoundation with 100M parameters and Tahoe-X1 with 1.3B parameters). For each layer, they evaluate downstream performance on trajectory inference and perturbation response prediction tasks, analyzing how the optimal layer changes with task type and T cell activation state.
AI.result: They find that the best-performing layer varies. For trajectory inference, optimal performance occurs around 60% of model depth and yields a 31% performance improvement over using the final layer. For perturbation response prediction, the optimal layer shifts dramatically (0–96% depth) depending on T cell activation state. Surprisingly, in quiescent cells, the first layer embeddings outperform all deeper layers.
AI.conclusion: The layer at which features are extracted is as important as the model architecture itself. Final-layer embeddings are not universally optimal; instead, layer choice must be tailored to the specific biological task and cellular context. Systematic layer-wise evaluation should become standard practice for using single-cell foundation models.

title: CURA: Clinical Uncertainty Risk Alignment for Language Model-Based Risk Prediction
summary: Clinical language models (LMs) are increasingly applied to support clinical risk prediction from free-text notes, yet their uncertainty estimates often remain poorly calibrated and clinically unreliable. In this work, we propose Clinical Uncertainty Risk Alignment (CURA), a framework that aligns clinical LM-based risk estimates and uncertainty with both individual error likelihoods and cohort-level ambiguities. CURA first fine-tunes domain-specific clinical LMs to obtain task-adapted patient embeddings, and then performs uncertainty fine-tuning of a multi-head classifier using a bi-level uncertainty objective. Specifically, an individual-level calibration term aligns predictive uncertainty with each patient's likelihood of error, while a cohort-aware regularizer pulls risk estimates toward event rates in their local neighborhoods in the embedding space and places extra weight on ambiguous cohorts near the decision boundary. We further show that this cohort-aware term can be interpreted as a cross-entropy loss with neighborhood-informed soft labels, providing a label-smoothing view of our method. Extensive experiments on MIMIC-IV clinical risk prediction tasks across various clinical LMs show that CURA consistently improves calibration metrics without substantially compromising discrimination. Further analysis illustrates that CURA reduces overconfident false reassurance and yields more trustworthy uncertainty estimates for downstream clinical decision support.

AI.tldr: They propose CURA, a framework to better calibrate uncertainty estimates of clinical language models for risk prediction, aligning them with both individual error likelihoods and cohort-level ambiguity.
AI.motivation: Clinical language models used for risk prediction often output poorly calibrated uncertainty, which makes their predictions clinically unreliable. There's a need to align model confidence with true error likelihood, and to incorporate cohort-level ambiguity to avoid overconfident predictions in uncertain regions.
AI.method: CURA first fine-tunes clinical LMs to create task-specific patient embeddings. Then a multi-head classifier is trained with a bi-level uncertainty objective: (1) an individual-level calibration loss aligning predictive uncertainty with per-patient error likelihood, and (2) a cohort-aware regularizer that nudges risk estimates toward local event rates in the embedding space and emphasizes ambiguous neighborhoods near the decision boundary. This regularizer can be reframed as cross-entropy with neighborhood-informed soft labels, akin to label smoothing.
AI.result: On MIMIC-IV risk prediction tasks and across multiple clinical LMs, CURA consistently improves calibration metrics while maintaining similar discrimination performance compared to baselines, leading to better-aligned uncertainty estimates.
AI.conclusion: CURA yields better-calibrated and more trustworthy uncertainty estimates from clinical LMs, reducing overconfident false reassurance and making these models more reliable for downstream clinical decision support.

title: SPAGBias: Uncovering and Tracing Structured Spatial Gender Bias in Large Language Models
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are being increasingly used in urban planning, but since gendered space theory highlights how gender hierarchies are embedded in spatial organization, there is concern that LLMs may reproduce or amplify such biases. We introduce SPAGBias - the first systematic framework to evaluate spatial gender bias in LLMs. It combines a taxonomy of 62 urban micro-spaces, a prompt library, and three diagnostic layers: explicit (forced-choice resampling), probabilistic (token-level asymmetry), and constructional (semantic and narrative role analysis). Testing six representative models, we identify structured gender-space associations that go beyond the public-private divide, forming nuanced micro-level mappings. Story generation reveals how emotion, wording, and social roles jointly shape "spatial gender narratives". We also examine how prompt design, temperature, and model scale influence bias expression. Tracing experiments indicate that these patterns are embedded and reinforced across the model pipeline (pre-training, instruction tuning, and reward modeling), with model associations found to substantially exceed real-world distributions. Downstream experiments further reveal that such biases produce concrete failures in both normative and descriptive application settings. This work connects sociological theory with computational analysis, extending bias research into the spatial domain and uncovering how LLMs encode social gender cognition through language.

AI.tldr: Introduces SPAGBias, the first systematic framework to evaluate spatial gender bias in LLMs, showing that models encode structured, micro-level gender–space associations that exceed real-world patterns and cause downstream failures in urban-planning-related tasks.
AI.motivation: LLMs are increasingly used in urban planning tasks where spatial decisions affect people’s lives. Gendered space theory suggests that social gender hierarchies are embedded in how spaces are organized and described. There is concern that LLMs may reproduce or amplify such spatial gender biases, yet there has been no systematic, theory-grounded way to measure these biases in spatial contexts. The paper aims to fill this gap by connecting sociological theory of gendered space with computational evaluation of LLM behavior.
AI.method: The authors propose SPAGBias, a framework combining: (1) a taxonomy of 62 urban micro-spaces; (2) a curated prompt library that probes LLMs about these spaces; and (3) three diagnostic layers: (a) explicit bias via forced-choice resampling (e.g., which gender is linked to which space); (b) probabilistic bias via token-level asymmetry in model likelihoods; and (c) constructional bias via semantic and narrative role analysis in generated stories. They apply SPAGBias to six representative LLMs, vary prompt design, temperature, and model scale, and perform tracing experiments across training stages (pre-training, instruction tuning, reward modeling). They also conduct downstream application experiments in normative and descriptive urban-planning settings.
AI.result: Across six LLMs, the framework uncovers consistent, structured gender–space associations that are more nuanced than a simple public–private split, revealing fine-grained micro-space patterns.
