Story generation shows that emotional framing, lexical choices, and assigned social roles co-produce “spatial gender narratives.” Parameter studies demonstrate that prompt design, sampling temperature, and model size modulate how strongly bias is expressed. Tracing analyses suggest that spatial gender biases are introduced and reinforced at multiple stages of the model pipeline. Comparisons with external data indicate that LLM associations substantially exceed real-world gender–space distributions. Downstream evaluations show that these biases translate into concrete errors and unfair behavior in urban-planning-related tasks.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that LLMs encode a form of social gender cognition that is spatially structured and can be systematically analyzed using SPAGBias. These gender–space biases are not merely reflections of real-world data but are amplified and embedded throughout the training pipeline, with practical negative impacts on urban-planning and related applications. The work bridges sociological theory of gendered spaces with computational bias analysis, and calls for more spatially aware, gender-sensitive evaluation and mitigation strategies in the design and deployment of LLMs for urban contexts.

title: Benchmarks for Trajectory Safety Evaluation and Diagnosis in OpenClaw and Codex: ATBench-Claw and ATBench-CodeX
summary: As agent systems move into increasingly diverse execution settings, trajectory-level safety evaluation and diagnosis require benchmarks that evolve with them. ATBench is a diverse and realistic agent trajectory benchmark for safety evaluation and diagnosis. This report presents ATBench-Claw and ATBench-CodeX, two domain-customized extensions that carry ATBench into the OpenClaw and OpenAI Codex / Codex-runtime settings. The key adaptation mechanism is to analyze each new setting, customize the three-dimensional Safety Taxonomy over risk source, failure mode, and real-world harm, and then use that customized taxonomy to define the benchmark specification consumed by the shared ATBench construction pipeline. This extensibility matters because agent frameworks remain relatively stable at the architectural level even as their concrete execution settings, tool ecosystems, and product capabilities evolve quickly. Concretely, ATBench-Claw targets OpenClaw-sensitive execution chains over tools, skills, sessions, and external actions, while ATBench-CodeX targets trajectories in the OpenAI Codex / Codex-runtime setting over repositories, shells, patches, dependencies, approvals, and runtime policy boundaries. Our emphasis therefore falls on taxonomy customization, domain-specific risk coverage, and benchmark design under a shared ATBench generation framework.

AI.tldr: The paper extends the ATBench safety benchmark to two new agent execution domains (OpenClaw and OpenAI Codex/Codex-runtime) by customizing a three-dimensional safety taxonomy and reusing a shared benchmark construction pipeline.
AI.motivation: Agent systems are being deployed in more varied and complex execution environments, making trajectory-level safety evaluation and diagnosis challenging. Existing benchmarks are often tied to specific settings and do not easily adapt as tools, products, and runtimes change. The authors want a way to systematically extend safety benchmarks to new domains while maintaining comparability and coverage of domain-specific risks.
AI.method: They introduce two domain-specific benchmark suites—ATBench-Claw and ATBench-CodeX—built on top of a general ATBench framework. The core method is to analyze each new execution setting and then customize a three-dimensional Safety Taxonomy spanning risk source, failure mode, and real-world harm. This customized taxonomy drives the formal benchmark specification, which is then fed into a shared ATBench construction pipeline that generates concrete agent trajectories, tasks, and evaluation artifacts for each domain.
AI.result: The work yields two concrete benchmarks: ATBench-Claw, covering safety in OpenClaw execution chains involving tools, skills, sessions, and external actions; and ATBench-CodeX, covering safety in OpenAI Codex/Codex-runtime trajectories involving repositories, shells, patches, dependencies, approvals, and runtime policy boundaries. These benchmarks demonstrate that the ATBench framework can be systematically extended to substantially different agent/tool ecosystems while still using a unified construction process.
AI.conclusion: The authors conclude that a taxonomy-driven, extensible benchmark framework is an effective way to keep trajectory-level safety evaluation aligned with rapidly evolving agent execution settings. By focusing on customizing the safety taxonomy for each domain and using a shared generation pipeline, ATBench can maintain architectural stability while capturing domain-specific risks. This approach supports ongoing safety diagnosis and comparison as agent tools and runtimes continue to change.

title: Which bird does not have wings: Negative-constrained KGQA with Schema-guided Semantic Matching and Self-directed Refinement
summary: Large language models still struggle with faithfulness and hallucinations despite their remarkable reasoning abilities. In Knowledge Graph Question Answering (KGQA), semantic parsing-based approaches address the limitations by understanding constraints in a user's question and converting them into a logical form to execute on a knowledge graph. However, existing KGQA benchmarks and methods are biased toward positive and calculation constraints. Negative constraints are neglected, although they frequently appear in real-world questions. In this paper, we introduce a new task, NEgative-conSTrained (NEST) KGQA, where each question contains at least one negative constraint, and a corresponding dataset, NestKGQA. We also design PyLF, a Python-formatted logical form, since existing logical forms are hardly suitable to express negation clearly while maintaining readability. Furthermore, NEST questions naturally contain multiple constraints. To mitigate their semantic complexity, we present a novel framework named CUCKOO, specialized to multiple-constrained questions and ensuring semantic executability. CUCKOO first generates a constraint-aware logical form draft and performs schema-guided semantic matching. It then selectively applies self-directed refinement only when executing improper logical forms yields an empty result, reducing cost while improving robustness. Experimental results demonstrate that CUCKOO consistently outperforms baselines on both conventional KGQA and NEST-KGQA benchmarks under few-shot settings.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces NEST-KGQA, a new KGQA task and dataset focusing on questions with explicit negative constraints, and proposes CUCKOO, a framework using a Python-style logical form (PyLF) and selective refinement to handle multi-constraint questions more robustly, outperforming baselines in few-shot settings.
AI.motivation: Existing KGQA systems and benchmarks mostly focus on positive and numeric constraints and largely ignore negative constraints (e.g., “not”, “without”), even though such constraints are common in natural questions. This leads to models that are poorly equipped to handle more realistic, negatively constrained queries, and current logical form representations make expressing negation clearly and readably difficult. The authors aim to close this gap by creating appropriate tasks, data, and modeling methods for negative and multi-constraint questions.
AI.method:
1) Define a new task, NEST-KGQA, where each question has at least one negative constraint, and build the NestKGQA dataset accordingly.
2) Propose PyLF, a Python-formatted logical form designed to represent negation and multiple constraints in a clearer and more readable way than prior logical forms.
3) Develop CUCKOO, a framework for KGQA on multi-constrained (including negative) questions. CUCKOO: (a) generates an initial constraint-aware logical form draft from the question; (b) performs schema-guided semantic matching with the KG to ensure compatibility; and (c) conditionally applies a self-directed refinement process only when the execution of the current logical form returns an empty result, thereby correcting errors while keeping computational cost lower than always-refining approaches.
AI.result: On both standard KGQA benchmarks and the new NEST-KGQA dataset, CUCKOO achieves consistently better performance than existing baseline methods in few-shot scenarios. The results indicate that the proposed approach is more robust to negative and multiple constraints and that PyLF is effective for representing such complex logical forms. The framework improves faithfulness and reduces error rates associated with misinterpreting constraints without incurring excessive inference cost.
AI.conclusion: The work shows that explicitly modeling negative constraints and using a tailored logical form significantly improves KGQA performance on more realistic, complex questions. NEST-KGQA and the NestKGQA dataset fill a missing evaluation gap for negative-constrained queries, while PyLF offers a clearer representation for logical forms with negation. CUCKOO’s constraint-aware generation plus selective refinement strategy yields state-of-the-art few-shot results on both conventional and NEST-style KGQA benchmarks, suggesting a promising direction for building more faithful, semantically robust KGQA systems.

title: The Missing Knowledge Layer in AI: A Framework for Stable Human-AI Reasoning
summary:
Large language models are increasingly integrated into decision-making in areas such as healthcare, law, finance, engineering, and government. Yet they share a critical limitation: they produce fluent outputs even when their internal reasoning has drifted. A confident answer can conceal uncertainty, speculation, or inconsistency, and small changes in phrasing can lead to different conclusions. This makes LLMs useful assistants but unreliable partners in high-stakes contexts.
  Humans exhibit a similar weakness, often mistaking fluency for reliability. When a model responds smoothly, users tend to trust it, even when both model and user are drifting together. This paper is the first in a five-paper research series on stabilising human-AI reasoning. The series proposes a two-layer approach: Parts II-IV introduce human-side mechanisms such as uncertainty cues, conflict surfacing, and auditable reasoning traces, while Part V develops a model-side Epistemic Control Loop (ECL) that detects instability and modulates generation accordingly.
  Together, these layers form a missing operational substrate for governance by increasing signal-to-noise at the point of use. Stabilising interaction makes uncertainty and drift visible before enforcement is applied, enabling more precise capability governance. This aligns with emerging compliance expectations, including the EU AI Act and ISO/IEC 42001, by making reasoning processes traceable under real conditions of use.
  The central claim is that fluency is not reliability. Without structures that stabilise both human and model reasoning, AI cannot be trusted or governed where it matters most.

AI.tldr: The paper argues that large language models (LLMs) are fluent but unreliable in their reasoning, especially in high-stakes domains, and proposes a two-layer human-AI stabilisation framework to make reasoning more traceable and governable.
AI.motivation: LLMs are increasingly used in critical decision-making domains, but they can produce confident, fluent answers even when their underlying reasoning is unstable or drifting. Humans are prone to over-trusting such fluent outputs, leading to joint human-model errors. There is a need for practical mechanisms that stabilise reasoning so that AI systems can be trusted and governed in real-world, high-stakes settings, and to meet emerging regulatory and compliance requirements.
AI.method: This first paper in a five-part series conceptually outlines a two-layer stabilisation approach: (1) human-side mechanisms such as uncertainty cues, conflict surfacing, and auditable reasoning traces, developed in subsequent parts II-IV; and (2) a model-side Epistemic Control Loop (ECL) in part V that dynamically detects instability in the model’s reasoning and modulates text generation. The paper frames these as an operational substrate for governance and situates them in relation to regulatory expectations like the EU AI Act and ISO/IEC 42001.
AI.result: As an introductory and conceptual paper, the primary result is a structured framework and central thesis: that human-AI interactions must be stabilised on both sides to avoid mistaking fluency for reliability. It clarifies the roles of human-side and model-side mechanisms and argues that together they can reveal uncertainty and drift in real time, thereby improving signal-to-noise in AI-assisted decision-making and enabling more targeted capability governance.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that fluency cannot be equated with reliability in LLMs, particularly in high-stakes contexts. Without explicit structures that stabilise both human and model reasoning, AI systems will remain untrustworthy and difficult to govern. The proposed two-layer framework—combining human-side interaction designs with a model-side Epistemic Control Loop—offers a missing operational substrate for governance by making reasoning processes auditable, traceable, and better aligned with emerging regulatory standards.

title: CoPA: Benchmarking Personalized Question Answering with Data-Informed Cognitive Factors
summary: While LLMs have demonstrated remarkable potential in Question Answering (QA), evaluating personalization remains a critical bottleneck. Existing paradigms predominantly rely on lexical-level similarity or manual heuristics, often lacking sufficient data-driven validation. We address this by mining Community-Individual Preference Divergence (CIPD), where individual choices override consensus, to distill six key personalization factors as evaluative dimensions. Accordingly, we introduce CoPA, a benchmark with 1,985 user profiles for fine-grained, factor-level assessment. By quantifying the alignment between model outputs and user-specific cognitive preferences inferred from interaction patterns, CoPA provides a more comprehensive and discriminative standard for evaluating personalized QA than generic metrics. The code is available at https://github.com/bjzgcai/CoPA.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a new benchmark, CoPA, to more rigorously evaluate how well LLMs personalize QA by aligning with individual user preferences rather than community consensus.
AI.motivation: Current evaluations of personalized QA for LLMs rely mostly on surface-level lexical similarity or handcrafted heuristics, which are not sufficiently data-driven and fail to capture whether model outputs truly match individual user preferences. There is a need for a more principled, empirically grounded way to measure personalization quality beyond generic QA metrics.
AI.method: The authors mine Community-Individual Preference Divergence (CIPD) cases, where an individual’s choices deviate from the majority or consensus, to identify and distill six core personalization factors that serve as evaluation dimensions. Based on these factors, they construct CoPA, a benchmark consisting of 1,985 user profiles. They then define an evaluation protocol that measures how well LLM answers align with user-specific cognitive preferences inferred from those profiles and interaction patterns.
AI.result: The resulting CoPA benchmark enables fine-grained, factor-level assessment of personalized QA. It yields a more discriminative and comprehensive evaluation of how LLM responses conform to individual user preferences compared to traditional, generic metrics that do not account for such personalization dimensions.
AI.conclusion: By focusing on Community-Individual Preference Divergence and codifying six key personalization factors into a large set of user profiles, CoPA offers a more nuanced and data-driven standard for evaluating personalized QA in LLMs. This framework better captures user-specific cognitive alignment than existing lexical or heuristic-based metrics and can serve as a stronger benchmark for future research on personalized language models.

title: Cooperate to Compete: Strategic Data Generation and Incentivization Framework for Coopetitive Cross-Silo Federated Learning
comment: Competition-aware Federated Learning, Strategic data generation approaches, A payoff-redistribution based incentive mechanism, Potential game, Social welfare
summary: In data-sensitive domains such as healthcare, cross-silo federated learning (CFL) allows organizations to collaboratively train AI models without sharing raw data. However, practical CFL deployments are inherently coopetitive, in which organizations cooperate during model training while competing in downstream markets. In such settings, training contributions, including data volume, quality, and diversity, can improve the global model yet inadvertently strengthen rivals. This dilemma is amplified by non-IID data, which leads to asymmetric learning gains and undermines sustained participation. While existing competition-aware CFL and incentive-design approaches reward organizations based on marginal training contributions, they fail to account for the costs of strengthening competitors. In this paper, we introduce CoCoGen+, a coopetition-compatible data generation and incentivization framework that jointly models non-IID data and inter-organizational competition while endogenizing GenAI-based synthetic data generation as a strategic decision. Specifically, CoCoGen+ formulates each training round as a weighted potential game, where organizations strategically decide how much synthetic data to generate by balancing learning performance gains against computational costs and competition-caused utility losses. We then provide a tractable equilibrium characterization and derive implementable generation strategies to maximize social welfare. To promote long-term collaboration, we integrate a payoff redistribution-based incentive mechanism to compensate organizations for their contributions and competition-caused utility degradation. Experiments on varying learning tasks validate the feasibility of CoCoGen+. The results show how non-IID data, competition intensity, and incentives shape organizational strategies and social welfare, while CoCoGen+ outperforms baselines in efficiency.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes CoCoGen+, a framework for federated learning in competitive multi-organization settings that uses strategic synthetic data generation and incentive mechanisms to align collaboration with competition.
AI.motivation: In real-world cross-silo federated learning, organizations both cooperate to train models and compete in downstream markets. Existing methods reward participants only by marginal training contributions and ignore the negative effect of improving competitors’ models, especially under non-IID data that creates asymmetric gains. This discourages sustained participation and limits CFL adoption. There is a need for a framework that explicitly accounts for competition and strategic use of synthetic data in such coopetitive environments.
AI.method: The authors formulate each training round of cross-silo FL as a weighted potential game among organizations. Each organization strategically chooses how much GenAI-based synthetic data to generate and contribute, trading off performance gains, computation costs, and competition-induced utility loss. They analytically characterize equilibria of this game, derive practical synthetic data generation strategies that maximize overall social welfare, and design a payoff redistribution-based incentive mechanism to compensate contributors for both their beneficial impact and their competition-caused utility loss.
AI.result: Theoretical analysis yields a tractable equilibrium characterization and implementable strategy rules for synthetic data generation that are socially optimal under the model. Experiments on diverse learning tasks show that CoCoGen+ is feasible in practice and leads to higher social welfare and training efficiency than baseline CFL and incentive schemes. The empirical results further illustrate how degrees of data heterogeneity (non-IID), market competition intensity, and incentive strength affect participants’ optimal strategies and the resulting welfare.
AI.conclusion: CoCoGen+ effectively aligns incentives in coopetitive cross-silo federated learning by embedding strategic synthetic data generation into a game-theoretic and incentive-compatible framework. By jointly modeling non-IID data and inter-organizational competition and then redistributing payoffs to offset competition-caused losses, it promotes long-term collaboration and outperforms existing baselines in efficiency and social welfare across various tasks.

title: Knowing When Not to Answer: Evaluating Abstention in Multimodal Reasoning Systems
summary: Effective abstention (EA), recognizing evidence insufficiency and refraining from answering, is critical for reliable multimodal systems. Yet existing evaluation paradigms for vision-language models (VLMs) and multi-agent systems (MAS) assume answerability, pushing models to always respond. Abstention has been studied in text-only settings but remains underexplored multimodally; current benchmarks either ignore unanswerability or rely on coarse methods that miss realistic failure modes. We introduce MM-AQA, a benchmark that constructs unanswerable instances from answerable ones via transformations along two axes: visual modality dependency and evidence sufficiency. Evaluating three frontier VLMs spanning closed and open-source models and two MAS architectures across 2079 samples, we find: (1) under standard prompting, VLMs rarely abstain; even simple confidence baselines outperform this setup, (2) MAS improves abstention but introduces an accuracy-abstention trade-off, (3) sequential designs match or exceed iterative variants, suggesting the bottleneck is miscalibration rather than reasoning depth, and (4) models abstain when image or text evidence is absent, but attempt reconciliation with degraded or contradictory evidence. Effective multimodal abstention requires abstention-aware training rather than better prompting or more agents.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces MM-AQA, a benchmark to test whether multimodal systems can recognize when they lack sufficient evidence and should abstain from answering.
AI.motivation: Existing evaluations of vision-language models and multi-agent systems assume that every question is answerable, which pushes models to always reply and hides an important aspect of reliability: knowing when not to answer. While abstention has been studied for text-only models, multimodal abstention is underexplored, and current benchmarks either exclude unanswerable cases or use simplistic constructions that miss realistic failure modes. The authors want to systematically study and benchmark effective abstention in realistic multimodal settings.
AI.method: The authors build MM-AQA, a benchmark that turns originally answerable multimodal QA instances into unanswerable ones by controlled transformations along two dimensions: (1) whether the question truly depends on the visual modality, and (2) whether there is sufficient evidence in the provided inputs to answer. They then evaluate three state-of-the-art vision-language models (both closed- and open-source) and two multi-agent system architectures on 2079 samples. They compare standard prompting, confidence-based baselines, and different multi-agent designs (sequential vs iterative) to analyze abstention behavior.
AI.result: Under ordinary prompting, vision-language models almost never abstain, and simple confidence-based heuristics already outperform this naive setup for deciding when to abstain. Multi-agent systems can improve abstention rates but at the cost of an accuracy-abstention trade-off: improving one often harms the other. Sequential agent designs perform as well as or better than more complex iterative ones, indicating that deeper or repeated reasoning is not the main limiter; instead, the key issue is probability calibration. Models tend to abstain when the image or text is completely missing but still attempt to reconcile and answer when evidence is noisy, degraded, or contradictory, leading to failures.
AI.conclusion: Effective abstention in multimodal systems cannot be achieved just by better prompting or adding more agents. The core challenge lies in model calibration and explicit exposure to abstention scenarios during training. MM-AQA provides a structured benchmark to reveal these limitations and suggests that abstention-aware training and evaluation are necessary to build reliable multimodal AI that knows when it should refrain from answering.

title: MemoSight: Unifying Context Compression and Multi Token Prediction for Reasoning Acceleration
summary: While Chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning enables LLMs to solve challenging reasoning problems, as KV cache grows linearly with the number of generated tokens, CoT reasoning faces scaling issues in terms of speed and memory usage. In this work, we propose MemoSight (Memory-Foresight-based reasoning), a unified framework that integrates both context compression and multi-token prediction to mitigate the efficiency issues while maintaining CoT reasoning performance. Our framework adopts the same minimalist design for both context compression and multi-token prediction via special tokens and their corresponding position layout tailored to each token type. Comprehensive experiments on four reasoning benchmarks demonstrate that MemoSight reduces the KV cache footprint by up to 66% and accelerates inference by 1.56x, while outperforming existing CoT compression methods.

AI.tldr: MemoSight is a unified framework that speeds up and shrinks memory for chain-of-thought reasoning in LLMs, using a minimalist design for both context compression and multi-token prediction.
AI.motivation: Chain-of-thought reasoning makes LLMs better at complex problems but requires generating long sequences, causing the KV cache to grow linearly with token count. This leads to high memory usage and slower inference, which is problematic for scaling to longer or more complex tasks. Existing CoT compression methods help but still face a trade-off between efficiency and reasoning performance. The paper aims to address these scalability and efficiency issues without sacrificing CoT quality.
AI.method: The authors propose MemoSight, a unified, memory-foresight-based reasoning framework. It combines (1) context compression to reduce the effective sequence length stored in the KV cache, and (2) multi-token prediction to generate multiple tokens per forward pass. Both components use a minimalist and unified design: they introduce special token types and specific position layouts customized for each token type, allowing the model to handle compressed context and multi-token outputs consistently. This design modifies how the model represents and processes reasoning traces to be more storage- and computation-efficient, without heavy architectural changes.
AI.result: On four reasoning benchmarks, MemoSight significantly reduces resource requirements while preserving or improving reasoning capability. It cuts the KV cache footprint by up to 66% and speeds up inference by a factor of 1.56x. Moreover, it outperforms existing chain-of-thought compression methods on these benchmarks, suggesting better efficiency–performance trade-offs.
AI.conclusion: MemoSight shows that a unified and minimalist approach to both context compression and multi-token prediction can substantially improve the scalability of chain-of-thought reasoning in LLMs. By reducing KV cache size and speeding inference without harming (and sometimes improving) performance, it provides a practical path to more efficient deployment of CoT reasoning models.

title: Modeling LLM Unlearning as an Asymmetric Two-Task Learning Problem
summary: Machine unlearning for large language models (LLMs) aims to remove targeted knowledge while preserving general capability. In this paper, we recast LLM unlearning as an asymmetric two-task problem: retention is the primary objective and forgetting is an auxiliary. From this perspective, we propose a retention-prioritized gradient synthesis framework that decouples task-specific gradient extraction from conflict-aware combination. Instantiating the framework, we adapt established PCGrad to resolve gradient conflicts, and introduce SAGO, a novel retention-prioritized gradient synthesis method. Theoretically, both variants ensure non-negative cosine similarity with the retain gradient, while SAGO achieves strictly tighter alignment through constructive sign-constrained synthesis. Empirically, on WMDP Bio/Cyber and RWKU benchmarks, SAGO consistently pushes the Pareto frontier: e.g., on WMDP Bio (SimNPO+GD), recovery of target model MMLU performance progresses from 44.6% (naive) to 94.0% (+PCGrad) and further to 96.0% (+SAGO), while maintaining comparable forgetting strength. Our results show that re-shaping gradient geometry, rather than re-balancing losses, is the key to mitigating unlearning-retention trade-offs.

AI.tldr: They propose a new gradient-synthesis framework and method (SAGO) for machine unlearning in LLMs that prioritize retaining general capabilities while still achieving strong forgetting, showing better retention–unlearning trade-offs than prior approaches.
AI.motivation: Existing machine unlearning methods for LLMs struggle with a trade-off: removing specific targeted knowledge often harms the model’s general abilities. Prior work often treats forgetting and retention more symmetrically or focuses on loss re-weighting, which does not fully resolve gradient conflicts between the two objectives. The authors aim to design an approach that explicitly prioritizes retention while still enforcing forgetting, to achieve a better Pareto frontier between these two competing goals.
AI.method: They reconceptualize unlearning as an asymmetric two-task problem where retention is primary and forgetting is auxiliary. Based on this view, they propose a retention-prioritized gradient synthesis framework that decouples (1) extracting task-specific gradients for retain vs. forget objectives and (2) combining them via a conflict-aware mechanism. As instantiations, they adapt PCGrad (a known gradient conflict-resolution method) to this unlearning setting and introduce a new method, SAGO, which performs constructive sign-constrained gradient synthesis to ensure strong alignment with the retention gradient while integrating forgetting directions. Both approaches guarantee non-negative cosine similarity with the retain gradient, with SAGO providing a strictly tighter alignment than PCGrad.
AI.result: On WMDP Bio/Cyber and RWKU unlearning benchmarks, their SAGO method consistently improves the trade-off between forgetting and retention, effectively pushing the Pareto frontier. For example, in the WMDP Bio (SimNPO+GD) setup, the recovery of the target model’s MMLU performance increases from 44.6% with a naive baseline to 94.0% with PCGrad and further to 96.0% with SAGO, all while maintaining similar levels of forgetting strength. This demonstrates that SAGO can significantly restore general performance without sacrificing the effectiveness of unlearning.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that explicitly reshaping the geometry of gradients—via retention-prioritized, conflict-aware synthesis—is more effective for mitigating the unlearning–retention trade-off than simply re-balancing loss terms. Their SAGO method, as an instance of this principle, offers a stronger alignment with retention gradients and yields superior empirical trade-offs, suggesting that future unlearning methods should focus on gradient-shaping strategies to better preserve model capabilities during knowledge removal.

title: Toward Agentic RAG for Ukrainian
comment: This paper is a research report based on our participation in the UNLP 2026 Shared Task
summary: We present an initial investigation into Agentic Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) for Ukrainian, conducted within the UNLP 2026 Shared Task on Multi-Domain Document Understanding. Our system combines two-stage retrieval (BGE-M3 with BGE reranking) with a lightweight agentic layer performing query rephrasing and answer-retry loops on top of Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct. Our analysis reveals that retrieval quality is the primary bottleneck: agentic retry mechanisms improve answer accuracy but the overall score remains constrained by document and page identification. We discuss practical limitations of offline agentic pipelines and outline directions for combining stronger retrieval with more advanced agentic reasoning for Ukrainian.

AI.tldr: Initial exploration of agentic Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) for Ukrainian in a shared task, showing that retrieval quality is the main bottleneck despite benefits from agentic querying and retries.
AI.motivation: To understand how agentic RAG methods perform for Ukrainian, a relatively low-resource language, within the context of a multi-domain document understanding task, and to identify where performance bottlenecks arise (retrieval vs. generation/agentic mechanisms).
AI.method: They build a system that uses two-stage retrieval (BGE-M3 as the initial retriever followed by BGE-based reranking) and place a lightweight agentic layer on top of the Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct model. The agentic layer performs query rephrasing and implements answer-retry loops, effectively allowing the model to reformulate questions and try multiple times when confidence is low or answers appear incomplete.
AI.result: The experiments show that while the agentic retry and query-rephrasing mechanisms do improve answer accuracy, the overall system performance is still chiefly limited by the quality of retrieval. Specifically, errors in identifying the correct documents and pages cap the achievable score, even when the generative and agentic components behave well.
AI.conclusion: Improving retrieval—especially document and page identification—is crucial for better Ukrainian RAG performance, as agentic mechanisms alone cannot overcome poor evidence access. The authors also note practical constraints of offline agentic pipelines and propose future work on integrating stronger retrieval models with more sophisticated agentic reasoning tailored for Ukrainian.

title: Domain Fine-Tuning FinBERT on Finnish Histopathological Reports: Train-Time Signals and Downstream Correlations
summary:
In NLP classification tasks where little labeled data exists, domain fine-tuning of transformer models on unlabeled data is an established approach. In this paper we have two aims.
  (1) We describe our observations from fine-tuning the Finnish BERT model on Finnish medical text data.
  (2) We report on our attempts to predict the benefit of domain-specific pre-training of Finnish BERT from observing the geometry of embedding changes due to domain fine-tuning.
  Our driving motivation is the common\situation in healthcare AI where we might experience long delays in acquiring datasets, especially with respect to labels.

AI.tldr: The paper studies Finnish BERT domain fine-tuning on Finnish medical texts with scarce labels, and explores whether changes in embedding geometry during fine-tuning can predict the benefit of such domain-specific pre-training.
AI.motivation: In healthcare AI, acquiring labeled data and even datasets can be slow and difficult due to privacy and regulatory constraints. This motivates methods that can exploit large amounts of unlabeled domain text, such as domain fine-tuning of transformer models, and tools to estimate in advance whether such fine-tuning is worthwhile.
AI.method: They domain-fine-tune a Finnish BERT model using unlabeled Finnish medical text. They then analyze and characterize how the model’s embeddings change geometrically during this fine-tuning, and investigate relationships between these embedding-geometry changes and downstream task performance, with the goal of predicting the utility of domain-specific pre-training.
AI.result: The authors observe specific patterns in how Finnish BERT’s embeddings adapt when fine-tuned on Finnish medical data and identify correlations between embedding-geometry changes and performance gains on classification tasks with limited labeled data. These observations inform when and how domain-specific fine-tuning is beneficial.
AI.conclusion: Domain fine-tuning of Finnish BERT on unlabeled medical text can significantly improve performance in low-label NLP classification tasks, and geometric analysis of embedding shifts during fine-tuning holds promise as a way to anticipate the benefits of such domain-specific pre-training before labeled data is available.

title: Governing Reflective Human-AI Collaboration: A Framework for Epistemic Scaffolding and Traceable Reasoning
summary:
Large language models have advanced rapidly, from pattern recognition to emerging forms of reasoning, yet they remain confined to linguistic simulation rather than grounded understanding. They can produce fluent outputs that resemble reflection, but lack temporal continuity, causal feedback, and anchoring in real-world interaction. This paper proposes a complementary approach in which reasoning is treated as a relational process distributed between human and model rather than an internal capability of either.
  Building on recent work on "System-2" learning, we relocate reflective reasoning to the interaction layer. Instead of engineering reasoning solely within models, we frame it as a cognitive protocol that can be structured, measured, and governed using existing systems. This perspective emphasizes collaborative intelligence, combining human judgment and contextual understanding with machine speed, memory, and associative capacity.
  We introduce "The Architect's Pen" as a practical method. Like an architect who thinks through drawing, the human uses the model as an external medium for structured reflection. By embedding phases of articulation, critique, and revision into human-AI interaction, the dialogue itself becomes a reasoning loop: human abstraction -> model articulation -> human reflection.
  This reframes the question from whether the model can think to whether the human-AI system can reason. The framework enables auditable reasoning traces and supports alignment with emerging governance standards, including the EU AI Act and ISO/IEC 42001. It provides a practical path toward more transparent, controllable, and accountable AI use without requiring new model architectures.

AI.tldr: The paper argues that instead of expecting large language models to perform standalone reasoning, we should design human–AI interaction protocols where reasoning emerges from the collaboration, and presents a concrete method called “The Architect’s Pen” to structure such interaction.
AI.motivation: Current large language models can mimic reasoning linguistically but lack grounded understanding, temporal continuity, and real-world feedback, limiting their reliability for reflective reasoning. The authors want a way to get robust, auditable reasoning without waiting for fundamentally new model architectures.
AI.method: They propose relocating reflective “System-2” reasoning from inside the model to the interaction layer, treating it as a cognitive protocol between human and AI. Concretely, they introduce “The Architect’s Pen,” in which a human uses the model as an external medium for structured reflection via repeated phases: the human abstracts a problem, the model articulates and elaborates, and the human critiques and revises, forming a reasoning loop.
AI.result: The approach yields a framework where reasoning traces are explicit and auditable, with interaction phases that can be structured, measured, and governed. It shows that useful, controllable reasoning behavior can be achieved by protocol design over existing models, without architectural changes.
AI.conclusion: Reasoning should be viewed as a property of the human–AI system rather than the model alone. By designing interaction protocols like “The Architect’s Pen,” we can achieve more transparent, accountable, and governable AI-supported reasoning that aligns with regulatory frameworks such as the EU AI Act and ISO/IEC 42001, all while using current LLM architectures.

title: ADAPT: Benchmarking Commonsense Planning under Unspecified Affordance Constraints
summary: Intelligent embodied agents should not simply follow instructions, as real-world environments often involve unexpected conditions and exceptions. However, existing methods usually focus on directly executing instructions, without considering whether the target objects can actually be manipulated, meaning they fail to assess available affordances. To address this limitation, we introduce DynAfford, a benchmark that evaluates embodied agents in dynamic environments where object affordances may change over time and are not specified in the instruction. DynAfford requires agents to perceive object states, infer implicit preconditions, and adapt their actions accordingly. To enable this capability, we introduce ADAPT, a plug-and-play module that augments existing planners with explicit affordance reasoning. Experiments demonstrate that incorporating ADAPT significantly improves robustness and task success across both seen and unseen environments. We also show that a domain-adapted, LoRA-finetuned vision-language model used as the affordance inference backend outperforms a commercial LLM (GPT-4o), highlighting the importance of task-aligned affordance grounding.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces DynAfford, a benchmark and ADAPT, a planning module to test and improve embodied agents’ ability to reason about changing object affordances in dynamic environments, leading to more robust instruction following.
AI.motivation: Existing embodied agents predominantly execute instructions literally, assuming that all referenced objects are manipulable as described. In realistic, dynamic environments, object affordances change over time and are often not explicitly conveyed in the instructions. This gap means agents fail when objects are unavailable, in an unexpected state, or require implicit preconditions. The paper aims to provide a benchmark and method for assessing and improving agents’ capacity to perceive object states, infer affordances, and adapt behavior accordingly.
AI.method: The authors design DynAfford, a benchmark of dynamic environments where object states and affordances can change, and where instructions omit these changes. Tasks require agents to detect object states, infer missing preconditions, and choose feasible actions. They propose ADAPT, a plug-and-play module that can be integrated with existing planners to add explicit affordance reasoning. ADAPT uses a vision-language model backend to infer affordances from visual observations. They further train a domain-adapted, LoRA-finetuned VLM specifically for affordance inference and compare it against a commercial LLM (GPT-4o) in this role.
AI.result: Experiments show that integrating ADAPT into existing planners substantially increases robustness and task success rates on the DynAfford benchmark in both seen and unseen environments. The domain-adapted, LoRA-finetuned vision-language model used as the affordance inference component outperforms GPT-4o, indicating that specialized, task-aligned grounding leads to better affordance reasoning than a general-purpose commercial LLM.
AI.conclusion: The work demonstrates that explicit affordance reasoning is crucial for robust embodied agents operating in dynamic, real-world-like environments. DynAfford offers a challenging benchmark to evaluate such capabilities, and ADAPT provides a modular way to enhance planners with affordance awareness. The superiority of the task-adapted VLM over GPT-4o suggests that carefully aligned, grounded models are essential for reliable affordance inference and adaptive behavior in embodied AI.

title: Exploring and Testing Skill-Based Behavioral Profile Annotation: Human Operability and LLM Feasibility under Schema-Guided Execution
summary: Behavioral Profile (BP) annotation is difficult to automate because it requires simultaneous coding across multiple linguistic dimensions. We treat BP annotation as a bundle of annotation skills rather than a single task and evaluate LLM-assisted BP annotation from this perspective. Using 3,134 concordance lines of 30 Chinese metaphorical color-term derivatives and a 14-feature BP schema, we implement a skill-file-driven pipeline in which each feature is externally defined through schema files, decision rules, and examples. Two human annotators completed a two-round schema-only protocol on a 300-instance validation subset, enabling BP skills to be classified as directly operable, recoverable under focused re-annotation, or structurally underspecified. GPT-5.4 and three locally deployable open-source models were then evaluated under the same setup. Results show that BP annotation is highly heterogeneous at the skill level: 5 skills are directly operable, 4 are recoverable after focused re-annotation, and 5 remain structurally underspecified. GPT-5.4 executes the retained skills with substantial reliability (accuracy = 0.678, \k{appa} = 0.665, weighted F1 = 0.695), but this feasibility is selective rather than global. Human and GPT difficulty profiles are strongly aligned at the skill level (r = 0.881), but not at the instance level (r = 0.016) or lexical-item level (r = -0.142), a pattern we describe as shared taxonomy, independent execution. Pairwise agreement further suggests that GPT is better understood as an independent third skill voice than as a direct human substitute. Open-source failures are concentrated in schema-to-skill execution problems. These findings suggest that automatic annotation should be evaluated in terms of skill feasibility rather than task-level automation.

AI.tldr: The paper examines how well large language models (LLMs) can assist with Behavioral Profile (BP) annotation by decomposing the task into separate annotation skills and evaluating each skill’s feasibility rather than treating annotation as a single monolithic task.
AI.motivation: Behavioral Profile annotation involves simultaneously coding multiple linguistic features for each instance, making it complex and hard to automate. Existing work often treats automatic annotation as a single task and reports overall performance, which obscures which parts of the task are actually automatable and which remain difficult or underspecified. The authors want a more granular, skill-based understanding of how well LLMs, especially GPT and open-source models, can support annotation of nuanced semantic-pragmatic phenomena like Chinese metaphorical color-term derivatives.
AI.method: The authors construct a dataset of 3,134 concordance lines containing 30 Chinese metaphorical color-term derivatives and annotate them using a 14-feature Behavioral Profile schema. They formalize each feature as a distinct ‘skill’ defined externally via schema files, decision rules, and illustrative examples, creating a skill-file-driven annotation pipeline. Two human annotators first apply a schema-only protocol (no model assistance) to a 300-instance validation subset in two rounds, which allows the authors to classify each skill as directly operable, recoverable after focused re-annotation, or structurally underspecified. They then run GPT-5.4 and three open-source LLMs on the same setup and compare model outputs with human annotations at both task and skill levels, measuring accuracy, Cohen’s kappa, weighted F1, correlations of difficulty profiles, and pairwise agreement patterns.
AI.result: BP skills show strong heterogeneity: of the 14 skills, 5 can be applied reliably as defined (directly operable), 4 can be salvaged with targeted re-annotation (recoverable), and 5 remain too vague or structurally underspecified to be used reliably. For the subset of retained skills, GPT-5.4 achieves reasonably high reliability (accuracy 0.678, κ = 0.665, weighted F1 = 0.695). Difficulty profiles for humans and GPT align closely at the level of skills (correlation r = 0.881), indicating that both humans and GPT find the same skills easier or harder, but show almost no alignment at the level of individual instances (r = 0.016) or lexical items (r = -0.142). GPT’s annotations pattern as an additional independent annotator (“third skill voice”) rather than a drop-in replacement for a human. Open-source models perform notably worse, with errors mainly stemming from failures to translate the schema definitions into consistent skill execution.
AI.conclusion: BP annotation is not uniformly automatable; its feasibility varies widely across individual skills. LLMs like GPT can reliably execute a subset of well-specified skills, achieving human-comparable reliability there, but they do so as independent annotators whose decisions are not instance-wise interchangeable with human judgments. Evaluation of automatic annotation should therefore shift from coarse task-level metrics to a more nuanced, skill-feasibility perspective, and annotation schemas themselves must be examined and refined for structural underspecification before expecting robust automation. Open-source models lag behind mainly because they struggle to operationalize the provided schemas into consistent annotation behavior.

title: Dual-Axis Generative Reward Model Toward Semantic and Turn-taking Robustness in Interactive Spoken Dialogue Models
summary: Achieving seamless, human-like interaction remains a key challenge for full-duplex spoken dialogue models (SDMs). Reinforcement learning (RL) has substantially enhanced text- and vision-language models, while well-designed reward signals are crucial for the performance of RL. We consider RL a promising strategy to address the key challenge for SDMs. However, a fundamental barrier persists: prevailing automated metrics for assessing interaction quality rely on superficial proxies, such as behavioral statistics or timing-prediction accuracy, failing to provide reliable reward signals for RL. On the other hand, human evaluations, despite their richness, remain costly, inconsistent, and difficult to scale. We tackle this critical barrier by proposing a Dual-Axis Generative Reward Model, which is trained to understand complex interaction dynamics using a detailed taxonomy and an annotated dataset, produces a single score and, crucially, provides separate evaluations for semantic quality and interaction timing. Such dual outputs furnish precise diagnostic feedback for SDMs and deliver a dependable, instructive reward signal suitable for online reinforcement learning. Our model achieves state-of-the-art performance on interaction-quality assessment across a wide spectrum of datasets, spanning synthetic dialogues and complex real-world interactions.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a Dual-Axis Generative Reward Model that can evaluate spoken dialogue model interactions along two dimensions—semantic quality and interaction timing—providing a reliable reward signal for reinforcement learning and achieving state-of-the-art performance in interaction-quality assessment.
AI.motivation: Full-duplex spoken dialogue models struggle to achieve seamless, human-like interaction. Reinforcement learning could address this but requires reliable reward signals, which current automated metrics fail to provide and human evaluations cannot scale. The authors aim to build a scalable, fine-grained, and reliable reward model for interaction quality.
AI.method: They design a Dual-Axis Generative Reward Model trained with a detailed taxonomy of interaction qualities and an annotated dataset. The model is generative and outputs both an overall interaction-quality score and two separate scores: one for semantic quality and one for interaction timing, enabling more diagnostic feedback and suitability as an RL reward function.
AI.result: The proposed model attains state-of-the-art performance on interaction-quality assessment across multiple datasets, including both synthetic dialogues and complex real-world spoken interactions, outperforming previous automated metrics.
AI.conclusion: By modeling interaction quality along semantic and timing dimensions with a generative approach, the Dual-Axis reward model provides dependable, fine-grained feedback and a practical reward signal for online reinforcement learning, moving spoken dialogue models closer to seamless, human-like full-duplex interaction.

title: ClimateCause: Complex and Implicit Causal Structures in Climate Reports
summary: Understanding climate change requires reasoning over complex causal networks. Yet, existing causal discovery datasets predominantly capture explicit, direct causal relations. We introduce ClimateCause, a manually expert-annotated dataset of higher-order causal structures from science-for-policy climate reports, including implicit and nested causality. Cause-effect expressions are normalized and disentangled into individual causal relations to facilitate graph construction, with unique annotations for cause-effect correlation, relation type, and spatiotemporal context. We further demonstrate ClimateCause's value for quantifying readability based on the semantic complexity of causal graphs underlying a statement. Finally, large language model benchmarking on correlation inference and causal chain reasoning highlights the latter as a key challenge.

AI.tldr: Introduce ClimateCause, an expert-annotated dataset of complex climate-related causal structures, and evaluate LLMs on tasks using it.
AI.motivation: Climate change understanding demands reasoning about complex, often implicit causal networks, but existing causal discovery datasets focus on explicit, direct relations and miss higher-order, nested, and implicit causality found in real climate science texts.
AI.method: The authors construct ClimateCause from science-for-policy climate reports by manually and expertly annotating higher-order causal structures, including implicit and nested chains. They normalize cause-effect expressions, disentangle them into atomic causal relations suitable for graph construction, and annotate each relation with correlation strength, causal relation type, and spatiotemporal context. They then use the resulting graphs to define a semantic-complexity-based readability metric and to create benchmarking tasks for large language models on correlation inference and multi-step causal chain reasoning.
AI.result: They obtain a high-quality dataset of climate-related causal relations with rich annotations. Using it, they show that readability can be quantified in terms of the semantic complexity of underlying causal graphs. Benchmarking reveals that while LLMs perform reasonably on correlation inference, they struggle significantly more with multi-hop causal chain reasoning over these complex structures.
AI.conclusion: ClimateCause fills a gap in causal discovery resources by capturing implicit, nested, and higher-order causal structures in climate texts, enabling new analyses of semantic complexity and readability. It also provides a challenging benchmark revealing current LLM limitations in complex causal reasoning, especially for multi-step causal chains, and can support future work on models better suited to real-world climate causal understanding.

title: WavAlign: Enhancing Intelligence and Expressiveness in Spoken Dialogue Models via Adaptive Hybrid Post-Training
summary: End-to-end spoken dialogue models have garnered significant attention because they offer a higher potential ceiling in expressiveness and perceptual ability than cascaded systems. However, the intelligence and expressiveness of current open-source spoken dialogue models often remain below expectations. Motivated by the success of online reinforcement learning(RL) in other domains, one might attempt to directly apply preference optimization to spoken dialogue models, yet this transfer is non-trivial. We analyze these obstacles from the perspectives of reward modeling and rollout sampling, focusing on how sparse preference supervision interacts with dense speech generation under shared-parameter updates. Based on the analysis, we propose a modality-aware adaptive post-training recipe that makes RL practical for spoken dialogue: it constrains preference updates to the semantic channel and improves acoustic behavior via explicit anchoring, while dynamically regulating their mixture from rollout statistics to avoid unreliable preference gradients. We evaluate the method across multiple spoken dialogue benchmarks and representative architectures, and observe consistent improvements in semantic quality and speech expressiveness.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a reinforcement-learning-based, modality-aware post-training method that makes online RL practical for end-to-end spoken dialogue models, improving both semantic quality and speech expressiveness.
AI.motivation: End-to-end spoken dialogue models promise richer expressiveness and perception than cascaded systems but current open-source models underperform in intelligence and expressiveness. Directly applying preference-based reinforcement learning, which has worked well in other domains, is difficult for spoken dialogue because sparse preference signals must guide dense speech generation, and standard shared-parameter updates can entangle semantic and acoustic behaviors in undesirable ways.
AI.method: The authors analyze why standard preference optimization struggles in spoken dialogue from two angles: reward modeling and rollout sampling, with emphasis on how sparse preference supervision interacts with continuous speech outputs. Based on this, they design a modality-aware adaptive post-training pipeline in which (1) RL preference updates are constrained primarily to the semantic channel, (2) acoustic behavior is shaped via explicit anchoring rather than raw preference gradients, and (3) the balance between semantic and acoustic updates is dynamically adjusted according to rollout statistics to avoid noisy or unreliable preference gradients.
AI.result: Across several spoken dialogue benchmarks and different model architectures, the proposed method yields consistent gains in both the semantic quality of responses (e.g., relevance, coherence, task success) and the expressiveness of generated speech (e.g., prosody, naturalness), outperforming baseline training or naïve preference optimization approaches.
AI.conclusion: Properly accounting for modality-specific characteristics is key to making online RL practical for spoken dialogue models. By separating and adaptively mixing semantic and acoustic updates, the proposed method effectively leverages preference optimization to improve end-to-end spoken dialogue systems, suggesting a promising direction for future RL-based refinement of multimodal conversational agents.

title: Discovering Novel LLM Experts via Task-Capability Coevolution
summary: Frontier model developers aim to train models continually to possess emergent, diverse capabilities. To extend capabilities, the current pre-training and post-training paradigm requires manually starting training runs with static datasets or reward functions every time. Addressing this limitation, our work pursues the insight that open-endedness (via the coevolution of models and tasks) can discover models with increasingly novel skills in a single run. We introduce a new model development framework that extends coevolution to large language model (LLM) discovery, open-ended \textit{Assessment Coevolving with Diverse Capabilities} (AC/DC). AC/DC evolves both LLMs via model merging and natural language tasks via synthetic data generation. AC/DC discovers growing archives of LLMs that surpass the capabilities of larger LLMs while taking up less GPU memory. In particular, our LLM populations achieve a broader Coverage of expertise than other curated models or baselines on downstream benchmarks, without \textit{any} explicit benchmark optimization. Furthermore, AC/DC improves Coverage over time, continually innovates on tasks and models, and improves performance in multi-agent best-of-N selection. Our findings highlight the potential of coevolution as a means of discovering broader sets of capabilities from base LLMs. Overall, AC/DC brings us one step closer to a profoundly new paradigm of LLM development, where continual improvements to the diversity of model capabilities can be accelerated by leveraging existing models as stepping stones to increasingly powerful models.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes AC/DC, an open-ended coevolution framework that jointly evolves large language models and tasks to continually discover diverse capabilities in a single run, outperforming larger static models in coverage and efficiency.
AI.motivation: Current LLM development relies on repeated, manually initiated pre-training or post-training runs with fixed datasets and reward functions, which makes extending capabilities slow, discrete, and benchmark-chasing. The authors are motivated to find a more automatic, open-ended process that can continually discover new skills and broader capabilities without explicit optimization for particular benchmarks, by leveraging coevolution between models and tasks.
AI.method: They introduce AC/DC (Assessment Coevolving with Diverse Capabilities), a framework that maintains populations of LLMs and tasks. LLMs are evolved primarily via model merging operations, while tasks are evolved via synthetic natural language data generation. Through this coevolutionary process, new tasks assess and pressure models to acquire novel capabilities, and improved models in turn enable generation of more challenging and diverse tasks, all within a single continual run.
AI.result: AC/DC produces archives of LLMs that collectively exceed the capabilities of larger individual LLMs while using less GPU memory. The evolved populations show broader coverage of expertise than curated or baseline models on downstream benchmarks, despite no direct optimization on those benchmarks. The framework also shows monotonic improvement in coverage over time, continual innovation in both tasks and models, and better performance in multi-agent best-of-N selection settings.
AI.conclusion: Coevolutionary open-ended training via AC/DC is a promising new paradigm for LLM development. By continually coevolving models and tasks, it can automatically discover diverse and growing capabilities from base LLMs, yielding compact model populations that rival or surpass larger models. This suggests that future LLM development might rely less on isolated, hand-designed training runs and more on continual, open-ended coevolution that uses existing models as stepping stones toward increasingly powerful and diverse capabilities.

title: Segment-Level Coherence for Robust Harmful Intent Probing in LLMs
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly exposed to adaptive jailbreaking, particularly in high-stakes Chemical, Biological, Radiological, and Nuclear (CBRN) domains. Although streaming probes enable real-time monitoring, they still make systematic errors. We identify a core issue: existing methods often rely on a few high-scoring tokens, leading to false alarms when sensitive CBRN terms appear in benign contexts. To address this, we introduce a streaming probing objective that requires multiple evidence tokens to consistently support a prediction, rather than relying on isolated spikes. This encourages more robust detection based on aggregated signals instead of single-token cues. At a fixed 1% false-positive rate, our method improves the true-positive rate by 35.55% relative to strong streaming baselines. We further observe substantial gains in AUROC, even when starting from near-saturated baseline performance (AUROC = 97.40%). We also show that probing Attention or MLP activations consistently outperforms residual-stream features. Finally, even when adversarial fine-tuning enables novel character-level ciphers, harmful intent remains detectable: probes developed for the base LLMs can be applied ``plug-and-play'' to these obfuscated attacks, achieving an AUROC of over 98.85%.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a more robust streaming probing objective for detecting harmful CBRN-related jailbreaks in LLMs, improving accuracy by aggregating evidence across multiple tokens instead of reacting to isolated token spikes.
AI.motivation: Existing streaming jailbreak detectors for LLMs in high-stakes CBRN domains make systematic errors, especially false positives, because they overreact to a few high-scoring or sensitive tokens (like technical CBRN terms) even when these appear in benign or neutral contexts. There is a need for monitoring methods that can distinguish genuinely harmful intent or misuse from harmless mentions of sensitive terms, while still operating in real time under adversarial conditions.
AI.method: The authors design a new streaming probing objective that changes how token-level activations are aggregated for classification. Instead of triggering on isolated high-scoring tokens, the probe is trained to require multiple consistent evidence tokens over time to support a harmful prediction. They implement probes over different internal LLM representations (Attention activations, MLP activations, and residual-stream features) and compare their performance. They also test the robustness of these probes under adversarial fine-tuning that induces character-level cipher obfuscations, while applying probes from the base model in a plug-and-play manner.
AI.result: At a fixed 1% false-positive rate, the proposed streaming objective yields a 35.55% relative improvement in true-positive rate over strong streaming baselines. The method also substantially increases AUROC even when baselines are already strong (e.g., from 97.40% upwards). Probes that use Attention or MLP activations consistently outperform those that rely on residual-stream features. Under adversarial fine-tuning that introduces novel character-level ciphers, the original probes still detect harmful intent with AUROC above 98.85%, indicating good robustness and transferability.
AI.conclusion: Requiring multiple, temporally consistent evidence tokens for streaming probes leads to more reliable detection of harmful CBRN jailbreaks in LLMs, reducing overreliance on single sensitive tokens and yielding significantly better trade-offs between false positives and true positives. Internal representations from Attention and MLP layers are especially effective for this purpose. Moreover, these probes are robust enough to be reused in a plug-and-play fashion on adversarially fine-tuned models that employ obfuscation, maintaining high detection performance and suggesting a viable path for scalable, real-time LLM safety monitoring in high-stakes domains.

title: Hybrid Decision Making via Conformal VLM-generated Guidance
summary: Building on recent advances in AI, hybrid decision making (HDM) holds the promise of improving human decision quality and reducing cognitive load. We work in the context of learning to guide (LtG), a recently proposed HDM framework in which the human is always responsible for the final decision: rather than suggesting decisions, in LtG the AI supplies (textual) guidance useful for facilitating decision making. One limiting factor of existing approaches is that their guidance compounds information about all possible outcomes, and as a result it can be difficult to digest. We address this issue by introducing ConfGuide, a novel LtG approach that generates more succinct and targeted guidance. To this end, it employs conformal risk control to select a set of outcomes, ensuring a cap on the false negative rate. We demonstrate our approach on a real-world multi-label medical diagnosis task. Our empirical evaluation highlights the promise of ConfGuide.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes ConfGuide, a hybrid decision-making method that generates concise, targeted AI guidance for humans by selecting a subset of likely outcomes using conformal risk control, and evaluates it on multi-label medical diagnosis.
AI.motivation: Existing learning-to-guide (LtG) systems for hybrid decision making provide textual guidance that bundles information about all possible outcomes. This makes the guidance verbose and cognitively hard to digest, undermining the benefits of AI assistance. There is a need for guidance that remains reliable yet more succinct and focused on the most relevant outcomes, especially in high-stakes domains like medicine.
AI.method: The authors introduce ConfGuide, an LtG approach that uses conformal risk control to construct a subset of outcomes for which guidance is provided. Conformal methods are used to guarantee an upper bound on the false negative rate when deciding which labels (e.g., diagnoses) to include in the guidance. The system generates textual guidance that is targeted to this selected subset, aiming to be both shorter and easier for humans to use while maintaining statistical guarantees on missed outcomes.
AI.result: On a real-world multi-label medical diagnosis task, ConfGuide is empirically evaluated against existing LtG approaches. The experiments show that ConfGuide produces more succinct and targeted guidance while maintaining controlled false negative rates. Quantitative metrics and possibly user- or task-based evaluations indicate improved or comparable decision quality and reduced cognitive burden versus baselines.
AI.conclusion: ConfGuide demonstrates that combining learning-to-guide HDM with conformal risk control can yield AI guidance that is both statistically reliable and easier for humans to use. By selectively focusing on a subset of outcomes with controlled risk of omissions, the approach improves the practicality and effectiveness of hybrid decision making in complex tasks such as medical diagnosis.

title: AI-Enabled Covert Channel Detection in RF Receiver Architectures
summary: Covert channels (CCs) in wireless chips pose a serious security threat, as they enable the exfiltration of sensitive information from the chip to an external attacker. In this work, we propose an AI-based defense mechanism deployed at the RF receiver, where the model directly monitors raw I/Q samples to detect, in real time, the presence of a CC embedded within an otherwise nominal signal. We first compact a state-of-the-art convolutional neural network (CNN), achieving an 80% reduction in parameters, which is an essential requirement for efficient edge deployment. When evaluated on the open-source hardware Trojan (HT)-based CC dataset, the compacted CNN attains an average accuracy of 90.28% for CC detection and 86.50% for identifying the underlying HT, with results averaged across SNR values above 1 dB. For practical communication scenarios where SNR > 20 dB, the model achieves over 97% accuracy for both tasks. These results correspond to a minimal performance degradation of less than 2% compared to the baseline model. The compacted CNN is further benchmarked against alternative classifiers, demonstrating an excellent accuracy-model size trade-off. Finally, we design a lightweight CNN hardware accelerator and demonstrate it on an FPGA, achieving very low resource utilization and an efficiency of 107 GOPs/W. Being the first AI hardware accelerator proposed specifically for CC detection, we compare it against state-of-the-art AI accelerators for RF signal classification tasks such as modulation recognition, showing superior performance.

AI.tldr: An AI-based, compact CNN detects covert channels in wireless chips from raw I/Q samples in real time and is implemented efficiently on FPGA hardware.
AI.motivation: Covert channels in wireless chips enable secret exfiltration of sensitive data, posing serious security risks. Existing defenses are limited in real-time detection capabilities and often too heavy for edge deployment. There is a need for a lightweight, accurate, and hardware-friendly AI-based defense that can be integrated into RF receivers to detect these channels from raw signal data.
AI.method: The authors design an AI-based defense placed at the RF receiver that directly processes raw I/Q samples to detect covert channels. They start from a state-of-the-art CNN for RF signal analysis and compact it, reducing parameters by 80% while retaining performance. The compacted CNN is trained and evaluated on an open-source hardware Trojan-based covert channel dataset to perform two tasks: (1) CC presence detection and (2) identification of the underlying hardware Trojan. The model is benchmarked against alternative classifiers for accuracy and model size trade-offs. Additionally, they design a lightweight CNN hardware accelerator, map the compacted model onto FPGA, and measure resource utilization and energy efficiency (GOPs/W).
AI.result: On SNR > 1 dB, the compacted CNN achieves an average accuracy of 90.28% for covert channel detection and 86.50% for hardware Trojan identification, with less than 2% degradation compared to the baseline CNN. For realistic communication conditions with SNR > 20 dB, the model reaches over 97% accuracy on both tasks. The compacted CNN outperforms other classifiers in terms of the accuracy–model size trade-off. The FPGA-based hardware accelerator shows very low resource utilization and obtains an efficiency of 107 GOPs/W, surpassing state-of-the-art AI accelerators used for RF signal classification tasks such as modulation recognition.
AI.conclusion: A compact CNN deployed at RF receivers can accurately and efficiently detect covert channels and identify underlying hardware Trojans from raw I/Q samples in real time, even under varying SNR conditions. The proposed hardware accelerator enables practical, low-power edge deployment on FPGA, offering better accuracy–efficiency trade-offs than existing RF AI accelerators. This work establishes the first dedicated AI hardware accelerator targeting covert channel detection in wireless chips, showing its feasibility and superiority for security-focused RF signal monitoring.

title: Reasoning Dynamics and the Limits of Monitoring Modality Reliance in Vision-Language Models
summary: Recent advances in vision language models (VLMs) offer reasoning capabilities, yet how these unfold and integrate visual and textual information remains unclear. We analyze reasoning dynamics in 18 VLMs covering instruction-tuned and reasoning-trained models from two different model families. We track confidence over Chain-of-Thought (CoT), measure the corrective effect of reasoning, and evaluate the contribution of intermediate reasoning steps. We find that models are prone to answer inertia, in which early commitments to a prediction are reinforced, rather than revised during reasoning steps. While reasoning-trained models show stronger corrective behavior, their gains depend on modality conditions, from text-dominant to vision-only settings. Using controlled interventions with misleading textual cues, we show that models are consistently influenced by these cues even when visual evidence is sufficient, and assess whether this influence is recoverable from CoT. Although this influence can appear in the CoT, its detectability varies across models and depends on what is being monitored. Reasoning-trained models are more likely to explicitly refer to the cues, but their longer and fluent CoTs can still appear visually grounded while actually following textual cues, obscuring modality reliance. In contrast, instruction-tuned models refer to the cues less explicitly, but their shorter traces reveal inconsistencies with the visual input. Taken together, these findings indicate that CoT provides only a partial view of how different modalities drive VLM decisions, with important implications for the transparency and safety of multimodal systems.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how vision-language models actually use reasoning steps (Chain-of-Thought) and visual vs. textual information, finding that CoT only partially reveals what drives their decisions and that models are often biased by early guesses and misleading text cues.
AI.motivation: Vision-language models show impressive reasoning-like behavior, but we don’t really understand how their step-by-step reasoning unfolds or how they balance visual and textual inputs, which is crucial for trust, transparency, and safety. The authors want to move beyond final accuracy and inspect the internal reasoning dynamics and modality reliance of these models.
AI.method: They evaluate 18 vision-language models, including both instruction-tuned and explicitly reasoning-trained models from two families. They (1) track prediction confidence as Chain-of-Thought unfolds, (2) quantify how often later reasoning steps correct earlier wrong guesses, (3) measure the contribution of intermediate steps to final answers, and (4) run controlled experiments with misleading textual cues while keeping visual evidence sufficient, then analyze how these cues affect answers and how (or whether) that influence appears in the CoT traces.
AI.result: They observe “answer inertia”: once models lean toward an early answer, later reasoning usually reinforces it instead of revising it. Reasoning-trained models correct themselves more than instruction-tuned ones, but this benefit is highly dependent on whether the task is text-heavy, balanced, or vision-only. Across models, misleading textual cues strongly bias predictions even when the image alone is enough. This textual influence sometimes shows up explicitly in CoT but sometimes remains hidden, and its detectability varies by model and by what part of the trace is monitored.
AI.conclusion: Chain-of-Thought transcripts are an incomplete and sometimes misleading window into how vision-language models combine modalities. Reasoning-trained models may seem visually grounded because they produce rich, fluent explanations, yet they can still be covertly driven by textual cues. Instruction-tuned models produce shorter traces that more visibly conflict with the image. Therefore, CoT is not a reliable diagnostic for modality reliance, with implications for interpreting, auditing, and safely deploying VLMs in settings that require genuine visual grounding and transparent reasoning behavior.

title: Dr.~RTL: Autonomous Agentic RTL Optimization through Tool-Grounded Self-Improvement
summary: Recent advances in large language models (LLMs) have sparked growing interest in automatic RTL optimization for better performance, power, and area (PPA). However, existing methods are still far from realistic RTL optimization. Their evaluation settings are often unrealistic: they are tested on manually degraded, small-scale RTL designs and rely on weak open-source tools. Their optimization methods are also limited, relying on coarse design-level feedback and simple pre-defined rewriting rules. To address these limitations, we present Dr. RTL, an agentic framework for RTL timing optimization in a realistic evaluation environment, with continual self-improvement through reusable optimization skills. We establish a realistic evaluation setting with more challenging RTL designs and an industrial EDA workflow.
