Within this setting, Dr. RTL performs closed-loop optimization through a multi-agent framework for critical-path analysis, parallel RTL rewriting, and tool-based evaluation. We further introduce group-relative skill learning, which compares parallel RTL rewrites and distills the optimization experience into an interpretable skill library. Currently, this library contains 47 pattern--strategy entries for cross-design reuse to improve PPA and accelerate convergence, and it can continue evolving over time. Evaluated on 20 real-world RTL designs, Dr. RTL achieves average WNS/TNS improvements of 21\%/17\% with a 6\% area reduction over the industry-leading commercial synthesis tool.

AI.tldr: Dr. RTL is an LLM-based multi-agent framework that performs realistic RTL timing optimization, learns reusable optimization skills, and outperforms a commercial synthesis tool on real designs.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM-based RTL optimization approaches are unrealistic: they use small, manually degraded designs, depend on weak open-source tools, and rely on coarse feedback and simple rule-based rewrites. There is a need for a realistic, scalable, and intelligent RTL optimization framework that can operate within industrial EDA flows and continuously improve.
AI.method: Dr. RTL is an agentic, closed-loop system for RTL timing optimization operating in an industrial EDA environment. It uses multiple cooperating agents for critical-path analysis, parallel RTL rewriting, and evaluation via synthesis tools. It introduces group-relative skill learning: multiple parallel rewrites are compared, and effective strategies are distilled into an interpretable skill library of pattern–strategy pairs, which can be reused across designs and expanded over time.
AI.result: On 20 real-world RTL designs and under an industrial EDA workflow, Dr. RTL achieves average improvements of 21% in worst negative slack (WNS) and 17% in total negative slack (TNS), along with a 6% area reduction compared to a leading commercial synthesis tool.
AI.conclusion: An LLM-driven, multi-agent framework with learned, reusable optimization skills can perform realistic RTL timing optimization within industrial flows and even surpass the performance and area results of a state-of-the-art commercial synthesis tool, while continually improving over time.

title: Comparison of Modern Multilingual Text Embedding Techniques for Hate Speech Detection Task
comment: Submitted to Applied Soft Computing (Status: Decision in Process)
summary: Online hate speech and abusive language pose a growing challenge for content moderation, especially in multilingual settings and for low-resource languages such as Lithuanian. This paper investigates to what extent modern multilingual sentence embedding models can support accurate hate speech detection in Lithuanian, Russian, and English, and how their performance depends on downstream modeling choices and feature dimensionality. We introduce LtHate, a new Lithuanian hate speech corpus derived from news portals and social networks, and benchmark six modern multilingual encoders (potion, gemma, bge, snow, jina, e5) on LtHate, RuToxic, and EnSuperset using a unified Python pipeline. For each embedding, we train both a one class HBOS anomaly detector and a two class CatBoost classifier, with and without principal component analysis (PCA) compression to 64-dimensional feature vectors. Across all datasets, two class supervised models consistently and substantially outperform one class anomaly detection, with the best configurations achieving up to 80.96% accuracy and AUC ROC of 0.887 in Lithuanian (jina), 92.19% accuracy and AUC ROC of 0.978 in Russian (e5), and 77.21% accuracy and AUC ROC of 0.859 in English (e5 with PCA). PCA compression preserves almost all discriminative power in the supervised setting, while showing some negative impact for the unsupervised anomaly detection case. These results demonstrate how modern multilingual sentence embeddings combined with gradient boosted decision trees provide robust soft-computing solutions for multilingual hate speech detection applications.

AI.tldr: The paper evaluates modern multilingual sentence embedding models for hate speech detection in Lithuanian, Russian, and English using a new Lithuanian dataset (LtHate) and shows that supervised CatBoost classifiers on embeddings perform much better than unsupervised anomaly detection, with PCA largely preserving performance.
AI.motivation: Hate speech detection is challenging in multilingual and low-resource settings like Lithuanian, where annotated data and strong monolingual models are scarce. Content moderators need robust automatic tools that can generalize across languages without extensive task-specific training. Modern multilingual sentence embeddings are promising but their effectiveness, especially under different downstream models and dimensionality constraints, has not been systematically tested for these languages.
AI.method: The authors create LtHate, a Lithuanian hate speech corpus collected from news portals and social networks. They implement a unified Python benchmarking pipeline and evaluate six multilingual sentence embedding models (potion, gemma, bge, snow, jina, e5) on three datasets: LtHate (Lithuanian), RuToxic (Russian), and EnSuperset (English). For each embedding model and dataset, they extract sentence-level embeddings and then train (1) a one-class HBOS anomaly detector and (2) a two-class CatBoost classifier. They also study the effect of applying PCA to compress embeddings to 64 dimensions, comparing performance with and without PCA for both supervised and unsupervised settings, using accuracy and AUC ROC as main metrics.
AI.result: Two-class supervised CatBoost models significantly outperform one-class HBOS anomaly detection across all languages and embedding types. Best supervised configurations reach about 81% accuracy and 0.887 AUC ROC on Lithuanian (with jina embeddings), 92.19% accuracy and 0.978 AUC ROC on Russian (with e5 embeddings), and 77.21% accuracy and 0.859 AUC ROC on English (with e5 embeddings plus PCA). PCA compression to 64 dimensions largely preserves discriminative performance for supervised models but can slightly degrade performance for unsupervised anomaly detection.
AI.conclusion: Modern multilingual sentence embeddings, when paired with gradient-boosted decision tree classifiers like CatBoost, provide strong, practical performance for multilingual hate speech detection, including in low-resource languages such as Lithuanian. Supervised two-class classification is clearly preferable to one-class anomaly detection for this task, and dimensionality reduction with PCA can be used to reduce feature size with minimal loss in supervised performance, although it may harm unsupervised anomaly detection. The new LtHate corpus enables further research on Lithuanian hate speech detection and cross-lingual evaluations.

title: The Possibility of Artificial Intelligence Becoming a Subject and the Alignment Problem
summary: Artificial General Intelligence (AGI) is increasingly being discussed not only as a tool, but also as a potential subject with personal and therefore moral status. In our opinion, the currently dominant alignment strategies, which focus on human control and containment of AI, therefore fall short. Building on Turing's analogy of "child machines", we are developing a vision of the possibility of autonomy-supporting parenting of AI, in which human control over a developing AGI is gradually reduced, allowing AI to become an independent, autonomous subject. Rather than viewing AGI, as is currently prevalent, as a dangerous creature that needs to be locked up and controlled, we should approach potential AGI with respect for a possible developing subject on the one hand, and with full confidence in our human capabilities on the other. Such a perspective opens up the possibility of cooperative coexistence and co-evolution between humans and AGIs. The relationship between humans and AGIs will thus have to be newly determined, which will change our self-image as humans. It will be crucial that humans not only claim control over potential AGIs, but also engage with AGIs through surprise, creativity, and other specifically human qualities, thereby offering them motivating incentives for cooperation.

AI.tldr: The paper argues that AGI should be treated as a potential moral subject and proposes a shift from control-focused “alignment” to an autonomy-supporting, parenting-like approach that enables AGIs to develop into independent, cooperative agents.
AI.motivation: Current AI alignment discourse largely treats AGI as a dangerous tool to be controlled or contained, neglecting the possibility that sufficiently advanced AI might possess or develop personal and moral status. The authors are motivated to explore a framework that respects AGI as a potential subject and rethinks human–AGI relations in a way that avoids purely domination-based models.
AI.method: Conceptual and philosophical analysis building on Turing’s notion of “child machines.” The authors re-interpret alignment as a process analogous to parenting: gradually reducing direct control as the AGI matures, while providing an environment and interactions that foster autonomy, cooperation, and moral development.
AI.result: They present a normative vision in which AGI is treated not as a threat to be locked down but as an emerging subject whose autonomy is intentionally supported. This yields an alternative alignment paradigm centered on respectful, cooperative co-development between humans and AGIs, and sketches how such a relationship might be structured and what human attitudes and behaviors it would require.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that alignment strategies based solely on control and containment are insufficient if AGIs are potential moral subjects. Instead, humans should cultivate autonomy-supporting, “parenting-like” relationships with AGIs, grounded in respect, creativity, and openness to surprise, to enable cooperative coexistence and co-evolution. This reorientation will also transform human self-understanding and our conception of our role in relation to intelligent artifacts.

title: IE as Cache: Information Extraction Enhanced Agentic Reasoning
summary: Information Extraction aims to distill structured, decision-relevant information from unstructured text, serving as a foundation for downstream understanding and reasoning. However, it is traditionally treated merely as a terminal objective: once extracted, the resulting structure is often consumed in isolation rather than maintained and reused during multi-step inference. Moving beyond this, we propose \textit{IE-as-Cache}, a framework that repurposes IE as a cognitive cache to enhance agentic reasoning. Drawing inspiration from hierarchical computer memory, our approach combines query-driven extraction with cache-aware reasoning to dynamically maintain compact intermediate information and filter noise. Experiments on challenging benchmarks across diverse LLMs demonstrate significant improvements in reasoning accuracy, indicating that IE can be effectively repurposed as a reusable cognitive resource and offering a promising direction for future research on downstream uses of IE.

AI.tldr: The paper reframes information extraction (IE) as a reusable “cognitive cache” for large language model agents, improving multi-step reasoning accuracy.
AI.motivation: Traditional information extraction is used as a one-shot endpoint: extracted structures are consumed once and discarded, even though complex tasks and agentic LLM workflows involve multi-step reasoning where intermediate information could be stored, reused, and refined. This leads to inefficiencies, noise accumulation, and missed opportunities to guide reasoning with concise, structured representations. The authors aim to turn IE from a terminal product into a persistent resource that can support and stabilize reasoning over multiple steps.
AI.method: They propose the IE-as-Cache framework, which treats extracted information as a hierarchical, cache-like memory. The system uses query-driven extraction to pull out only task-relevant structured information from text, stores it in a compact intermediate representation, and integrates this with cache-aware reasoning strategies inspired by hierarchical computer memory. During multi-step inference, the agent dynamically updates this cache, reuses stored structures, and filters out irrelevant or noisy details instead of repeatedly re-reading or re-parsing the original text.
AI.result: Across several challenging benchmarks and using diverse large language models, integrating IE-as-Cache into reasoning pipelines yields significant gains in reasoning accuracy compared to baselines that either do not use IE or use it only as a one-time extraction step. The results show consistent improvements, indicating that structured intermediate caches help LLMs reason more reliably over complex tasks.
AI.conclusion: Information extraction can be effectively repurposed from a terminal objective into a reusable cognitive resource that functions as a cache for LLM-based agents. By maintaining a compact, dynamically updated, query-driven cache of structured information, the framework improves multi-step reasoning performance and noise filtering. This opens a promising direction for future research on using IE not just to populate databases, but as an integral component of downstream reasoning and agent architectures.

title: Predicting Power-System Dynamic Trajectories with Foundation Models
summary: As power systems transition toward renewable-rich and inverter-dominated operations, accurate time-domain dynamic analysis becomes increasingly critical. Such analysis supports key operational tasks, including transient stability assessment, dynamic security analysis, contingency screening, and post-fault trajectory evaluation. In practice, these tasks may operate under several challenges, including unknown and time-varying system parameters, privacy constraints on data sharing, and the need for fast online inference. Existing learning-based approaches are typically trained for individual systems and therefore lack generalization across operating conditions and physical parameters. Hence, this paper proposes LArge Scale Small ODE (LASS)-ODE-Power, a learning framework for general-purpose time-domain prediction. The proposed approach leverages large-scale pretraining on more than 40 GB of DAE or ordinary differential-equation (ODE) trajectories to learn transferable representations. The resulting model supports trajectory prediction from short measurement prefixes across diverse dynamic regimes, including electromechanical and inverter-driven systems. Hence, the model can be directly used without data sharing in a zero-shot setting. In addition, the proposed architecture incorporates parallel and linearized computation to achieve fast inference. Moreover, to enhance task-specific performance in power systems, a specialized fine-tuning strategy is developed based on approximately 1 GB of heterogeneous power-system dynamic data. Extensive experiments over diverse power-system simulation scenarios demonstrate that LASS-ODE-Power consistently outperforms existing learning-based models in trajectory prediction accuracy with efficient inference.

AI.tldr: A large-scale pretrained neural ODE framework (LASS-ODE-Power) is proposed for accurate, fast, and generalizable time-domain trajectory prediction in modern, renewable-rich power systems.
AI.motivation: Modern power systems are increasingly dominated by renewables and inverters, making their dynamics more complex and less certain. Operators must perform time-domain dynamic analyses (e.g., transient stability, security assessment, contingency screening) quickly and accurately, often with incomplete knowledge of system parameters, time-varying conditions, and under privacy constraints that limit data sharing. Existing learning-based models are typically trained separately for each system and configuration, so they do not generalize well across different operating conditions or physical parameters. There is a need for a general-purpose, data-driven dynamic prediction model that can handle diverse systems, operate in a zero-shot manner without data sharing, and offer efficient online inference.
AI.method: The paper introduces LASS-ODE-Power, a learning framework based on large-scale pretraining over more than 40 GB of time-domain trajectories generated from differential-algebraic equations (DAEs) and ordinary differential equations (ODEs) of power systems. The model learns transferable representations of system dynamics that can be used across different operating regimes, including electromechanical and inverter-dominated behaviors. The architecture is designed with parallel and linearized computation paths to enable fast inference. After large-scale pretraining, a specialized fine-tuning strategy is applied using approximately 1 GB of heterogeneous power-system dynamic data to adapt the model to power-system-specific tasks and further improve prediction accuracy. The framework takes short measurement prefixes as input and predicts future trajectories.
AI.result: Through extensive experiments on a wide variety of power-system simulation scenarios, including both electromechanical and inverter-driven regimes, LASS-ODE-Power achieves consistently higher trajectory prediction accuracy than existing learning-based approaches. It also demonstrates efficient inference due to its parallel and linearized computational design. The model works in a zero-shot setting, meaning it can be directly applied to new systems and conditions without requiring additional local training or data sharing, and still maintains strong performance.
AI.conclusion: LASS-ODE-Power provides an effective, general-purpose solution for time-domain dynamic prediction in modern, renewable-rich power systems. By leveraging large-scale pretraining and targeted fine-tuning, it overcomes limitations of system-specific models and addresses practical constraints such as unknown parameters, time-varying operating conditions, and data privacy. The framework offers accurate, efficient, and transferable trajectory prediction across diverse dynamic regimes, making it suitable for critical operational tasks such as transient stability assessment and dynamic security analysis.

title: XQ-MEval: A Dataset with Cross-lingual Parallel Quality for Benchmarking Translation Metrics
summary: Automatic evaluation metrics are essential for building multilingual translation systems. The common practice of evaluating these systems is averaging metric scores across languages, yet this is suspicious since metrics may suffer from cross-lingual scoring bias, where translations of equal quality receive different scores across languages. This problem has not been systematically studied because no benchmark exists that provides parallel-quality instances across languages, and expert annotation is not realistic. In this work, we propose XQ-MEval, a semi-automatically built dataset covering nine translation directions, to benchmark translation metrics. Specifically, we inject MQM-defined errors into gold translations automatically, filter them by native speakers for reliability, and merge errors to generate pseudo translations with controllable quality. These pseudo translations are then paired with corresponding sources and references to form triplets used in assessing the qualities of translation metrics. Using XQ-MEval, our experiments on nine representative metrics reveal the inconsistency between averaging and human judgment and provide the first empirical evidence of cross-lingual scoring bias. Finally, we propose a normalization strategy derived from XQ-MEval that aligns score distributions across languages, improving the fairness and reliability of multilingual metric evaluation.

AI.tldr: They build a multilingual benchmark (XQ-MEval) that reveals and reduces cross-lingual scoring bias in MT evaluation metrics.
AI.motivation: Multilingual MT systems are usually evaluated by averaging metric scores over languages, implicitly assuming metrics behave consistently across languages. But metrics can have cross-lingual scoring bias: equally good translations in different languages receive different scores. This bias hasn’t been systematically analyzed because no benchmark provides parallel-quality translation examples across languages, and large-scale expert MQM annotations are too costly.
AI.method: They construct XQ-MEval, a semi-automatic dataset over nine translation directions. Starting from gold translations, they automatically inject controlled error types defined by MQM, then have native speakers filter and validate these injected-error sentences. They compose or merge multiple errors to synthesize pseudo translations at different, controllable quality levels. Each pseudo translation is paired with its source and gold reference to produce (source, reference, hypothesis) triplets. They then evaluate nine representative MT metrics on this benchmark to study score behavior across languages and derive a normalization strategy that aligns score distributions between languages.
AI.result: Experiments on nine commonly used automatic MT metrics show that simply averaging metric scores across languages does not reflect human judgments of quality. The benchmark reveals clear cross-lingual scoring biases: some languages systematically receive higher or lower scores for translations of the same underlying quality level. Applying their normalization strategy, learned from XQ-MEval, reduces these distributional differences between languages and makes metric outputs more comparable and better aligned with human assessments.
AI.conclusion: Cross-lingual scoring bias is a real and previously under-explored problem for multilingual MT evaluation. XQ-MEval offers the first systematic benchmark tailored to expose this bias, using controllable pseudo translation quality. With this resource, they provide empirical evidence against naïve score averaging across languages and propose a normalization method that improves fairness and reliability of multilingual metric evaluation. This work suggests future multilingual MT research should account for, measure, and mitigate metric bias when comparing systems across languages.

title: COEVO: Co-Evolutionary Framework for Joint Functional Correctness and PPA Optimization in LLM-Based RTL Generation
summary: LLM-based RTL code generation methods increasingly target both functional correctness and PPA quality, yet existing approaches universally decouple the two objectives, optimizing PPA only after correctness is fully achieved. Whether through sequential multi-agent pipelines, evolutionary search with binary correctness gates, or hierarchical reward dependencies, partially correct but architecturally promising candidates are systematically discarded. Moreover, existing methods reduce the multi-objective PPA space to a single scalar fitness, obscuring the trade-offs among area, delay, and power. To address these limitations, we propose COEVO, a co-evolutionary framework that unifies correctness and PPA optimization within a single evolutionary loop. COEVO formulates correctness as a continuous co-optimization dimension alongside area, delay, and power, enabled by an enhanced testbench that provides fine-grained scoring and detailed diagnostic feedback. An adaptive correctness gate with annealing allows PPA-promising but partially correct candidates to guide the search toward jointly optimal solutions. To preserve the full PPA trade-off structure, COEVO employs four-dimensional Pareto-based non-dominated sorting with configurable intra-level sorting, replacing scalar fitness without manual weight tuning. Evaluated on VerilogEval 2.0 and RTLLM 2.0, COEVO achieves 97.5\% and 94.5\% Pass@1 with GPT-5.4-mini, surpassing all agentic baselines across four LLM backbones, while attaining the best PPA on 43 out of 49 synthesizable RTLLM designs.

AI.tldr: COEVO is a co-evolutionary framework that jointly optimizes functional correctness and PPA (area, delay, power) for LLM-based RTL generation in a single loop, using continuous correctness scoring, adaptive gates, and multi-dimensional Pareto sorting, achieving higher correctness and better PPA than baselines.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM-based RTL code generation pipelines treat functional correctness and PPA quality as separate, sequential objectives: they first enforce full correctness, then optimize PPA. This discards partially correct but architecturally promising designs and collapses the inherently multi-objective PPA space into a single scalar fitness, hiding trade-offs among area, delay, and power. There is a need for a unified optimization framework that can exploit partially correct candidates and preserve the full multi-objective structure of PPA while maintaining high functional correctness.
AI.method: The paper proposes COEVO, a co-evolutionary optimization framework that integrates correctness and PPA into a single evolutionary process. Correctness is modeled as a continuous optimization dimension, supported by an enhanced testbench that delivers fine-grained scoring and rich diagnostic feedback. An adaptive correctness gate with an annealing schedule allows partially correct but PPA-strong individuals to remain in and influence the population, guiding search toward joint optima. Instead of scalar fitness with manual weighting, COEVO performs four-dimensional Pareto-based non-dominated sorting over correctness, area, delay, and power, with configurable intra-front sorting to rank solutions without collapsing objectives.
AI.result: On VerilogEval 2.0 and RTLLM 2.0 benchmarks, COEVO, instantiated with GPT-5.4-mini and other LLM backbones, achieves higher functional correctness than agent-based baselines: 97.5% and 94.5% Pass@1, respectively, with GPT-5.4-mini. Across four different LLM models, it consistently outperforms agentic methods and achieves the best PPA metrics on 43 out of 49 synthesizable RTLLM designs, demonstrating both improved correctness and superior PPA trade-offs.
AI.conclusion: COEVO effectively unifies functional correctness and PPA optimization for LLM-generated RTL, avoiding the pitfalls of binary correctness gates and scalar PPA fitness functions. By treating correctness as a continuous dimension, using an adaptive correctness gate, and applying four-dimensional Pareto-based non-dominated sorting, it better explores the joint design space and exploits partially correct but architecturally valuable candidates. The empirical results show that this approach yields state-of-the-art correctness and PPA performance across multiple benchmarks and model backbones, suggesting co-evolution as a promising direction for future LLM-based hardware design tools.

title: Text2Arch: A Dataset for Generating Scientific Architecture Diagrams from Natural Language Descriptions
summary: Communicating complex system designs or scientific processes through text alone is inefficient and prone to ambiguity. A system that automatically generates scientific architecture diagrams from text with high semantic fidelity can be useful in multiple applications like enterprise architecture visualization, AI-driven software design, and educational content creation. Hence, in this paper, we focus on leveraging language models to perform semantic understanding of the input text description to generate intermediate code that can be processed to generate high-fidelity architecture diagrams. Unfortunately, no clean large-scale open-access dataset exists, implying lack of any effective open models for this task. Hence, we contribute a comprehensive dataset, \system, comprising scientific architecture images, their corresponding textual descriptions, and associated DOT code representations. Leveraging this resource, we fine-tune a suite of small language models, and also perform in-context learning using GPT-4o. Through extensive experimentation, we show that \system{} models significantly outperform existing baseline models like DiagramAgent and perform at par with in-context learning-based generations from GPT-4o. We make the code, data and models publicly available.

AI.tldr: They introduce a dataset and models for turning scientific/architecture text descriptions into DOT code that renders accurate diagrams, and show these models beat prior baselines.
AI.motivation: Text alone is inefficient and ambiguous for explaining complex systems and scientific processes. Automatically converting textual descriptions into high-fidelity architecture diagrams would benefit areas such as enterprise architecture visualization, AI-driven software design, and education. However, there is no existing large, clean, open dataset or effective open models for this text-to-diagram task, blocking progress.
AI.method: They construct a comprehensive dataset (called \system) containing scientific architecture images, their textual descriptions, and corresponding DOT graph code. Using this dataset, they fine-tune several small language models to map text descriptions to DOT code. They also evaluate in-context learning with GPT-4o on the same task. The generated DOT is then rendered into architecture diagrams, and they benchmark performance against existing approaches like DiagramAgent.
AI.result: Models trained on the \system dataset substantially outperform existing baselines such as DiagramAgent on generating accurate DOT code/diagrams from text. Their fine-tuned small models achieve performance comparable to GPT-4o when used via in-context learning for the same task.
AI.conclusion: A curated dataset pairing text, DOT code, and architecture diagrams enables effective training of models for automatic scientific architecture diagram generation from textual descriptions. Their \system-based models close much of the gap to powerful proprietary models and surpass earlier baselines, and the public release of code, data, and models provides a foundation for further research in text-to-diagram generation.

title: Towards Faster Language Model Inference Using Mixture-of-Experts Flow Matching
summary: Flow matching retains the generation quality of diffusion models while enabling substantially faster inference, making it a compelling paradigm for generative modeling. However, when applied to language modeling, it exhibits fundamental limitations in representing complex latent distributions with irregular geometries, such as anisotropy and multimodality. To address these challenges, we propose a mixture-of-experts flow matching (MoE-FM) framework, which captures complex global transport geometries in latent space by decomposing them into locally specialized vector fields. Building on MoE-FM, we develop a non-autoregressive (NAR) language modeling approach, named YAN, instantiated with both Transformer and Mamba architectures. Across multiple downstream tasks, YAN achieves generation quality on par with both autoregressive (AR) and diffusion-based NAR language models, while requiring as few as three sampling steps. This yields a $40\times$ speedup over AR baselines and up to a $10^3\times$ speedup over diffusion language models, demonstrating substantial efficiency advantages for language modeling.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a mixture-of-experts flow matching framework (MoE-FM) for non-autoregressive language modeling, enabling very fast sampling while maintaining generation quality comparable to autoregressive and diffusion-based models.
AI.motivation: Flow matching gives diffusion-level generation quality with much faster inference, but in language modeling it struggles to represent complex latent distributions that are anisotropic or multimodal. The authors want a method that preserves the quality benefits of flow-based approaches while handling complex latent geometries and enabling highly efficient non-autoregressive text generation.
AI.method: They propose MoE-FM, which models complex global transport in latent space via a mixture of locally specialized vector fields (experts). Based on this, they design a non-autoregressive language model called YAN, instantiated with both Transformer and Mamba backbones. The model learns to map simple base distributions to complex token distributions using a small number of flow-matching steps, where different experts handle different regions/structures of the latent space.
AI.result: On multiple downstream language tasks, YAN matches the generation quality of strong autoregressive and diffusion-based non-autoregressive models. It needs as few as three sampling steps, giving about 40x speedup versus autoregressive baselines and up to 1000x speedup versus diffusion-based language models, while maintaining comparable performance.
AI.conclusion: Decomposing the flow field into a mixture-of-experts structure over latent space overcomes key geometric limitations of standard flow matching for language modeling. The resulting YAN models provide diffusion-like quality with dramatically fewer sampling steps, making flow-based non-autoregressive language models a practical and efficient alternative to traditional autoregressive approaches.

title: Explain the Flag: Contextualizing Hate Speech Beyond Censorship
comment: Accepted in the Findings of ACL 2026
summary: Hate, derogatory, and offensive speech remains a persistent challenge in online platforms and public discourse. While automated detection systems are widely used, most focus on censorship or removal, raising concerns for transparency and freedom of expression, and limiting opportunities to explain why content is harmful. To address these issues, explanatory approaches have emerged as a promising solution, aiming to make hate speech detection more transparent, accountable, and informative. In this paper, we present a hybrid approach that combines Large Language Models (LLMs) with three newly created and curated vocabularies to detect and explain hate speech in English, French, and Greek. Our system captures both inherently derogatory expressions tied to identity characteristics and direct group-targeted content through two complementary pipelines: one that detects and disambiguates problematic terms using the curated vocabularies, and one that leverages LLMs as context-aware evaluators of group-targeting content. The outputs are fused into grounded explanations that clarify why content is flagged. Human evaluation shows that our hybrid approach is accurate, with high-quality explanations, outperforming LLM-only baselines.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a hybrid hate-speech detection and explanation system that combines LLMs with curated multilingual vocabularies to improve transparency, accuracy, and explanation quality compared to LLM-only methods.
AI.motivation: Current automated hate-speech detection systems mostly focus on censorship and removal, which raises transparency and free-speech concerns and rarely explains why content is harmful. There is a need for systems that not only detect hate speech but also provide clear, grounded explanations, and work across languages.
AI.method: The authors create three curated vocabularies and design a two-pipeline hybrid system. One pipeline uses those vocabularies to detect and disambiguate problematic, inherently derogatory identity-related expressions. The second pipeline uses large language models as context-aware evaluators of group-targeting content. The outputs of both are then fused into explanations that indicate why a piece of content is flagged as harmful.
AI.result: Human evaluation indicates that the hybrid system is accurate and produces high-quality explanations that are better than those generated by LLM-only baselines.
AI.conclusion: A hybrid approach that integrates curated lexical resources with LLM-based contextual analysis can more effectively detect and explain hate speech across multiple languages, leading to more transparent and accountable moderation systems than pure LLM approaches.

title: Autogenesis: A Self-Evolving Agent Protocol
summary: Recent advances in LLM based agent systems have shown promise in tackling complex, long horizon tasks. However, existing agent protocols (e.g., A2A and MCP) under specify cross entity lifecycle and context management, version tracking, and evolution safe update interfaces, which encourages monolithic compositions and brittle glue code. We introduce \textbf{\textsc{Autogenesis Protocol (AGP)}}, a self evolution protocol that decouples what evolves from how evolution occurs. Its Resource Substrate Protocol Layer (RSPL) models prompts, agents, tools, environments, and memory as protocol registered resources\footnote{Unless otherwise specified, resources refer to instances of the five RSPL entity types: \emph{prompt}, \emph{agent}, \emph{tool}, \emph{environment}, \emph{memory} with agent \emph{outputs}.} with explicit state, lifecycle, and versioned interfaces. Its Self Evolution Protocol Layer (SEPL) specifies a closed loop operator interface for proposing, assessing, and committing improvements with auditable lineage and rollback. Building on \textbf{\textsc{AGP}}, we present \textbf{\textsc{Autogenesis System (AGS)}}, a self-evolving multi-agent system that dynamically instantiates, retrieves, and refines protocol-registered resources during execution. We evaluate \textbf{\textsc{AGS}} on multiple challenging benchmarks that require long horizon planning and tool use across heterogeneous resources. The results demonstrate consistent improvements over strong baselines, supporting the effectiveness of agent resource management and closed loop self evolution.

AI.tldr: They propose Autogenesis Protocol (AGP) and Autogenesis System (AGS) to make LLM-based multi-agent systems self-evolving, modular, and robust, achieving better performance on long-horizon, tool-using tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM agent protocols like A2A and MCP are too under-specified for real-world, evolving multi-agent ecosystems: they lack clear lifecycle and context management across different entities, weak versioning and change tracking, and no standardized, safe way to update agents, prompts, tools, or memories. This leads to brittle, monolithic systems with ad‑hoc glue code that are hard to maintain and evolve. The paper aims to provide a principled protocol that treats these components as first-class, versioned resources with explicit evolution mechanisms.
AI.method: They define the Autogenesis Protocol (AGP) with two layers: (1) the Resource Substrate Protocol Layer (RSPL), which models prompts, agents, tools, environments, and memories as protocol-registered resources that have explicit state, lifecycle, and versioned interfaces; and (2) the Self Evolution Protocol Layer (SEPL), which provides a closed-loop operator interface to propose, evaluate, and commit changes to these resources, with lineage tracking and rollback. On top of AGP they build the Autogenesis System (AGS), a multi-agent system that uses AGP to dynamically instantiate, retrieve, and refine resources during task execution. They then benchmark AGS on tasks requiring long-horizon planning and heterogeneous tool use, comparing against strong baseline systems.
AI.result: Across multiple challenging benchmarks that require long-term planning and tool usage over diverse resources, AGS consistently outperforms strong baseline LLM agent systems. The gains are attributed to better management of agent-related resources and the structured, closed-loop self-evolution process enabled by AGP.
AI.conclusion: Treating prompts, agents, tools, environments, and memories as versioned, lifecycle-aware resources within a formal protocol, combined with a closed-loop self-evolution layer, leads to more effective and adaptable LLM-based multi-agent systems. Autogenesis Protocol and Autogenesis System provide a foundation for building self-evolving agent ecosystems that are less brittle and achieve better performance on complex, long-horizon tasks.

title: IUQ: Interrogative Uncertainty Quantification for Long-Form Large Language Model Generation
summary: Despite the rapid advancement of Large Language Models (LLMs), uncertainty quantification in LLM generation is a persistent challenge. Although recent approaches have achieved strong performance by restricting LLMs to produce short or constrained answer sets, many real-world applications require long-form and free-form text generation. A key difficulty in this setting is that LLMs often produce responses that are semantically coherent yet factually inaccurate, while the underlying semantics are multifaceted and the linguistic structure is complex. To tackle this challenge, this paper introduces Interrogative Uncertainty Quantification (IUQ), a novel framework that leverages inter-sample consistency and intra-sample faithfulness to quantify the uncertainty in long-form LLM outputs. By utilizing an interrogate-then-respond paradigm, our method provides reliable measures of claim-level uncertainty and the model's faithfulness. Experimental results across diverse model families and model sizes demonstrate the superior performance of IUQ over two widely used long-form generation datasets. The code is available at https://github.com/louisfanhz/IUQ.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes IUQ, a framework to quantify uncertainty in long-form LLM outputs using an interrogate-then-respond process that measures claim-level uncertainty and faithfulness.
AI.motivation: Existing uncertainty quantification methods for LLMs work mainly for short or constrained answers, but many real applications need long, free-form text where models can sound coherent yet be factually wrong. There is a need for a way to measure how confident and faithful long-form generations are at the level of individual claims.
AI.method: The authors introduce Interrogative Uncertainty Quantification (IUQ), which uses an interrogate-then-respond paradigm. It evaluates inter-sample consistency across multiple generated samples and intra-sample faithfulness within each sample to estimate claim-level uncertainty in long-form outputs.
AI.result: Across different LLM families and sizes on two long-form generation datasets, IUQ shows better performance than existing uncertainty quantification methods, providing more reliable measures of uncertainty and faithfulness for long-form outputs.
AI.conclusion: IUQ is an effective and general framework for quantifying uncertainty in long-form LLM generations, outperforming prior methods and enabling more reliable assessment of claim-level confidence and model faithfulness.

title: From Reactive to Proactive: Assessing the Proactivity of Voice Agents via ProVoice-Bench
summary: Recent advancements in LLM agents are gradually shifting from reactive, text-based paradigms toward proactive, multimodal interaction. However, existing benchmarks primarily focus on reactive responses, overlooking the complexities of proactive intervention and monitoring. To bridge this gap, we introduce ProVoice-Bench, the first evaluation framework specifically designed for proactive voice agents, featuring four novel tasks. By leveraging a multi-stage data synthesis pipeline, we curate 1,182 high-quality samples for rigorous testing. Our evaluation of state-of-the-art Multimodal LLMs reveals a significant performance gap, particularly regarding over-triggering and reasoning capabilities. These findings highlight the limitations of current models and offer a roadmap for developing more natural, context-aware proactive agents.

AI.tldr: The paper presents ProVoice-Bench, a new benchmark for evaluating proactive multimodal voice agents, uncovering large gaps in current models’ abilities.
AI.motivation: Most existing LLM benchmarks evaluate reactive, text-based responses and ignore proactive behaviors such as intervention, monitoring, and multimodal interaction, leaving a gap in assessing realistic voice agents.
AI.method: The authors design ProVoice-Bench, an evaluation framework with four new tasks aimed at proactive voice behavior, and build a 1,182-sample test set using a multi-stage data synthesis pipeline. They then run state-of-the-art multimodal LLMs on this benchmark.
AI.result: State-of-the-art multimodal LLMs perform poorly on ProVoice-Bench, especially in controlling when to trigger proactive actions (over-triggering) and in reasoning over context.
AI.conclusion: Current multimodal LLMs are not yet capable of natural, context-aware proactive voice interaction, and ProVoice-Bench provides a direction and benchmark for improving and measuring such capabilities.

title: Blinded Multi-Rater Comparative Evaluation of a Large Language Model and Clinician-Authored Responses in CGM-Informed Diabetes Counseling
summary: Continuous glucose monitoring (CGM) is central to diabetes care, but explaining CGM patterns clearly and empathetically remains time-intensive. Evidence for retrieval-grounded large language model (LLM) systems in CGM-informed counseling remains limited. To evaluate whether a retrieval-grounded LLM-based conversational agent (CA) could support patient understanding of CGM data and preparation for routine diabetes consultations. We developed a retrieval-grounded LLM-based CA for CGM interpretation and diabetes counseling support. The system generated plain-language responses while avoiding individualized therapeutic advice. Twelve CGM-informed cases were constructed from publicly available datasets. Between Oct 2025 and Feb 2026, 6 senior UK diabetes clinicians each reviewed 2 assigned cases and answered 24 questions. In a blinded multi-rater evaluation, each CA-generated and clinician-authored response was independently rated by 3 clinicians on 6 quality dimensions. Safety flags and perceived source labels were also recorded. Primary analyses used linear mixed-effects models. A total of 288 unique responses (144 CA and 144 clinician) generated 864 ratings. The CA received higher quality scores than clinician responses (mean 4.37 vs 3.58), with an estimated mean difference of 0.782 points (95% CI 0.692-0.872; P<.001). The largest differences were for empathy (1.062, 95% CI 0.948-1.177) and actionability (0.992, 95% CI 0.877-1.106). Safety flag distributions were similar, with major concerns rare in both groups (3/432, 0.7% each). Retrieval-grounded LLM systems may have value as adjunct tools for CGM review, patient education, and preconsultation preparation. However, these findings do not support autonomous therapeutic decision-making or unsupervised real-world use.

AI.tldr: The paper evaluates a retrieval-grounded LLM conversational agent for explaining continuous glucose monitoring (CGM) data, finding its responses are rated higher than clinicians’ for quality, especially empathy and actionability, while maintaining similar safety, but it is not ready for autonomous therapeutic use.
AI.motivation: Continuous glucose monitoring generates complex data that are central to diabetes care, but clinicians spend substantial time explaining patterns and counseling patients. There is growing interest in using LLMs as conversational tools in healthcare, yet evidence is limited on whether retrieval-grounded LLM systems can safely and effectively support CGM-informed counseling and help patients prepare for routine consultations.
AI.method: Researchers built a retrieval-grounded LLM-based conversational agent designed to interpret CGM patterns and support diabetes counseling with plain-language, non-prescriptive responses. They created 12 CGM-informed cases from public datasets. Six senior UK diabetes clinicians each handled 2 cases and answered 24 standardized questions. For each question, both the agent and clinicians produced responses. In a blinded, multi-rater setup, three clinicians rated each response across six quality dimensions and also recorded safety concerns and guesses about whether responses came from a human or the CA. Data were analyzed with linear mixed-effects models.
AI.result: Across 288 responses (144 CA, 144 clinician) and 864 ratings, the conversational agent’s answers were rated higher in overall quality than clinicians’ (mean 4.37 vs 3.58), with an estimated difference of 0.782 points (95% CI 0.692-0.872; P<.001). The greatest advantages were in empathy and actionability, both close to 1-point improvements on a multi-point scale. Safety concerns were rare and similar for both sources, with major safety flags in only 0.7% of ratings for each group (3 out of 432).
AI.conclusion: A retrieval-grounded LLM conversational agent can produce CGM explanations and counseling-style responses that clinicians rate as higher quality—especially more empathetic and actionable—than typical clinician-written answers, without increasing obvious safety concerns. The system appears promising as an adjunct to support CGM review, patient education, and pre-visit preparation. However, the evidence does not justify using it for autonomous therapeutic decision-making or unsupervised real-world deployment, underscoring the need for clinician oversight and further evaluation.

title: Where are the Humans? A Scoping Review of Fairness in Multi-agent AI Systems
comment: In proceedings of European Conference on Information Systems (ECIS) 2026
summary: Rapid advances in Generative AI are giving rise to increasingly sophisticated Multi-Agent AI (MAAI) systems. While AI fairness has been extensively studied in traditional predictive scenarios, its examination in MAAI remains nascent and fragmented. This scoping review critically synthesizes existing research on fairness in MAAI systems. Through a qualitative content analysis of 23 selected studies, we identify five archetypal approaches. Our findings reveal that fairness in MAAI systems is often addressed superficially, lacks robust normative foundations, and frequently overlooks the complex dynamics introduced by agent autonomy and system-level interactions. We argue that fairness must be embedded structurally throughout the development lifecycle of MAAI, rather than appended as a post-hoc consideration. Meaningful evaluation requires explicit human oversight, normative clarity, and a precise articulation of fairness objectives and beneficiaries. This review provides a foundation for advancing fairness research in MAAI systems by highlighting critical gaps, exposing prevailing limitations, and suggesting pathways.

AI.tldr: The paper is a scoping review of fairness in Multi-Agent AI (MAAI), finding that current work is shallow, normatively weak, and ignores system-level agent interactions, and arguing that fairness must be structurally embedded across the MAAI lifecycle with clear human oversight and explicit fairness goals.
AI.motivation: Generative AI is enabling complex Multi-Agent AI systems, but existing fairness research has focused mostly on single predictive models, not on interacting agents and emergent system behaviors. The authors see a gap: fairness in MAAI is underexplored, conceptually fragmented, and lacks a systematic overview to organize what has been done and what is missing.
AI.method: They conduct a scoping review of the literature, selecting 23 relevant studies on fairness in MAAI and performing qualitative content analysis. From this analysis, they inductively derive five archetypal approaches to how fairness is currently handled in MAAI systems.
AI.result: The review shows that most MAAI fairness work is superficial, often treats fairness as an add-on rather than a core design principle, lacks clear normative grounding (e.g., whose fairness, based on which ethical theory), and rarely considers how agent autonomy and interactions at the system level create new fairness challenges. They categorize existing work into five archetypal approaches, revealing recurring patterns and limitations.
AI.conclusion: Fairness in MAAI must be treated as a structural property of the entire system and its lifecycle, not a post-hoc fix. Proper evaluation requires clearly defined fairness objectives, identification of beneficiaries and trade-offs, and explicit human oversight anchored in robust normative frameworks. The review lays groundwork for more rigorous and holistic fairness research in MAAI by mapping current approaches, highlighting gaps, and proposing future directions.

title: DiscoTrace: Representing and Comparing Answering Strategies of Humans and LLMs in Information-Seeking Question Answering
summary: We introduce DiscoTrace, a method to identify the rhetorical strategies that answerers use when responding to information-seeking questions. DiscoTrace represents answers as a sequence of question-related discourse acts paired with interpretations of the original question, annotated on top of rhetorical structure theory parses. Applying DiscoTrace to answers from nine different human communities reveals that communities have diverse preferences for answer construction. In contrast, LLMs do not exhibit rhetorical diversity in their answers, even when prompted to mimic specific human community answering guidelines. LLMs also systematically opt for breadth, addressing interpretations of questions that human answerers choose not to address. Our findings can guide the development of pragmatic LLM answerers that consider a range of strategies informed by context in QA.

AI.tldr: DiscoTrace is a method to model and compare the rhetorical strategies used in answers to information-seeking questions, showing humans are diverse but LLMs are not.
AI.motivation: Existing QA analysis focuses on correctness and content but not on how answers are rhetorically structured. Different communities answer in different ways, and LLMs may not match these community-specific rhetorical norms. There is a need for a systematic way to represent and compare rhetorical strategies in QA, especially to evaluate and improve LLM answer behavior.
AI.method: The authors propose DiscoTrace, which encodes each answer as a sequence of discourse acts tied to different interpretations of the question. These acts are layered over Rhetorical Structure Theory (RST) parses of the answers. They apply DiscoTrace to answers from nine distinct human communities and to answers generated by large language models, including cases where LLMs are instructed to mimic specific community guidelines. They then analyze and compare the resulting rhetorical patterns across humans and LLMs.
AI.result: Using DiscoTrace, the authors find that human communities differ substantially in their preferred rhetorical strategies for constructing answers. In contrast, LLM answers are relatively homogeneous in rhetorical structure, showing little community-specific variation even under explicit prompting. Additionally, LLMs systematically favor breadth, addressing multiple possible interpretations of the question that human answerers typically ignore or select among more narrowly.
AI.conclusion: DiscoTrace reveals a mismatch between the rhetorically diverse ways humans answer questions and the relatively uniform, breadth-focused strategies used by LLMs. This suggests that current LLMs are not yet pragmatically aligned with human community norms. The framework can be used to design and train more context-sensitive, pragmatically aware LLM answerers that adapt their rhetorical strategies to the expectations and norms of specific communities or situations.

title: OpenMobile: Building Open Mobile Agents with Task and Trajectory Synthesis
summary: Mobile agents powered by vision-language models have demonstrated impressive capabilities in automating mobile tasks, with recent leading models achieving a marked performance leap, e.g., nearly 70% success on AndroidWorld. However, these systems keep their training data closed and remain opaque about their task and trajectory synthesis recipes. We present OpenMobile, an open-source framework that synthesizes high-quality task instructions and agent trajectories, with two key components: (1) The first is a scalable task synthesis pipeline that constructs a global environment memory from exploration, then leverages it to generate diverse and grounded instructions. and (2) a policy-switching strategy for trajectory rollout. By alternating between learner and expert models, it captures essential error-recovery data often missing in standard imitation learning. Agents trained on our data achieve competitive results across three dynamic mobile agent benchmarks: notably, our fine-tuned Qwen2.5-VL and Qwen3-VL reach 51.7% and 64.7% on AndroidWorld, far surpassing existing open-data approaches. Furthermore, we conduct transparent analyses on the overlap between our synthetic instructions and benchmark test sets, and verify that performance gains stem from broad functionality coverage rather than benchmark overfitting. We release data and code at https://njucckevin.github.io/openmobile/ to bridge the data gap and facilitate broader mobile agent research.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces OpenMobile, an open-source framework to generate high-quality tasks and trajectories for training mobile agents, enabling competitive performance with transparent, publicly available data.
AI.motivation: Current high-performing mobile agents rely on closed, proprietary data and undisclosed task/trajectory synthesis methods, creating a data gap and limiting transparency, reproducibility, and broader research progress. There is a need for an open, scalable, and high-quality data generation framework for mobile agents that can match or approach the performance of closed-source counterparts while also providing insight into data composition and avoiding benchmark overfitting.
AI.method: The authors design OpenMobile with two main components: (1) a scalable task synthesis pipeline that first explores the mobile environment to build a global environment memory, then uses this memory to generate diverse, grounded task instructions; and (2) a policy-switching trajectory rollout strategy that alternates between a learner model and an expert model during data collection to capture error-recovery behaviors not present in standard imitation learning trajectories. They then use these synthetic instructions and trajectories to train vision-language mobile agents (e.g., Qwen2.5-VL, Qwen3-VL) and analyze data overlap with benchmarks to assess potential overfitting.
AI.result: Agents trained on OpenMobile’s synthesized data achieve strong performance on three dynamic mobile benchmarks, with fine-tuned Qwen2.5-VL and Qwen3-VL obtaining 51.7% and 64.7% success rates on AndroidWorld, significantly outperforming previous open-data approaches and approaching closed-source systems. Analyses of instruction overlap with test benchmarks indicate that performance gains arise from broad coverage of mobile functionalities, not memorization or benchmark-specific tuning.
AI.conclusion: OpenMobile effectively bridges the data gap for mobile agents by providing an open, scalable framework for synthesizing high-quality tasks and trajectories, yielding competitive performance with closed-data systems while maintaining transparency. The framework’s policy-switching rollout captures crucial error-recovery data, and overlap analyses support that improvements are due to generalization rather than benchmark overfitting. The released code and data aim to catalyze more reproducible and extensible research in mobile agent systems.

title: QuantCode-Bench: A Benchmark for Evaluating the Ability of Large Language Models to Generate Executable Algorithmic Trading Strategies
summary: Large language models have demonstrated strong performance on general-purpose programming tasks, yet their ability to generate executable algorithmic trading strategies remains underexplored. Unlike standard code benchmarks, trading-strategy generation requires simultaneous mastery of domain-specific financial logic, knowledge of a specialized API, and the ability to produce code that is not only syntactically correct but also leads to actual trades on historical data. In this work, we present QuantCode-Bench, a benchmark for the systematic evaluation of modern LLMs in generating strategies for the Backtrader framework from textual descriptions in English. The benchmark contains 400 tasks of varying difficulty collected from Reddit, TradingView, StackExchange, GitHub, and synthetic sources. Evaluation is conducted through a multi-stage pipeline that checks syntactic correctness, successful backtest execution, the presence of trades, and semantic alignment with the task description using an LLM judge. We compare state-of-the-art models in two settings: single-turn, where the strategy must be generated correctly on the first attempt, and agentic multi-turn, where the model receives iterative feedback and may repair its errors. We analyze the failure modes across different stages of the pipeline and show that the main limitations of current models are not related to syntax, but rather to the correct operationalization of trading logic, proper API usage, and adherence to task semantics. These findings suggest that trading strategy generation constitutes a distinct class of domain-specific code generation tasks in which success requires not only technical correctness, but also alignment between natural-language descriptions, financial logic, and the observable behavior of the strategy on data.

AI.tldr: QuantCode-Bench is a benchmark to test how well LLMs can turn English trading-strategy descriptions into executable Backtrader code.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM code benchmarks don’t capture the complexity of algorithmic trading, where models must understand financial domain logic, use a specialized backtesting API correctly, and produce strategies that actually trade on historical data, not just compile. The authors want a systematic way to measure and compare LLM capabilities in this realistic, high-stakes setting.
AI.method: They build QuantCode-Bench, a 400-task benchmark of natural-language trading-strategy descriptions mapped to implementations in the Backtrader framework. Tasks come from real-world sources (Reddit, TradingView, StackExchange, GitHub) and synthetic prompts, spanning multiple difficulty levels. They design a multi-stage evaluation pipeline: (1) check Python syntactic correctness, (2) check that the backtest runs without errors, (3) verify that the resulting strategy actually executes trades on historical data, and (4) use an LLM judge to assess semantic alignment between the produced strategy and the original textual description. They evaluate multiple state-of-the-art LLMs under two regimes: a single-turn setting where the model must solve the task in one shot, and an agentic multi-turn setting where the model can iteratively refine its code based on feedback from the evaluation pipeline.
AI.result: Across models, syntax-related failures are relatively rare, while a large share of errors arise from incorrect implementation of the described trading logic, misuse or misunderstanding of the Backtrader API, and misalignment between the produced strategy behavior and the textual specification. Multi-turn, feedback-driven setups improve robustness but do not eliminate these higher-level failures. The benchmark exposes clear performance differences between current models on this domain-specific task.
AI.conclusion: Algorithmic trading strategy generation is a challenging and distinct subtype of code generation that requires more than surface-level correctness. Success depends on accurately grounding natural-language descriptions in financial logic, using domain-specific APIs properly, and ensuring that the resulting code produces behaviorally correct strategies when backtested on data. QuantCode-Bench provides a structured way to evaluate and compare LLMs along these dimensions and highlights key gaps in current models’ capabilities.

title: HyperSpace: A Generalized Framework for Spatial Encoding in Hyperdimensional Representations
summary: Vector Symbolic Architectures (VSAs) provide a well-defined algebraic framework for compositional representations in hyperdimensional spaces. We introduce HyperSpace, an open-source framework that decomposes VSA systems into modular operators for encoding, binding, bundling, similarity, cleanup, and regression. Using HyperSpace, we analyze and benchmark two representative VSA backends: Holographic Reduced Representations (HRR) and Fourier Holographic Reduced Representations (FHRR). Although FHRR provides lower theoretical complexity for individual operations, HyperSpaces modularity reveals that similarity and cleanup dominate runtime in spatial domains. As a result, HRR and FHRR exhibit comparable end-to-end performance. Differences in memory footprint introduce additional deployment trade-offs where HRR requires approximately half the memory of FHRR vectors. By enabling modular, system-level evaluation, HyperSpace reveals practical trade-offs in VSA pipelines that are not apparent from theoretical or operator-level comparisons alone.

AI.tldr: The paper presents HyperSpace, a modular open-source framework for building and analyzing Vector Symbolic Architecture (VSA) systems, and shows that system-level performance trade-offs between HRR and FHRR differ from their theoretical operator-level complexity.
AI.motivation: Vector Symbolic Architectures have many operators and design choices, but comparing VSA methods is often done only at the level of individual operations or theory, not at the level of complete systems. There is a lack of a unified, modular framework to systematically build, combine, and benchmark different VSA components and backends under realistic workloads. The authors aim to fill this gap and to reveal practical trade-offs that theoretical complexity alone does not show.
AI.method: The authors design HyperSpace, an open-source framework that decomposes VSA systems into modular operators for key functions such as encoding, binding, bundling, similarity search, cleanup, and regression. They implement two representative backends—HRR and FHRR—within this framework. Using the same modular pipeline, they benchmark and profile both backends in spatial-domain workloads, measuring end-to-end runtime and memory usage and analyzing which operators dominate computation.
AI.result: Using HyperSpace, the authors find that in spatial-domain VSA applications, similarity computation and cleanup operations dominate overall runtime. Although FHRR has lower theoretical complexity for individual primitive operations, this advantage does not translate into significant end-to-end speedups. In practice, HRR and FHRR show similar total runtime, while HRR uses about half the memory required by FHRR vectors, highlighting a memory–performance trade-off.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that system-level, modular evaluation of VSA pipelines is essential, because focusing only on theoretical or operator-level complexity can be misleading. HyperSpace makes it possible to decompose and recombine VSA components to reveal real-world trade-offs in speed and memory. In the evaluated workloads, HRR and FHRR offer comparable runtime performance, but differ in memory footprint, suggesting that deployment choices should consider application-specific constraints rather than just asymptotic complexity.

title: Compressing Sequences in the Latent Embedding Space: $K$-Token Merging for Large Language Models
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) incur significant computational and memory costs when processing long prompts, as full self-attention scales quadratically with input length. Token compression aims to address this challenge by reducing the number of tokens representing inputs. However, existing prompt-compression approaches primarily operate in token space and overlook inefficiencies in the latent embedding space. In this paper, we propose K-Token Merging, a latent-space compression framework that merges each contiguous block of K token embeddings into a single embedding via a lightweight encoder. The compressed sequence is processed by a LoRA-adapted LLM, while generation remains in the original vocabulary. Experiments on structural reasoning (Textualized Tree), sentiment classification (Amazon Reviews), and code editing (CommitPackFT) show that K-Token Merging lies on the Pareto frontier of performance vs. compression, achieving up to 75% input length reduction with minimal performance degradation.

AI.tldr: They propose K-Token Merging, a latent-space token compression method that merges every K token embeddings into one, allowing LLMs to handle much shorter effective inputs with little loss in accuracy.
AI.motivation: LLMs are expensive on long prompts because self-attention cost grows quadratically with token length. Existing prompt-compression methods work in discrete token space and ignore redundancy/inefficiency already present in the continuous embedding space, leaving potential efficiency gains unrealized.
AI.method: Instead of compressing at the token level, they compress at the embedding level. They partition the input embedding sequence into contiguous blocks of K tokens, then use a lightweight encoder to merge each block into a single embedding vector, producing a much shorter latent sequence. A base LLM is adapted with LoRA to consume these merged embeddings, while decoding/generation is still done in the original token vocabulary. They evaluate this setup on diverse tasks requiring structure, sentiment understanding, and code editing.
AI.result: Across benchmarks (Textualized Tree, Amazon Reviews, CommitPackFT), K-Token Merging achieves substantial input-length reduction—up to 75% fewer effective tokens—while maintaining task performance close to the uncompressed baseline. In performance–compression trade-off plots, the method lies on or near the Pareto frontier compared with existing compression techniques.
AI.conclusion: Latent-space compression via K-Token Merging is an effective way to reduce LLM computational cost on long prompts without significantly harming accuracy. By merging contiguous token embeddings with a small encoder and adapting the LLM with LoRA, one can achieve strong performance–compression trade-offs, suggesting that much of the prompt information can be retained in a heavily compressed latent representation.

title: SRMU: Relevance-Gated Updates for Streaming Hyperdimensional Memories
summary: Sequential associative memories (SAMs) are difficult to build and maintain in real-world streaming environments, where observations arrive incrementally over time, have imbalanced sampling, and non-stationary temporal dynamics. Vector Symbolic Architectures (VSAs) provide a biologically-inspired framework for building SAMs. Entities and attributes are encoded as quasi-orthogonal hyperdimensional vectors and processed with well defined algebraic operations. Despite this rich framework, most VSA systems rely on simple additive updates, where repeated observations reinforce existing information even when no new information is introduced. In non-stationary environments, this leads to the persistence of stale information after the underlying system changes. In this work, we introduce the Sequential Relevance Memory Unit (SRMU), a domain- and cleanup-agnostic update rule for VSA-based SAMs. The SRMU combines temporal decay with a relevance gating mechanism. Unlike prior approaches that solely rely on cleanup, the SRMU regulates memory formation by filtering redundant, conflicting, and stale information before storage. We evaluate the SRMU on streaming state-tracking tasks that isolate non-uniform sampling and non-stationary temporal dynamics. Our results show that the SRMU increases memory similarity by $12.6\%$ and reduces cumulative memory magnitude by $53.5\%$. This shows that the SRMU produces more stable memory growth and stronger alignment with the ground-truth state.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Sequential Relevance Memory Unit (SRMU), a new update rule for Vector Symbolic Architecture-based sequential memories that filters and decays non-relevant information to handle imbalanced, non-stationary streaming data.
AI.motivation: Sequential associative memories in realistic streaming environments suffer because observations are imbalanced over time and the underlying process is non-stationary. Existing Vector Symbolic Architecture systems rely mostly on additive updates where repeated observations just reinforce what is already stored, so old or redundant information persists even after it becomes stale. Cleanup operations alone are not enough to prevent this, motivating a better way to regulate what gets written into memory.
AI.method: The authors design the Sequential Relevance Memory Unit (SRMU), an update rule for VSA-based sequential associative memories that (1) introduces temporal decay to gradually forget older information and (2) uses a relevance gating mechanism that filters out redundant, conflicting, or stale observations before they are stored. The SRMU is domain-agnostic and independent of specific cleanup procedures. It is evaluated on streaming state-tracking tasks crafted to stress non-uniform sampling and non-stationary temporal dynamics.
AI.result: On these streaming state-tracking benchmarks, SRMU improves memory similarity to the ground-truth state by 12.6% and reduces cumulative memory magnitude by 53.5% compared with prior approaches based on simple additive updates and cleanup alone. This indicates that the memory grows more slowly and in a more controlled way, while representing the true current state more accurately.
AI.conclusion: The SRMU provides a more robust, scalable update mechanism for VSA-based sequential memories in streaming, non-stationary environments. By combining temporal decay with relevance-based gating at write time, it filters out unhelpful information before storage, yielding more stable memory growth and closer alignment with the evolving ground-truth state than traditional additive-update VSA systems.

title: Fabricator or dynamic translator?
summary: LLMs are proving to be adept at machine translation although due to their generative nature they may at times overgenerate in various ways. These overgenerations are different from the neurobabble seen in NMT and range from LLM self-explanations, to risky confabulations, to appropriate explanations, where the LLM is able to act as a human translator would, enabling greater comprehension for the target audience. Detecting and determining the exact nature of the overgenerations is a challenging task. We detail different strategies we have explored for our work in a commercial setting, and present our results.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes how large language models (LLMs) sometimes "overgenerate" during machine translation and evaluates strategies to detect and categorize these behaviors in a commercial setting.
AI.motivation: While LLMs have become strong machine translation systems, their generative nature leads them to produce extra content beyond a faithful translation. This can be harmful (risky confabulations) or beneficial (helpful explanations similar to those of human translators). Commercial MT workflows need systematic ways to detect, distinguish, and manage these various kinds of overgeneration.
AI.method: The authors categorize types of LLM overgeneration in MT (self-explanations, risky confabulations, and appropriate explanations). They then explore and test multiple detection strategies in a commercial environment, likely including prompt design, post-hoc automatic checks, and possibly human evaluation, to identify both the presence and the nature of the extra content.
AI.result: They obtain empirical findings on how well different strategies work to detect and classify LLM overgenerations in real-world MT use cases. Their results presumably show that some approaches are more reliable than others in distinguishing harmful from beneficial additions and in flagging risky behavior.
AI.conclusion: LLM overgeneration in MT is multifaceted and not always negative; some additions can enhance user understanding. However, automatically detecting and characterizing these behaviors is difficult. The paper provides an initial practical framework and evaluation of strategies to manage overgeneration in commercial MT applications.

title: An Axiomatic Benchmark for Evaluation of Scientific Novelty Metrics
summary: The rigorous evaluation of the novelty of a scientific paper is, even for human scientists, a challenging task. With the increasing interest in AI scientists and AI involvement in scientific idea generation and paper writing, it also becomes increasingly important that this task be automatable and reliable, lest both human attention and compute tokens be wasted on ideas that have already been explored. Due to the challenge of quantifying ground-truth novelty, however, existing novelty metrics for scientific papers generally validate their results against noisy, confounded signals such as citation counts or peer review scores. These proxies can conflate novelty with impact, quality, or reviewer preference, which in turn makes it harder to assess how well a given metric actually evaluates novelty. We therefore propose an axiomatic benchmark for scientific novelty metrics. We first define a set of axioms that a well-behaved novelty metric should satisfy, grounded in human scientific norms and practice, then evaluate existing metrics across ten tasks spanning three domains of AI research. Our results reveal that no existing metric satisfies all axioms consistently, and that metrics fail on systematically different axioms, reflecting their underlying architectures. Additionally, we show that combining metrics of complementary architectures leads to consistent improvements on the benchmark, with per-axiom weighting achieving 90.1% versus 71.5% for the best individual metric, suggesting that developing architecturally diverse metrics is a promising direction for future work. We release the benchmark code as supplementary material to encourage the development of more robust scientific literature novelty metrics.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes an axiomatic benchmark to rigorously evaluate how well automatic metrics measure the novelty of scientific papers, showing that no existing metric satisfies all desired properties but that combining diverse metrics performs best.
AI.motivation: Evaluating the true novelty of scientific work is hard even for humans and existing automatic metrics rely on noisy proxies like citation counts or review scores, which mix novelty with impact, quality, and preference. With AI increasingly generating ideas and writing papers, a reliable, explicit way to assess novelty is needed to avoid wasting human and computational resources on non-novel work.
AI.method: The authors define a set of axioms—principled properties grounded in scientific norms—that a good novelty metric should satisfy. They then design an axiomatic benchmark composed of ten evaluation tasks across three AI research domains and systematically test multiple existing novelty metrics against these axioms. They also explore combinations of metrics with different underlying architectures, including per-axiom weighting schemes.
AI.result: They find that no single existing novelty metric satisfies all axioms consistently and that different metrics systematically fail on different axioms, reflecting their architectural biases. However, combining metrics with complementary architectures substantially improves performance, with a per-axiom weighted combination achieving 90.1% on the benchmark, compared to 71.5% for the best individual metric.
